topics.title: Basics of Computer Graphics and Its Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basics of Computer Graphics and Its Applications # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Computer Graphics (CG)** is a discipline of computer science that deals with the **generation, storage, manipulation, and display of pictorial output** (images, diagrams, animations, and 3D scenes) by a digital computer using specially designed hardware and software. It is the science of converting data into visual form using computational algorithms. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECST835)**, Computer Graphics is defined as the methodology and technology concerned with: 1. **Generating images** algorithmically from 2D or 3D models (geometric primitives, equations, datasets). 2. **Rendering** these models into raster/vector images for visual perception. 3. **Interacting** with the displayed data through input devices. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Digital Artist's Studio 🎨** Imagine you are a **movie director** who wants to create a scene showing a car driving on a road. In real life, you need: - A real car 🚗 - A real road 🛣️ - Cameras, lighting crew, actors, and post-production editing 🎬 In **Computer Graphics**, you replace *every physical element* with **mathematics and code**: - The car → a **3D mathematical model** (a mesh of triangles defined by vertices) - The road → a **2D texture mapped onto a virtual plane** - The camera → a **virtual viewpoint** (a 4×4 transformation matrix) - The lighting → **mathematical equations** (Phong, Lambert, Ray Tracing) - The final frame → a **2D array of pixels** stored in memory So, **Computer Graphics = Geometry + Mathematics + Physics of Light + Hardware Acceleration**, all working together to "trick" the human eye into seeing something that doesn't physically exist. --- ## 1.3 Sub-Disciplines of Computer Graphics | Sub-Area | Focus | Example | |----------|-------|---------| | **2D Graphics** | Flat images, drawings, diagrams | Adobe Photoshop, MS Paint | | **3D Graphics** | Solid models, scenes with depth | Blender, Maya, Unity | | **Image Processing** | Manipulation of existing images | Filters, denoising, OCR | | **Animation** | Time-based sequence of frames | Pixar movies, motion graphics | | **Virtual Reality (VR)** | Immersive 3D environment | Meta Quest, HTC Vive | | **Augmented Reality (AR)** | Overlay graphics on real world | Pokémon Go, surgical AR | | **Scientific Visualization** | Visualizing scientific data | MRI, weather maps, CFD | | **GUI (Graphical User Interface)** | Visual interaction layer | Windows, macOS, Android | --- ## 1.4 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Highlighted) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Know Primitive Terms (Board Exam Favorites):** - **Pixel (Picture Element):** The smallest addressable unit of a raster image. A pixel stores color information (typically 8 bits per channel in RGB). - **Resolution:** The total number of pixels along width × height (e.g., **1920 × 1080** for Full HD). - **Aspect Ratio:** The proportional ratio of width to height of a display or image (e.g., **16:9**, **4:3**). - **Frame Buffer:** A dedicated memory area in video hardware that holds the bitmap image to be displayed. - **Refresh Rate:** The number of times per second the display redraws the image, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. - **Raster Image:** A 2D grid of pixels (e.g., `.jpg`, `.png`). - **Vector Image:** Defined by mathematical paths, geometric primitives, and equations (e.g., `.svg`, `.pdf`). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Computer Graphics? — The Motivation Computer Graphics transforms **abstract numerical data** into a **visually meaningful form** that the human brain can interpret in milliseconds. This is critical because: - Human visual perception processes images **~60,000 times faster** than text. - A 3D visualization of a molecule is more useful to a chemist than 1000 rows of atomic coordinates. - Real-time rendering allows pilots, surgeons, and engineers to interact with simulated systems safely. --- ## 2.2 Major Applications of Computer Graphics (Board-Favorite) ### A. Entertainment & Media 🎮🎬 - Movies: CGI in Marvel, Pixar (RenderMan engine), VFX. - Video Games: Unity, Unreal Engine, real-time ray tracing (NVIDIA RTX). - Streaming: Animated infographics, motion graphics. ### B. Education & Training 📚 - E-learning platforms with interactive 3D models (BioDigital, Visible Body). - Flight simulators (Pilots train on Boeing 737 simulators before flying). - Medical simulators for surgery training. ### C. Engineering & CAD/CAM ⚙️ - **CAD (Computer-Aided Design):** AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA. - **CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing):** CNC machine toolpath generation. - Finite Element Analysis (FEA) visualization. ### D. Scientific Visualization 🔬 - Weather forecasting (Doppler radar maps). - Molecular biology (protein folding visualizations like PyMOL). - Astrophysics (galaxy simulations, NASA visualizations). ### E. Medicine 🏥 - **CT, MRI, PET scan** reconstruction in 2D/3D. - Surgical planning (e.g., tumor removal path planning). - Virtual colonoscopy. ### F. Business & GUI 💼 - Office software, dashboards, charts, graphs. - Operating System interfaces (Windows, macOS, Ubuntu GNOME). - Web design (CSS, HTML5 Canvas, WebGL). ### G. Defense & Aerospace 🛰️ - Radar/sonar visualization. - Drone control interfaces. - Missile trajectory simulation. ### H. Art & Design 🎨 - Digital painting, generative art, NFTs. - Architectural walkthroughs (AutoCAD Revit, Lumion). --- ## 2.3 Display Devices — Core Classifications > [!NOTE] > **KTU often asks: "Categorize display devices."** Use this exact taxonomy. | Display Type | Technology | Examples | |--------------|------------|----------| | **CRT (Cathode Ray Tube)** | Electron beam strikes phosphor | Old TVs, early monitors | | **LCD (Liquid Crystal Display)** | Backlit liquid crystals | Monitors, TVs, phones | | **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** | LEDs backlight/inorganic emitters | Modern TVs, signage | | **OLED** | Organic LEDs (self-emissive) | iPhone, high-end TVs | | **Plasma** | Ionized gas cells | Old large flat TVs | | **DLP (Digital Light Processing)** | Micromirror chips (DMD) | Projectors | --- ## 2.4 Raster vs. Vector Graphics (Conceptual) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Exam Frequently Asked Distinction:** | Property | Raster | Vector | |----------|--------|--------| | **Definition** | Grid of pixels | Mathematical equations | | **File Types** | `.jpg`, `.png`, `.bmp`, `.gif` | `.svg`, `.pdf`, `.ai`, `.eps` | | **Scaling** | Loses quality (pixelation) | Infinitely scalable | | **File Size** | Large for high resolution | Small (compact) | | **Best For** | Photos, textures | Logos, fonts, CAD drawings | | **Color Model** | Indexed/RGB | RGB, CMYK, fills/strokes | --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Master these 6 formulas — they form 70% of Part A numericals.** | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | **Total Pixels** | $N = W \times H$ | $N$: pixels, $W$: width, $H$: height | | 2 | **Aspect Ratio** | $AR = \dfrac{W}{H}$ | Expressed as $W:H$ | | 3 | **Frame Buffer Size** | $B = N \times b$ | $B$: bytes, $b$: bits-per-pixel / 8 | | 4 | **Refresh Rate / Time** | $t = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | $f$: Hz, $t$: seconds | | 5 | **Frame Rate (FPS)** | $\text{FPS} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{frame}}}$ | $T_{\text{frame}}$: time per frame | | 6 | **Color Depth** | $C = 2^{b}$ | $C$: number of distinct colors, $b$: bits per pixel | ### Color Capacity (Quick Reference Table) | Bits per Pixel (b) | Number of Colors ($2^b$) | |--------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | **2** (Monochrome) | | 4 | **16** | | 8 | **256** | | 16 (High Color) | **65,536** | | 24 (True Color) | **16,777,216** | | 32 (True Color + Alpha) | **4,294,967,296** | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Real-time flight simulators must sustain **≥ 60 FPS** at **1920 × 1080** resolution to avoid motion sickness. - **Medical Imaging:** CT scanners produce **1024 × 1024 × 12-bit** slices; a single patient scan may exceed 2 GB. - **Telecommunications:** Streaming services (Netflix, YouTube) compress 4K video (3840 × 2160) using H.264/HEVC codecs. - **Game Development:** Modern AAA games run at **4K (3840 × 2160) @ 120 Hz** with HDR10 (~10 bits per channel). # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **The derivations below are the **most frequently tested numericals** in KTU 2024 OEC papers. Practice each one until the steps become muscle memory.** --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Frame Buffer Memory Calculation ### Problem Statement (Standard Board Format): A display system has a resolution of **1280 × 1024** pixels and uses **24 bits per pixel** for color. Calculate the **size of the frame buffer in kilobytes (KB) and megabytes (MB)**. ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1 — Identify the given data:** - Width of screen, $W = 1280$ pixels - Height of screen, $H = 1024$ pixels - Color depth, $b = 24$ bits per pixel **Step 2 — Compute the total number of pixels:** The total pixel count is the area of the display in pixel units. $$N = W \times H = 1280 \times 1024$$ $$N = 1,310,720 \text{ pixels}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the total bits required:** Multiply the number of pixels by the bit depth (since each pixel stores $b$ bits of color info). $$B_{bits} = N \times b = 1,310,720 \times 24$$ $$B_{bits} = 31,457,280 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 4 — Convert bits to bytes (divide by 8):** Since $1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$: $$B_{bytes} = \frac{B_{bits}}{8} = \frac{31,457,280}{8}$$ $$B_{bytes} = 3,932,160 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 5 — Convert bytes to kilobytes (divide by 1024):** $$B_{KB} = \frac{B_{bytes}}{1024} = \frac{3,932,160}{1024}$$ $$B_{KB} = 3840 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 6 — Convert kilobytes to megabytes (divide by 1024 again):** $$B_{MB} = \frac{B_{KB}}{1024} = \frac{3840}{1024}$$ $$\boxed{B_{MB} = 3.75 \text{ MB}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Aspect Ratio & Scaling ### Problem Statement: A monitor's native resolution is **1920 × 1080**. The horizontal width of the monitor is **52 cm**. Compute the **aspect ratio** and the **physical height** of the monitor screen. ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1 — Compute the aspect ratio:** The aspect ratio is simply the ratio of width to height in lowest integer form. $$AR = \frac{W}{H} = \frac{1920}{1080}$$ To reduce, divide numerator and denominator by the GCD. GCD(1920, 1080) = 120. $$AR = \frac{1920 \div 120}{1080 \div 120} = \frac{16}{9}$$ $$\boxed{AR = 16:9 \text{ (Widescreen HD)}}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the physical height using the aspect ratio:** Since aspect ratio = physical width : physical height, we have: $$\frac{52}{h} = \frac{16}{9}$$ Solving for $h$: $$h = \frac{52 \times 9}{16} = \frac{468}{16}$$ $$\boxed{h = 29.25 \text{ cm}}$$ **Step 3 — Diagonal of the monitor (optional but common follow-up):** Using the Pythagorean theorem: $$d = \sqrt{W_{cm}^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{52^2 + 29.25^2}$$ $$d = \sqrt{2704 + 855.5625} = \sqrt{3559.5625}$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 59.66 \text{ cm} \approx 23.49 \text{ inches}}$$ This is why a "24-inch monitor" is described as such — the diagonal measurement is the industry standard. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Refresh Rate and Bandwidth ### Problem Statement: A display operates at **resolution 1920 × 1080** with a **refresh rate of 75 Hz** and **24 bits per pixel**. Calculate the **required video bandwidth in Gigabits per second (Gbps)**. ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1 — Identify data rate per frame:** Each frame contains $W \times H$ pixels, each with $b$ bits. $$D_{frame} = W \times H \times b = 1920 \times 1080 \times 24 \text{ bits}$$ $$D_{frame} = 49,766,400 \text{ bits per frame}$$ **Step 2 — Multiply by refresh rate to get bits per second:** The display is refreshed $f$ times per second. $$\text{Bandwidth} = D_{frame} \times f = 49,766,400 \times 75$$ $$\text{Bandwidth} = 3,732,480,000 \text{ bits/second}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to Gigabits per second:** $$\text{Bandwidth} = \frac{3,732,480,000}{10^9} = 3.732 \text{ Gbps}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Required Bandwidth} \approx 3.73 \text{ Gbps}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Industry context:** HDMI 2.0 supports up to 18 Gbps, while HDMI 2.1 supports 48 Gbps — explaining why higher resolutions / refresh rates need newer cables. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Number of Distinct Colors ### Problem Statement: A graphics card supports **32 bits per pixel** with 8 bits each for R, G, B, and 8 bits for the **alpha (transparency) channel**. How many distinct colors can be displayed, and how does this compare to the human eye's perception? ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1 — Apply the color depth formula:** $$C = 2^{b} = 2^{32}$$ $$\boxed{C = 4,294,967,296 \text{ distinct color values}}$$ **Step 2 — Human eye comparison:** - The human eye can distinguish roughly **10 million distinct colors**. - Therefore, **24-bit True Color ($2^{24} = 16,777,216$ colors)** is already "photorealistic" — the additional 8 bits are reserved for the **alpha (transparency) channel**, not new visible colors. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Resolution Reference Table | Standard Name | Resolution (W × H) | Total Pixels (N) | Aspect Ratio | Bits @ 24 bpp | Size (MB) | |---------------|--------------------|------------------|--------------|---------------|-----------| | VGA | 640 × 480 | 307,200 | 4:3 | 921,600 bits | 0.88 MB | | SVGA | 800 × 600 | 480,000 | 4:3 | 1,440,000 bits | 1.37 MB | | XGA | 1024 × 768 | 786,432 | 4:3 | 2,359,296 bits | 2.25 MB | | HD 720p | 1280 × 720 | 921,600 | 16:9 | 2,764,800 bits | 2.64 MB | | Full HD 1080p | 1920 × 1080 | 2,073,600 | 16:9 | 6,220,800 bits | 5.93 MB | | 4K UHD | 3840 × 2160 | 8,294,400 | 16:9 | 24,883,200 bits | 23.73 MB | | 8K UHD | 7680 × 4320 | 33,177,600 | 16:9 | 99,532,800 bits | 94.92 MB | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use this table to quickly verify answers in KTU Part B numerics. Memorizing 2–3 rows is sufficient.** # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — High-Level Computer Graphics Pipeline The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end graphics pipeline** — from application data to the final pixel displayed on screen. This is a **Module 1 essential concept** tested frequently. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[APPLICATION
PROGRAM] --> B[GRAPHICS DATA
VERTICES MESHES TEXTURES] B --> C[MODELING
3D SCENE CONSTRUCTION] C --> D[TRANSFORMATION
TRANSLATION ROTATION SCALING] D --> E[PROJECTION
3D TO 2D MAPPING] E --> F[CLIPPING AND
WINDOW VIEWPORT] F --> G[RASTERIZATION
VECTORS TO PIXELS] G --> H[FRAGMENT SHADING
TEXTURING LIGHTING] H --> I[FRAME BUFFER
VIDEO RAM] I --> J[DISPLAY
CONTROLLER] J --> K[MONITOR
SCREEN OUTPUT] A -->|USER INPUT| A K -->|FEEDBACK| A ``` ### Diagram Explanation (For Board Answers): 1. **Application Program** creates the 3D model (e.g., a car) using geometry. 2. **Modeling** stores the 3D scene in world coordinates. 3. **Transformation** repositions the model in the scene (move, rotate, scale). 4. **Projection** converts 3D world to a 2D view (perspective or orthographic). 5. **Clipping** removes parts outside the visible window. 6. **Rasterization** converts geometric primitives into pixels. 7. **Fragment Shading** applies textures, lighting, and shadows per pixel. 8. **Frame Buffer** holds the final bitmap ready for the monitor. 9. **Display Controller** continuously reads the frame buffer and refreshes the screen. --- ## 4.2 Diagram — Application Domains of Computer Graphics ```mermaid graph TD ROOT[COMPUTER GRAPHICS APPLICATIONS] ROOT --> P1[ENTERTAINMENT] ROOT --> P2[EDUCATION] ROOT --> P3[ENGINEERING CAD CAM] ROOT --> P4[SCIENTIFIC VISUALIZATION] ROOT --> P5[MEDICINE] ROOT --> P6[BUSINESS GUI] ROOT --> P7[DEFENSE AEROSPACE] ROOT --> P8[ART DESIGN] P1 --> P1A[Movies CGI] P1 --> P1B[Video Games] P1 --> P1C[Animation] P3 --> P3A[AutoCAD SolidWorks] P3 --> P3B[3D Printing Models] P3 --> P3C[FEA Visualization] P4 --> P4A[Weather Maps] P4 --> P4B[Molecular Modeling] P4 --> P4C[Astrophysics] P5 --> P5A[CT MRI PET Scans] P5 --> P5B[Surgical Simulators] P5 --> P5C[Telemedicine] P7 --> P7A[Flight Simulators] P7 --> P7B[Radar Displays] P7 --> P7C[Drone Interfaces] ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram — Raster vs. Vector Comparison (Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[INPUT DATA] --> B{TYPE OF GRAPHIC} B -->|PHOTO OR PAINTING| C[RASTER PIPELINE] B -->|MATHEMATICAL SHAPES| D[VECTOR PIPELINE] C --> C1[CAPTURE OR DRAW PIXEL BY PIXEL] C1 --> C2[STORE IN PIXEL GRID ARRAY] C2 --> C3[APPLY FILTERS COMPRESSION] C3 --> C4[OUTPUT JPG PNG BMP] D --> D1[DEFINE GEOMETRY POINTS LINES CURVES] D1 --> D2[APPLY FILLS STROKES TRANSFORMS] D2 --> D3[RENDER ON DEMAND AT ANY RESOLUTION] D3 --> D4[OUTPUT SVG PDF EPS] ``` ### Why This Diagram Matters: - **Raster files** lose quality on scaling because they interpolate pixel values. - **Vector files** scale infinitely because they re-render the math at any output size. - This distinction is a **favourite 3-mark question** in KTU exams. --- ## 4.4 Diagram — Display System Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR HOST[CPU OR GPU] -->|Draw Commands| DRV[GRAPHICS DRIVER] DRV --> GPUC[GRAPHICS PROCESSING UNIT] GPUC --> VB[VIDEO MEMORY VRAM] VB -->|Bitmap Data| DC[DISPLAY CONTROLLER] DC -->|Pixel Clock| DAC[DIGITAL TO ANALOG CONVERTER] DAC --> MON[MONITOR SCREEN] USER[USER] -->|Mouse Keyboard| DRV MON -->|Visual Output| USER ``` **Key Insight:** The **VRAM (Video RAM)** is dedicated memory that holds: 1. The **front buffer** (currently being displayed). 2. The **back buffer** (next frame being drawn). 3. The **depth buffer (Z-buffer)** for 3D hidden surface removal. 4. **Texture memory** for image data used on 3D surfaces. This double-buffering technique prevents flickering (called **tearing**) during real-time rendering. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Format:** KTU-style direct questions, typically 2–3 sentence model answers with bullet points. ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define Computer Graphics. List any four major applications of computer graphics.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Computer Graphics is a branch of computer science concerned with the generation, representation, manipulation, and display of pictures and images using computers. It involves the use of specialized hardware (GPU) and software (OpenGL, DirectX) to convert data into visual output. **Applications (2 Marks — 0.5 each):** - **Entertainment:** 3D movies, video games, and animation (e.g., Pixar films, Unreal Engine games). - **Engineering:** Computer-Aided Design (CAD) tools like AutoCAD and SolidWorks for mechanical part design. - **Medicine:** Visualization of CT, MRI, and PET scans for diagnosis and surgical planning. - **Education:** Interactive e-learning platforms with 3D anatomical models and virtual labs. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Differentiate between Raster Graphics and Vector Graphics.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Property | Raster Graphics | Vector Graphics | |----------|-----------------|-----------------| | **Definition** | Composed of a grid of pixels | Composed of mathematical paths | | **File Size** | Large (depends on resolution) | Small (depends on complexity) | | **Scaling** | Quality degrades on zoom | Infinitely scalable, no quality loss | | **Best Use** | Photographs, complex images | Logos, fonts, line drawings, CAD | | **Examples** | JPG, PNG, BMP, GIF | SVG, PDF, AI, EPS | --- ## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Format:** KTU End Semester Exam (ESE) Module 1 — provides INTERNAL CHOICE between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### ✅ **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the major applications of computer graphics in detail. Discuss its role in the fields of **entertainment, education, engineering, and medicine**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A display system has a resolution of **2560 × 1440 pixels** with **32 bits per pixel**. The refresh rate is **60 Hz**.     **(i)** Calculate the **total number of pixels**.     **(ii)** Calculate the **frame buffer size in MB**.     **(iii)** Calculate the **required video bandwidth in Gbps**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Applications of Computer Graphics (7 Marks):** **1. Entertainment (1.5 Marks):** - **Movies:** Modern films use heavy CGI (Computer-Generated Imagery). Examples: Marvel Cinematic Universe uses CG for characters like Thanos, while Pixar produces entirely computer-animated films using the RenderMan engine. - **Video Games:** Real-time 3D rendering engines like Unity and Unreal Engine power AAA games such as Cyberpunk 2077 and Call of Duty. Modern games use ray tracing, physics simulation, and AI for realistic behavior. - **Animation:** Tool-assisted animation like Blender, Maya, and 3ds Max are used for both 2D and 3D motion graphics. **2. Education (1.5 Marks):** - Interactive e-learning platforms like **Khan Academy, Coursera, and BioDigital Human** use 3D models to teach anatomy, chemistry, and engineering. - **Flight simulators** train pilots in a safe, risk-free environment using realistic 3D models of cockpits, weather, and airports. - **Virtual labs** allow students to perform chemistry experiments or physics simulations without physical equipment. **3. Engineering — CAD/CAM (2 Marks):** - **CAD (Computer-Aided Design):** Tools like AutoCAD, SolidWorks, and CATIA allow engineers to design mechanical parts, circuits, and buildings. - **CAM (Computer-Aided Manufacturing):** Translates CAD designs into CNC machine instructions for manufacturing. - **FEA (Finite Element Analysis):** Visualizes stress, strain, and heat distribution on a designed part under load. **4. Medicine (2 Marks):** - **Medical Imaging:** Devices like CT, MRI, and PET scanners produce internal body scans that are reconstructed into 2D/3D images for diagnosis. - **Surgical Planning:** Surgeons can practice complex procedures (e.g., tumor removal) on a 3D model of the patient's actual anatomy before the operation. - **VR Surgical Training:** Platforms like Osso VR allow medical students to practice surgeries in virtual reality. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 Marks):** **Given Data:** - Width, $W = 2560$ pixels - Height, $H = 1440$ pixels - Bits per pixel, $b = 32$ bits - Refresh rate, $f = 60$ Hz **(i) Total Pixels (2 Marks):** $$N = W \times H = 2560 \times 1440$$ $$\boxed{N = 3,686,400 \text{ pixels}}$$ **(ii) Frame Buffer Size in MB (3 Marks):** $$B_{bits} = N \times b = 3,686,400 \times 32 = 117,964,800 \text{ bits}$$ $$B_{bytes} = \frac{117,964,800}{8} = 14,745,600 \text{ bytes}$$ $$B_{MB} = \frac{14,745,600}{1024 \times 1024} = \frac{14,745,600}{1,048,576}$$ $$\boxed{B_{MB} \approx 14.0625 \text{ MB}}$$ **(iii) Video Bandwidth in Gbps (2 Marks):** $$\text{Bits per second} = N \times b \times f = 3,686,400 \times 32 \times 60$$ $$= 7,077,888,000 \text{ bits/second}$$ $$\text{Bandwidth} = \frac{7,077,888,000}{10^9}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Bandwidth} \approx 7.078 \text{ Gbps}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Bandwidth Question:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to multiply by the refresh rate $f$ — students often compute only the per-frame data, losing **1 mark**. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $1,000,000$ instead of $1,048,576$ for MB conversion. KTU expects $1024 \times 1024 = 1,048,576$. Wrong conversion = **lose 1 mark**. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing **GB instead of MB** in the final answer — read the question carefully. --- ### ✅ **QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper]** **(a)** What is a **pixel**? Explain the concepts of **resolution, aspect ratio, and color depth** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A monitor has a native resolution of **3840 × 2160** with a **physical screen width of 70 cm**. Calculate:     **(i)** The **aspect ratio**.     **(ii)** The **physical height** of the screen in cm.     **(iii)** The **diagonal size** of the screen in inches. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Pixel & Key Concepts (7 Marks):** **1. Pixel (1 Mark):** A **pixel** (Picture Element) is the smallest addressable unit of a raster image. Each pixel stores color information, typically in RGB format, occupying a few bits (commonly 8, 16, 24, or 32 bits). **2. Resolution (2 Marks):** Resolution refers to the total number of distinct pixels that can be displayed on a screen, expressed as $W \times H$ (width × height). - **Example 1:** Full HD = $1920 \times 1080$ → 2,073,600 pixels. - **Example 2:** 4K UHD = $3840 \times 2160$ → 8,294,400 pixels. Higher resolution = more detail, sharper image. **3. Aspect Ratio (2 Marks):** Aspect ratio is the proportional relationship between the width and height of a display. - **Example 1:** $1920 / 1080 = 16/9$ → **16:9 widescreen**. - **Example 2:** $1024 / 768 = 4/3$ → **4:3 standard** (older monitors). - **Example 3:** $2560 / 1440 = 16/9$ → **QHD widescreen**. **4. Color Depth (2 Marks):** Color depth (or bit depth) is the number of bits used to represent the color of a single pixel. - **8 bpp:** $2^8 = 256$ colors (old GIF images). - **16 bpp:** $2^{16} = 65,536$ colors (High Color). - **24 bpp:** $2^{24} = 16,777,216$ colors (True Color). - **32 bpp:** $2^{32} = 4.29$ billion colors (True Color + Alpha channel). --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 Marks):** **Given Data:** - Resolution: $3840 \times 2160$ (4K UHD) - Physical width: $W_{cm} = 70$ cm **(i) Aspect Ratio (2 Marks):** $$AR = \frac{W}{H} = \frac{3840}{2160}$$ Reducing by GCD = 120: $$AR = \frac{3840 \div 120}{2160 \div 120} = \frac{32}{18} = \frac{16}{9}$$ $$\boxed{AR = 16:9}$$ **(ii) Physical Height (2 Marks):** $$\frac{70}{h} = \frac{16}{9}$$ $$h = \frac{70 \times 9}{16} = \frac{630}{16} = 39.375$$ $$\boxed{h = 39.375 \text{ cm}}$$ **(iii) Diagonal in inches (3 Marks):** Using the Pythagorean theorem: $$d_{cm} = \sqrt{W_{cm}^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{70^2 + 39.375^2}$$ $$d_{cm} = \sqrt{4900 + 1550.39} = \sqrt{6450.39}$$ $$d_{cm} \approx 80.31 \text{ cm}$$ Converting to inches ($1 \text{ inch} = 2.54 \text{ cm}$): $$d_{in} = \frac{80.31}{2.54} \approx 31.62$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 31.62 \text{ inches (a "32-inch" 4K monitor)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Aspect Ratio / Diagonal Question:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to reduce the aspect ratio to its lowest form (e.g., writing 3840:2160 instead of 16:9) — loses **0.5 to 1 mark**. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the conversion $1 \text{ inch} = 2.54 \text{ cm}$ — this is the most common error in diagonal problems. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Mixing up $W$ and $H$ in the Pythagorean formula. Always square the width first. --- ## 5.3 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision? Read this section 30 minutes before the exam.** ### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions - **Computer Graphics:** The science of generating, manipulating, and displaying images using computers. - **Pixel:** Smallest unit of a raster image (stores color info). - **Resolution:** $W \times H$ total pixel count. - **Aspect Ratio:** Width-to-height proportional relationship (e.g., 16:9, 4:3). - **Color Depth:** Number of bits per pixel (determines color richness). - **Frame Buffer:** Dedicated video memory holding the image to be displayed. - **Refresh Rate:** Number of times/second the screen redraws (Hz). - **Raster:** Pixel-based image. - **Vector:** Math-based image (scales infinitely). ### 🧮 Must-Know Formulas (Top 6) 1. $N = W \times H$ 2. $B = N \times b$ (bits) 3. $B_{bytes} = B \div 8$ 4. $B_{MB} = B_{bytes} \div 1{,}048{,}576$ 5. $C = 2^b$ (number of distinct colors) 6. $\text{Bandwidth (bps)} = N \times b \times f$ ### 🏭 Must-Know Applications (8 Domains) 1. Entertainment (movies, games, animation) 2. Education (e-learning, simulators, virtual labs) 3. Engineering (CAD, CAM, FEA) 4. Scientific Visualization (weather, molecular, astrophysics) 5. Medicine (CT/MRI, surgical planning, telemedicine) 6. Business & GUI (dashboards, OS interfaces) 7. Defense & Aerospace (flight simulators, radar, drones) 8. Art & Design (digital art, architectural walkthroughs) ### ⚠️ Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid - ❌ Using $1000$ instead of $1024$ for byte conversions (KTU follows binary convention). - ❌ Confusing bits and bytes (1 byte = 8 bits). - ❌ Forgetting to multiply by refresh rate in bandwidth problems. - ❌ Writing aspect ratio as a decimal instead of $W:H$ format. - ❌ Mixing up raster and vector file extensions. ### 📊 Quick-Recall Color Depth Table | bpp | Colors | Common Name | |-----|--------|-------------| | 1 | 2 | Monochrome | | 8 | 256 | Indexed | | 16 | 65,536 | High Color | | 24 | 16.7M | True Color | | 32 | 4.29B | True Color + Alpha |

topics.title: Video Display Devices: LED, OLED, PDP, FED, and Reflective Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Video Display Devices: LED, OLED, PDP, FED, and Reflective Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Video Display Device** is an electronic output peripheral that visually presents digitally generated graphical, textual, or pictorial information produced by a computer graphics system. According to the KTU 2024 Computer Graphics syllabus (Module 1), display devices are broadly classified into two fundamental categories based on the optical emission mechanism:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1.1):**
> Display devices are hardware components that convert electrical signals from a graphics controller into visible light patterns (pixels). They are classified as:
> 1. **Emissive Displays** — Devices that emit light directly in response to electrical stimulation (LED, OLED, PDP, FED).
> 2. **Non-Emissive Displays** — Devices that modulate or reflect an external light source (Reflective LCD, E-Paper).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stage Spotlight" Intuition

Imagine a **dark theatre stage** (your computer screen) where a performance (the image) must be made visible to the audience (the user).

- **Emissive Displays** are like **performers wearing their own internal LED lights** — they generate their own glow from within, so the stage remains visible even when the house lights are off. (LED, OLED, PDP, FED)
- **Non-Emissive Displays** are like **performers holding reflective silver costumes** — they depend entirely on the theatre's overhead spotlight (the backlight or ambient light) to bounce light off themselves and become visible. (Reflective LCD, E-Paper, Electrophoretic Displays)

This simple analogy makes it instantly clear *why* OLEDs look perfect in dark rooms (they self-illuminate) while reflective displays are unusable in darkness (they have no internal light source).

## 1.3 Key Display Performance Metrics (Must Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard metrics are universally tested in KTU examinations:
> - **Pixel (Picture Element):** The smallest addressable point on a display, typically a square or rectangular region.
> - **Resolution:** The total number of distinct pixels in each dimension (e.g., **1920 × 1080 = Full HD**).
> - **Aspect Ratio:** The proportional relationship between width and height (e.g., **16:9**, **4:3**).
> - **Refresh Rate:** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, the number of times per second the display redraws the image.
> - **Dot Pitch:** The diagonal distance between two same-colored sub-pixels, measured in **millimeters (mm)**.
> - **Luminance (Brightness):** Measured in **candela per square meter (cd/m²)** or **nits**.
> - **Contrast Ratio:** The ratio between the brightest white and darkest black luminance values.
> - **Response Time:** Time taken for a pixel to change state, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Technology-by-Technology Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 LED (Light Emitting Diode) Displays

- **Operating Principle:** A **p-n junction semiconductor** emits photons when forward-biased through a process called **electroluminescence**. When electrons cross the junction and recombine with holes, energy is released as light.
- **Direct-View LED:** Each pixel is composed of discrete red, green, and blue LEDs packed tightly together. Commonly used in **stadium screens, billboards, and large outdoor displays** because of extreme brightness (often >5000 nits).
- **LED-Backlit LCD:** In modern televisions, white LEDs are used as a *backlight* for an LCD panel. Variants include **Edge-Lit** (LEDs on the perimeter) and **Full-Array Local Dimming (FALD)** (LED matrix directly behind the panel).
- **Why it matters:** LEDs are the most energy-efficient emissive technology for large panels, with operational lifetimes exceeding **100,000 hours**.

### 2.1.2 OLED (Organic Light Emitting Diode) Displays

- **Operating Principle:** Uses **organic carbon-based compounds** (e.g., Alq₃, polymers) sandwiched between two electrodes (anode and cathode). When current flows, the organic layer emits light via electroluminescence.
- **Key Distinction:** Unlike LEDs, OLEDs are **surface emitters** — they produce light across the entire organic layer rather than at a point, enabling truly thin and flexible displays.
- **AMOLED vs PMOLED:**
  - **AMOLED (Active Matrix):** Each pixel has its own thin-film transistor (TFT) and capacitor. Used in smartphones, TVs.
  - **PMOLED (Passive Matrix):** Rows and columns are scanned sequentially. Limited to small displays (watches, MP3 players).
- **True Black Levels:** Because each pixel can be individually turned off, OLED achieves an **infinite contrast ratio**.

### 2.1.3 PDP (Plasma Display Panel)

- **Operating Principle:** Each pixel is a tiny cell filled with **noble gases (neon + xenon)**. When a voltage is applied across electrodes, the gas ionizes into a **plasma state**, releasing ultraviolet (UV) photons. These UV photons strike **phosphor coatings** (red, green, blue), which then emit visible light.
- **Key Facts:**
  - Each sub-pixel cell is essentially a **miniature fluorescent lamp**.
  - Plasma displays are **self-emissive** and offer excellent viewing angles and contrast.
  - Disadvantages: Heavy, power-hungry, susceptible to **screen burn-in**, and largely discontinued in favor of OLED/LCD.

### 2.1.4 FED (Field Emission Display)

- **Operating Principle:** A modern flat-panel evolution of the CRT. Each pixel contains an array of **tiny carbon nanotubes (CNT) or Spindt tips** that act as **cathodes (electron emitters)**. When a strong electric field is applied, electrons tunnel from these tips toward a phosphor-coated anode through a vacuum gap, exciting the phosphors to emit light.
- **Key Facts:**
  - Combines the **picture quality of a CRT** (rich colors, fast response) with the **thin form factor of LCD**.
  - Operates at lower power than PDP and LCD.
  - Cathode materials: **Molybdenum (Mo), Silicon, Carbon Nanotubes**.

### 2.1.5 Reflective Displays (Non-Emissive)

- **Operating Principle:** These displays contain no backlight. They use ambient light reflected off a modulator layer to create an image. The modulator controls whether light is reflected (bright pixel) or absorbed (dark pixel).
- **Types:**
  - **Reflective LCD:** Uses a single polarizer and reflector. Common in digital watches, calculators, e-readers.
  - **Electrophoretic Display (E-Paper / E-Ink):** Tiny microcapsules containing positively charged white particles and negatively charged black particles suspended in a clear fluid. Applying voltage moves particles to the surface, creating text/images.
  - **Electromechanical (MEMS) Displays:** Tiny movable mirrors (e.g., Digital Micromirror Device, DMD) that tilt to reflect light into or out of the projection lens.
- **Key Facts:**
  - **Ultra-low power consumption** (only uses power during image refresh).
  - Excellent **sunlight readability**.
  - E-Ink displays have **refresh rates of ~1 Hz** (slow), so they cannot show video.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Pixels | $N = W \times H$ | pixels | Resolution = Width × Height |
| 2 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{W}{H}$ | unitless | Ratio of width to height |
| 3 | Total Sub-pixels | $N_{sub} = 3 \times N$ | sub-pixels | RGB triplet per pixel |
| 4 | Screen Diagonal | $d = \sqrt{W_{in}^2 + H_{in}^2}$ | inches | Pythagoras on physical dimensions |
| 5 | Frame Time | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}}$ | seconds | Time per frame (e.g., 16.67 ms @ 60 Hz) |
| 6 | Bandwidth | $BW = N_{sub} \times \text{bits per sub-pixel} \times f_{refresh}$ | bits/sec | Data throughput required |
| 7 | Dot Pitch | $p = \dfrac{25.4 \times d_{screen}}{N_{diagonal}}$ | mm | Pixels per mm mapping |
| 8 | Luminance | $L = \dfrac{I}{A \cos \theta}$ | $\text{cd/m}^2$ (nits) | Perceived brightness |
| 9 | Contrast Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{L_{white}}{L_{black}}$ | unitless | White/black luminance ratio |
| 10 | Power (LED) | $P = V_f \times I_f$ | Watts | Forward voltage × forward current |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are critical for designing GPU memory pipelines, calculating VRAM bandwidth, and selecting display panels for embedded systems, smartphones, and medical imaging displays.

## 2.3 Display Classification Master Chart

| Property | LED | OLED | PDP | FED | Reflective |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Emissive Type | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (uses ambient) |
| Backlight Needed | No (or as backlight) | No | No | No | No |
| Black Level | Good | Perfect | Good | Good | Excellent (no backlight) |
| Response Time | ~1 ms | <0.1 ms | ~1 ms | <0.01 ms | >100 ms |
| Power Efficiency | High | Medium | Low | High | Ultra-high |
| Viewing Angle | ~160° | ~180° | ~180° | ~180° | ~140° |
| Current Status | Mainstream | Premium Mainstream | Discontinued | R&D/Niche | E-Readers, Wearables |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Display Pixels from Aspect Ratio

**Problem:** A display has an aspect ratio of **16:9** and a screen diagonal of **46 inches**. Calculate the number of horizontal and vertical pixels, assuming a Full HD resolution class.

**Step 1: Express dimensions using aspect ratio.**

Let the width be $W = 16k$ and height be $H = 9k$ for some scaling constant $k$.

**Step 2: Apply Pythagoras' theorem to find $k$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d^2 &= W^2 + H^2 \\
(46)^2 &= (16k)^2 + (9k)^2 \\
2116 &= 256k^2 + 81k^2 \\
2116 &= 337k^2 \\
k^2 &= \dfrac{2116}{337} \\
k^2 &= 6.2789 \\
k &= 2.5058 \text{ inches}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the physical width and height.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 16 \times 2.5058 = 40.0928 \text{ inches} \\
H &= 9 \times 2.5058 = 22.5522 \text{ inches}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify with Pythagoras.**

$$
\sqrt{(40.0928)^2 + (22.5522)^2} = \sqrt{1607.43 + 508.60} = \sqrt{2116.03} \approx 46.0 \text{ inches} \checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Convert to pixel counts using standard Full HD mapping.**

For 1080 vertical resolution:
$$
H_{pixels} = 1080
$$
Using aspect ratio $16:9$:
$$
W_{pixels} = 1080 \times \dfrac{16}{9} = 1920 \text{ pixels}
$$
Total pixels:
$$
N = 1920 \times 1080 = 2,073,600 \text{ pixels} \approx 2.07 \text{ Megapixels}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: VRAM Bandwidth Required for a Display

**Problem:** A 4K OLED display (3840 × 2160) operates at **120 Hz** refresh rate with **10-bit color depth** per sub-pixel. Compute the required VRAM bandwidth in **Gigabits per second (Gbps)**.

**Step 1: Total sub-pixel count.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{sub} &= 3840 \times 2160 \times 3 \\
N_{sub} &= 8,294,400 \times 3 = 24,883,200 \text{ sub-pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Bits per frame.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{frame} &= N_{sub} \times 10 \text{ bits} \\
B_{frame} &= 24,883,200 \times 10 = 248,832,000 \text{ bits/frame}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply by refresh rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= B_{frame} \times f_{refresh} \\
BW &= 248,832,000 \times 120 \\
BW &= 29,859,840,000 \text{ bits/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to Gbps.**

$$
BW = \dfrac{29,859,840,000}{10^9} = 29.86 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This is why modern GPUs use **GDDR6X** or **HBM2e** memory with bandwidths of 500+ Gbps, and why DisplayPort 2.1 and HDMI 2.1 were developed to support such data rates.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Dot Pitch Calculation

**Problem:** A 24-inch monitor has a resolution of 2560 × 1440 (QHD). Calculate the dot pitch in millimeters.

**Step 1: Diagonal in pixels.**

$$
N_{diag} = \sqrt{2560^2 + 1440^2} = \sqrt{6,553,600 + 2,073,600} = \sqrt{8,627,200} \approx 2937.21 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step 2: Convert screen diagonal to mm.**

$$
d_{screen} = 24 \times 25.4 = 609.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Dot pitch formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \dfrac{d_{screen}}{N_{diag}} \\
p &= \dfrac{609.6}{2937.21} \\
p &= 0.2075 \text{ mm} \approx 0.21 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A dot pitch of **0.21 mm** is excellent for text clarity and is the standard for high-end 24" QHD monitors.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Video Display Devices

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Video Display Devices] --> B[Emissive Displays]
    A --> C[Non-Emissive Displays]

    B --> D1[LED Display]
    B --> D2[OLED Display]
    B --> D3[PDP Plasma Display]
    B --> D4[FED Field Emission Display]

    D1 --> D1a[Direct View LED]
    D1 --> D1b[LED Backlit LCD]

    D2 --> D2a[AMOLED]
    D2 --> D2b[PMOLED]

    D3 --> D3a[DC Plasma]
    D3 --> D3b[AC Plasma]

    D4 --> D4a[Spindt Tip FED]
    D4 --> D4b[Carbon Nanotube FED]

    C --> E1[Reflective LCD]
    C --> E2[Electrophoretic E Ink]
    C --> E3[MEMS Digital Micromirror]
    C --> E4[Electroluminescent EL]
```

## 4.2 OLED Pixel Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Voltage Source] --> B1[Anode ITO Layer]
    B1 --> C1[Organic Hole Transport Layer HTL]
    C1 --> D1[Organic Emissive Layer EML]
    D1 --> E1[Organic Electron Transport Layer ETL]
    E1 --> F1[ Cathode Metal Layer]
    F1 --> G1[Light Emission to User]

    D1 --> D1a[Red Subpixel]
    D1 --> D1b[Green Subpixel]
    D1 --> D1c[Blue Subpixel]

    H1[TFT Backplane] -.->|Controls Current| D1
    I1[Capacitor Storage] -.->|Maintains Pixel State| D1
```

## 4.3 PDP Cell Operational Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Address Electrode Y] --> B1[Plasma Cell Neon Xenon Gas]
    A2[Display Electrode X] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Gas Ionization Plasma State]
    C1 --> D1[UV Photon Emission 147 nm]
    D1 --> E1[Phosphor Coating Excited]
    E2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Visible Light Output RGB]
    F1 --> G1[Front Glass Viewer]

    E2[Phosphor Materials] --> E1
```

## 4.4 FED Vacuum Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Cathode Spindt Tip] -->|High Electric Field| B1[Electron Emission Tunneling]
    B1 --> C1[Vacuum Gap 1 to 2 mm]
    C1 --> D1[Anode Substrate]
    D1 --> E1[Phosphor Layer RGB]
    E1 --> F1[Visible Light to Viewer]
    G1[Gate Electrode] -.->|Modulates Emission| B1
    H1[Spacer Posts] -.->|Maintain Vacuum Gap| C1
```

## 4.5 Reflective Display Optical Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Ambient Light Source Sun Room] --> B1[Front Polarizer]
    B1 --> C1[Liquid Crystal Layer]
    C1 --> D1[Reflector Mirror Back Layer]
    D4 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Light Reflects Back Through LC]
    E1 --> F1[Modulated Image to Eye]
    D2[Voltage Driver TFT] -.->|Aligns Crystals| C1
    D3[Color Filter Array] -.->|Sub Pixel Coloring| C1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**"Briefly explain the working principle of an OLED display. Mention any two advantages over traditional LED-LCD displays."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **OLED (Organic Light Emitting Diode)** display works on the principle of **electroluminescence** through organic carbon-based compounds. When a voltage is applied across the anode and cathode, electrons and holes recombine in the emissive layer, releasing energy as visible light. Each pixel emits its own light and can be individually turned off.

**Two Advantages over LED-LCD:**
1. **True Black Levels:** Since each pixel can be switched off completely, OLED achieves an **infinite contrast ratio**, while LED-LCDs suffer from light leakage through the LCD layer.
2. **No Backlight Required:** This allows for **thinner, lighter, and flexible** panel designs, and reduces power consumption when displaying dark content.
3. **Wider Viewing Angles:** OLEDs maintain color accuracy up to **~180°**, whereas LCDs often shift colors beyond 120°.

*(Allocate: 1 mark for working principle, 2 marks for advantages)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**"Compare emissive and non-emissive displays with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Emissive | Non-Emissive |
|---|---|---|
| Light Source | Generates its own light | Modulates external/reflected light |
| Backlight | Not required | Required (or ambient light) |
| Power Consumption | Higher (in bright scenes) | Lower |
| Example | **OLED, LED, PDP, FED** | **Reflective LCD, E-Paper** |
| Use Case | TVs, Smartphones, Monitors | E-Readers, Watches, Calculators |

**Example:** A *Plasma Display Panel (PDP)* is emissive because it ionizes gas to produce UV light that excites phosphors. A *Reflective LCD* is non-emissive because it relies on external light reflected from a back mirror to display the image.

*(Allocate: 2 marks for comparison, 1 mark for examples)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a **Plasma Display Panel (PDP)** with a neat diagram. Discuss its merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working of a **Field Emission Display (FED)**. Compare it with CRT and LCD technologies. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Construction of PDP (3 Marks):**
A Plasma Display Panel consists of two parallel glass plates separated by a small gap (~0.1 mm). The gap is filled with a mixture of **neon (Ne) and xenon (Xe) gases** at low pressure. Each sub-pixel cell is formed by **transparent display electrodes** on the front plate and **address electrodes** on the rear plate, with **barrier ribs** separating the cells. The inside walls of each cell are coated with **red, green, or blue phosphors**.

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
1. A voltage is applied across the electrodes, ionizing the gas mixture.
2. The ionized gas forms a **plasma** — a soup of free electrons and ions.
3. Colliding xenon atoms release energy as **ultraviolet (UV) photons** (~147 nm).
4. UV photons strike the phosphor coating, exciting its electrons to higher energy levels.
5. When electrons return to the ground state, they emit **visible photons** of the corresponding color (red, green, or blue).
6. By varying the pulse count per frame, the intensity of each sub-pixel is controlled, producing the full color gamut.

**Merits and Demerits (1 Mark):**
- **Merits:** Excellent color accuracy, wide viewing angle, high contrast, large screen sizes possible.
- **Demerits:** High power consumption, heavy weight, screen burn-in risk, shorter lifespan (~30,000 hours vs OLED's 100,000).

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**FED Working Principle (4 Marks):**
A Field Emission Display uses **micro-fabricated cathode tips** (Spindt tips made of molybdenum or carbon nanotubes) at the rear of each pixel. A strong **electric field** (typically 10⁹ V/m) at the tip causes electrons to **tunnel out via quantum mechanical field emission** into a vacuum gap. These electrons are accelerated toward the **phosphor-coated anode** at the front, exciting phosphors to emit visible light, just like in a CRT. A **gate electrode** modulates the emission per sub-pixel.

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Feature | FED | CRT | LCD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Depth | Thin (flat panel) | Bulbous (deep) | Thin |
| Electron Source | CNT/Spindt tips | Heated filament | None (uses backlight) |
| Phosphor Use | Yes | Yes | No |
| Power | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Picture Quality | Excellent (CRT-like) | Excellent | Good (with backlight) |

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the working of an **LED display**. Differentiate between **direct-view LED** and **LED-backlit LCD** displays. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of **E-Paper (Electrophoretic Display)**. Why is it preferred for e-readers? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**LED Working Principle (3 Marks):**
A Light Emitting Diode is a **p-n junction diode** made from direct bandgap semiconductors like **GaAs (red), GaP (green), or InGaN (blue)**. When forward-biased, electrons from the n-side cross the junction and recombine with holes in the p-side. The energy difference (bandgap) is released as a **photon** of wavelength:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}
$$

where $E_g$ is the bandgap energy. The color depends on $E_g$:
- Red: $E_g \approx 1.8$ eV
- Green: $E_g \approx 2.2$ eV
- Blue: $E_g \approx 2.7$ eV

**Direct-View LED vs LED-Backlit LCD (4 Marks):**

| Feature | Direct-View LED | LED-Backlit LCD |
|---|---|---|
| Pixel Composition | Each pixel = R, G, B LED | White LEDs illuminate LCD matrix |
| Use Case | Outdoor billboards, stadiums | TVs, monitors, laptops |
| Contrast | Excellent (self-emissive) | Limited (backlight bleed) |
| Brightness | Very high (>5000 nits) | Moderate (~1000 nits) |
| Thickness | Thicker for outdoor | Very thin |

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**E-Paper Construction (3 Marks):**
An Electrophoretic Display consists of a thin layer of **transparent microcapsules** (30-100 µm diameter) sandwiched between two electrode layers. Each microcapsule contains:
- A clear dielectric fluid
- **Positively charged white pigment particles** (e.g., titanium dioxide)
- **Negatively charged black pigment particles** (e.g., carbon black)

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
1. When a **positive voltage** is applied to the top electrode, the white particles are attracted to the surface (visible to the reader) and the black particles move to the bottom — pixel appears **white**.
2. When a **negative voltage** is applied, black particles rise to the surface — pixel appears **black**.
3. Intermediate voltages produce **grayscale** by varying the particle distribution.

**Why Preferred for E-Readers (2 Marks):**
- **Ultra-Low Power:** Power is consumed only during page refresh; a fully static image consumes **zero power**.
- **Sunlight Readability:** Reflective technology means it looks *better* in bright sunlight, unlike emissive displays.
- **Eye Comfort:** No flickering, no blue light emission, mimicking real paper.
- **Battery Life:** A single charge can last **weeks**, vs hours for LCD/OLED.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing LED with OLED:** LED is an inorganic semiconductor (GaAs); OLED is an *organic* carbon-based compound. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting that PDP is emissive:** Many students wrongly classify PDP as backlit. Always remember: **gas ionization = plasma = self-emission**.
> 3. **Mixing up FED and CRT:** FED is essentially a *flat-panel* CRT. Forgetting the **vacuum gap** and **Spindt tips** loses 2-3 marks.
> 4. **No diagram = -1 mark:** KTU examiners *always* deduct 1 mark if no diagram is drawn for construction-type questions.
> 5. **Skipping units in calculations:** Bandwidth, pixel counts, and dot pitch must include proper units (Gbps, mm, pixels). Wrong units = -0.5 to -1 mark.
> 6. **In Part A:** Writing only bullet points without explanation is penalized. Always write **2-3 lines** of explanation per point.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 / Video Display Devices:**

- **Two Categories:** **Emissive** (LED, OLED, PDP, FED) vs **Non-Emissive** (Reflective LCD, E-Paper).
- **LED:** Inorganic p-n junction electroluminescence; used in billboards and as LCD backlights.
- **OLED:** Organic carbon-based emissive layer; perfect blacks, flexible panels; **AMOLED** for phones/TVs.
- **PDP:** Neon-xenon gas plasma excites **phosphors** to emit RGB light; largely discontinued.
- **FED:** Carbon nanotube or Spindt tip cathodes emit electrons in **vacuum** to strike phosphors; CRT-quality in flat form.
- **Reflective LCD:** Uses **single polarizer + mirror**, no backlight; used in watches and calculators.
- **E-Paper (E-Ink):** Electrophoretic microcapsules with charged particles; ultra-low power, sunlight readable.
- **MEMS (DMD):** Tiltable micromirrors reflect light for projectors (DLP technology).
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Total Pixels: $N = W \times H$
  - Aspect Ratio: $AR = W/H$
  - Bandwidth: $BW = N_{sub} \times \text{bits} \times f_{refresh}$
  - Dot Pitch: $p = 25.4 \times d_{screen} / N_{diag}$
  - Frame Time: $T_{frame} = 1 / f_{refresh}$
- **High-Yield Comparison Points:** Emissive vs Non-Emissive, OLED vs LED, PDP vs LCD, FED vs CRT.
- **Exam Tip:** Always pair a definition with a **labeled diagram** for full marks in KTU 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random and Raster Scan Displays and Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Random and Raster Scan Displays and Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Display System?

A **Display System** in computer graphics is the hardware-software assembly that converts digitally stored graphical primitives (points, lines, polygons, characters) into a visible, persistent image on a physical viewing surface. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECST835)**, display systems are classified fundamentally by the way the **electron beam** (in CRTs) or the **pixel addressing logic** (in modern flat panels) traverses the screen to refresh the image.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> *"Random and Raster Scan Displays and Systems"* — This topic covers the two dominant CRT-era refresh architectures, their addressing schemes, video controllers, frame buffers, and display processors.

---

## 1.2 Random Scan Display (Vector Display / Line-Drawing Display)

### Formal Definition
A **Random Scan Display** is a CRT-based refresh display in which the electron beam is steered **directly and selectively** along arbitrary geometric paths between specified endpoints to "draw" the picture line-by-line. Only the lines that constitute the image are illuminated; the beam is repositioned (retraced) at high speed between successive draw commands.

### Conceptual Analogy
> 🎨 **Imagine a Calligraphy Artist on a Dark Canvas**
> A random scan display is exactly like a *calligrapher* working on a blackboard with a single pen. Instead of coloring the *entire* board with the pen (which would be raster scan), the calligrapher **moves the pen only along the strokes of the letters** and lifts the pen while jumping to the next stroke. The artist must redraw the same letters repeatedly before the ink fades (this continuous redrawing is the **refresh cycle**). The picture is built from **strokes**, not dots.

### Key Characteristics
- Beam driven by **line-drawing commands** (Move, Draw).
- Picture stored as a **Display List / Display File** of vector commands.
- Suitable for **line drawings, CAD, schematics** — not natural scenes.
- Resolution limited primarily by the **beam spot size and beam deflection bandwidth**.

---

## 1.3 Raster Scan Display

### Formal Definition
A **Raster Scan Display** is a display system in which the image is represented as a rectangular matrix of discrete picture elements (**pixels**), stored in a dedicated memory called the **Frame Buffer (Refresh Buffer)**. The electron beam (or pixel addressing circuit) sweeps the entire screen in a fixed, systematic, left-to-right, top-to-bottom pattern called the **raster**, refreshing every pixel on every pass.

### Conceptual Analogy
> 🖼️ **Imagine Coloring a Gridded Poster**
> A raster scan display is like a child filling in a giant poster made of **thousands of tiny squares** (pixels). Every single square is filled with a color — even if it's "white" or "black" — *whether or not it is part of the picture*. The child paints **row by row, left to right, top to bottom**, and the whole poster fades simultaneously, requiring a full repaint. The image is a **mosaic of colored dots**, not a sketch of lines.

### Key Characteristics
- Image is a **2D array of pixels** in the frame buffer.
- Beam is **synchronized to a scan clock**; deflection is purely horizontal+vertical sweep.
- Excellent for **realistic, shaded, textured imagery** (photographs, video).
- Resolution = **N_h × N_v** (e.g., **1920 × 1080** for Full HD).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - *Random Scan* → **Vector / Line-based**, draws only image segments.
> - *Raster Scan* → **Pixel / Matrix-based**, paints the *entire* screen every refresh.

---

## 1.4 Comparison at a Glance (Conceptual Level)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Aspect | Random Scan | Raster Scan |
> |---|---|---|
> | Drawing Unit | Line (vector) | Pixel (dot) |
> | Storage | Display List of commands | Frame Buffer of pixel colors |
> | Flicker | Higher (line count dependent) | Lower (fixed rate) |
> | Best For | Wireframes / CAD | Realistic / Shaded scenes |
> | Beam Motion | Random, jump-based | Systematic, sequential sweep |

*(Note: ASCII pipe `|` avoided inside math cells; the vertical separators in this table are the markdown table syntax itself.)*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Random Scan Display — Detailed Architecture

The random scan system revolves around a **Display Processor (DP)** and a **Display File (DF)**. The CPU builds a display list of graphic commands; the DP interprets this list and drives the **Vector Generator**, **Character Generator**, and **Attribute Generator**, which in turn control the **CRT beam deflection and intensity** circuits.

### Operational Logic Steps
1. **Application / CPU** issues drawing commands (e.g., `LINE(x1,y1,x2,y2)`, `MOVE(x,y)`, `TEXT(s)`).
2. Commands are **stored in the Display File** in the system memory.
3. The **Display Processor** traverses the display file cyclically at a fixed refresh rate.
4. For each `LINE` command, the **Vector Generator** produces analog X and Y deflection voltages that linearly deflect the electron beam between the two endpoints.
5. The **Character Generator** (ROM) maps ASCII codes to stroke patterns for text.
6. Beam **Z-axis (intensity)** is modulated to brighten drawn lines and dim during repositioning (blanking during re-trace).
7. When the picture content changes, the CPU **rewrites only the affected segment** of the display file.

### Why It Works the Way It Does
- Because only visible vectors are illuminated, **no scan conversion is needed** — the image is mathematically exact, line for line.
- Refresh is **content-dependent**: complex images need higher refresh rates to avoid flicker because the DP must redraw more vectors within the persistence of the phosphor.
- Memory footprint is **tiny** compared to raster — proportional to the *number of objects*, not screen area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Random scan displays dominated early **CAD/CAM** (e.g., IBM 2250, Tektronix 4014) and **flight simulators** of the 1970s because their line drawings were sharp, jitter-free, and required no large frame buffer memory in an era when RAM was extremely expensive.

---

## 2.2 Raster Scan Display — Detailed Architecture

The raster scan system uses a **Frame Buffer** (a large, dual-ported RAM) and a **Video Controller** (a dedicated scan-timing ASIC). Every pixel of the screen has at least one corresponding memory location in the frame buffer.

### Operational Logic Steps
1. The **Frame Buffer** is loaded with pixel color/intensity values by the CPU (or GPU).
2. The **Video Controller** generates a **raster timing chain**:
   - A **pixel clock** ticks out each pixel horizontally.
   - A **horizontal sync** pulse marks the end of each scanline and triggers a horizontal **retrace**.
   - A **vertical sync** pulse marks the end of a full frame and triggers a vertical **retrace**.
3. During active scan, the video controller fetches pixel values **sequentially from the frame buffer** and converts them via a **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** to drive the RGB guns of the CRT (or directly the LCD column drivers).
4. The beam (or LCD row driver) deflects/addresses in a fixed, repeating **left-to-right, top-to-bottom** trajectory.
5. A **Color Look-Up Table (CLUT / Palette)** may sit between the frame buffer and the DAC, allowing the buffer to store small *indices* (e.g., **8 bits**) but display a much larger *color space* (e.g., **24-bit** true color from 256 palette entries).

### Why It Works the Way It Does
- A raster display **does not need to know the geometric meaning** of an image — only the colors of individual pixels. This is why any photograph, video, or shaded surface can be displayed with no architectural change.
- The frame buffer is **content-agnostic**, which is why the same monitor can show text, games, or movies.
- **Refresh rate is decoupled from image complexity** — the screen is always repainted top-to-bottom, regardless of how many objects are on it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Every modern flat-panel display, smartphone screen, and TV is architecturally a raster system. The principles of frame buffer, video controller, sync pulses, and pixel clock directly translate to **HDMI, DisplayPort, MIPI-DSI** signaling in today's electronics.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE and Continuous Evaluation questions on this topic.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Total Pixels per Frame** | $N = N_h \times N_v$ | dimensionless; product of horizontal × vertical pixel count |
> | 2 | **Aspect Ratio** | $AR = \dfrac{N_h}{N_v}$ | e.g., 16:9 → $AR \approx 1.778$ |
> | 3 | **Frame Refresh Period** | $T_f = \dfrac{1}{f_v}$ | seconds; $f_v$ is vertical refresh rate in Hz |
> | 4 | **Horizontal Scan Frequency** | $f_h = N_v \times f_v$ | Hz; one H-sync per scanline per frame |
> | 5 | **Pixel Clock Frequency** | $f_{p} = N_h \times N_v \times f_v$ | Hz (pixels per second) |
> | 6 | **Frame Buffer Memory** | $M = N_h \times N_v \times b$ | bits; $b$ = bits per pixel |
> | 7 | **Display File Size (Random Scan)** | $D \approx k \times V$ | bytes; $V$ = number of vectors, $k$ = bytes per command |
> | 8 | **Color Palette Size** | $C = 2^{b}$ | colors; $b$ = color depth in bits |
> | 9 | **Bandwidth** (Video) | $BW = f_{p} \times b$ | bits/sec |
> | 10 | **Time per Scanline** | $T_h = \dfrac{T_f}{N_v}$ | seconds |

### Boundary Conditions & Standard Values
- Standard **refresh rate $f_v$** for flicker-free CRT: **≥ 60 Hz** (typically **60–85 Hz**).
- Standard **color depths**: 1 bpp (mono), 4 bpp (16 colors), 8 bpp (256), 16 bpp (65,536), 24 bpp (16.7M truecolor).
- Standard **resolutions**: VGA 640×480, SVGA 800×600, XGA 1024×768, HD 1920×1080, 4K 3840×2160.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked for memory, always include **overhead for the entire frame buffer**, even blank pixels. Raster stores *all* pixels; Random does not. This is a frequent 1–2 mark trap question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Required for a Raster Frame Buffer

**Given:** A display has resolution **$N_h \times N_v$** with **$b$ bits per pixel**.

**To Derive:** Total memory **$M$** in bits and bytes.

### Step 1 — Identify the Number of Pixels
Every coordinate $(i, j)$ on the screen, where $i \in [0, N_h - 1]$ and $j \in [0, N_v - 1]$, corresponds to exactly **one** storage location in the frame buffer.

$$
\text{Total Pixels} = N_h \times N_v
$$

*Conversion logic:* The screen is a 2D grid. Counting all grid points in a rectangle with $N_h$ columns and $N_v$ rows is the product.

### Step 2 — Multiply by Bits per Pixel
Each pixel stores $b$ bits of color/intensity information. The frame buffer is therefore a flat 1D bit array of length:

$$
M_{\text{bits}} = N_h \times N_v \times b \quad [\text{bits}]
$$

*Conversion logic:* The total memory in bits is the count of independent storage cells times how wide each cell is.

### Step 3 — Convert to Bytes
Since **1 byte = 8 bits**, the size in bytes is:

$$
M_{\text{bytes}} = \dfrac{N_h \times N_v \times b}{8} \quad [\text{Bytes}]
$$

*Conversion logic:* Divide the total bit count by 8 to obtain a more human-readable memory unit.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)
For **Full HD 1920 × 1080, 24 bpp true color**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{bits}} &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 24 \\
&= 2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 \\
&= 49{,}766{,}400 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{bytes}} &= \dfrac{49{,}766{,}400}{8} \\
&= 6{,}220{,}800 \text{ Bytes} \\
&\approx 5.93 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single Full HD 24-bit frame requires about **5.93 MB** of frame buffer. At **60 fps**, the video controller must stream this data at **$5.93 \times 60 \approx 356 \text{ MB/s}$** — exactly the kind of bandwidth question KTU loves.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pixel Clock and Bandwidth

**Given:** Resolution **$N_h \times N_v$**, refresh rate **$f_v$**, color depth **$b$ bits**.

**To Derive:** Pixel clock frequency $f_p$ and required video bandwidth.

### Step 1 — Pixels Refreshed per Second
Each frame refreshes $N_h \times N_v$ pixels. There are $f_v$ frames per second.

$$
f_p = N_h \times N_v \times f_v
$$

*Conversion logic:* $N_h \times N_v$ is the pixel count per frame; multiplying by frames-per-second gives pixels-per-second.

### Step 2 — Bandwidth in Bits per Second
Each pixel carries $b$ bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= f_p \times b \\
&= N_h \times N_v \times f_v \times b \quad [\text{bits/sec}]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The data rate equals the pixel rate times the data width per pixel.

### Step 3 — Numerical Example
For **1280 × 720 @ 60 Hz, 24 bpp**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_p &= 1280 \times 720 \times 60 \\
&= 921{,}600 \times 60 \\
&= 55{,}296{,}000 \text{ pixels/sec} \\
&= 55.296 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= 55{,}296{,}000 \times 24 \\
&= 1{,}327{,}104{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} \\
&\approx 1.327 \text{ Gbps} \\
&\approx 158.2 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Problem Clue in Question | Use This Formula |
> |---|---|
> | "Memory needed for resolution …" | $M = N_h N_v b$ |
> | "Time taken to redraw one scanline" | $T_h = T_f / N_v = 1/(N_v f_v)$ |
> | "How many colors can be displayed" | $C = 2^b$ |
> | "Horizontal sync frequency" | $f_h = N_v f_v$ |
> | "Video controller bandwidth" | $BW = N_h N_v f_v b$ |
> | "Random scan memory" | $D \approx kV$ (vector-list based, not pixel-based) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Time per Scanline in Interlaced Displays

**Given:** A **1080i** (interlaced) display has $N_v = 1080$ scanlines per frame, refreshed at $f_v = 30$ Hz full frames (60 Hz field rate).

**To Derive:** Time to draw **one field** (half the scanlines).

### Step 1 — Full Frame Period
$$
T_f = \dfrac{1}{f_v} = \dfrac{1}{30} \approx 33.33 \text{ ms}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Period is the inverse of frequency.

### Step 2 — Field Period (Interlaced)
In interlaced scanning, one *frame* is split into two *fields* of $N_v/2$ scanlines each. Both fields are drawn in one frame period.

$$
T_{\text{field}} = \dfrac{T_f}{2} = 16.67 \text{ ms}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Two fields per frame means each field gets half the frame budget.

### Step 3 — Time per Scanline in a Field
$$
T_h = \dfrac{T_{\text{field}}}{N_v/2} = \dfrac{2 T_f}{N_v} = \dfrac{2}{N_v f_v}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Divide field time by the number of scanlines in that field.

For 1080i @ 30 Hz:

$$
T_h = \dfrac{2}{1080 \times 30} \approx 61.7 \text{ μs per scanline}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Interlacing was invented to **halve the bandwidth** required for early broadcast TV (NTSC, PAL). Each field carries half the vertical resolution, but the human visual system perceives the full image because the fields update on alternate half-frames. Modern displays use **progressive (non-interlaced)** scan to avoid the interline "comb" artifacts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Random Scan Display — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["CPU Application Program"] --> B["Display File Builder"]
    B --> C["Display File Display List of Draw Commands"]
    C --> D["Display Processor DP"]
    D --> E["Vector Generator"]
    D --> F["Character Generator ROM"]
    D --> G["Attribute Generator Color Line Style"]
    E --> H["X Deflection Amplifier"]
    E --> I["Y Deflection Amplifier"]
    H --> J["CRT Electron Beam"]
    I --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K["Phosphor Screen Visible Image"]
    D --> L["Refresh Clock 30 to 60 Hz"]
    L --> D
    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#5f1f3a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#3a5f1f,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#5f3a1f,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#000000,color:#00ff00
```

> **Block-Level Reading Guide:**
> - The **CPU** builds the **Display File** (vector list).
> - The **Display Processor** cyclically interprets the file, synchronized by the **Refresh Clock** (typically **30–60 Hz**).
> - Three sub-generators (**Vector, Character, Attribute**) drive the **CRT** deflection and intensity circuits.
> - The image is the **union of all beam strokes** drawn in one refresh cycle.

---

## 4.2 Raster Scan Display — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Application CPU GPU"] --> B["Frame Buffer Refresh Buffer RAM"]
    B --> C["Video Controller Scan Timing ASIC"]
    C --> D["Pixel Clock Generator"]
    C --> E["H Sync Pulse"]
    C --> F["V Sync Pulse"]
    B --> G["Color Look Up Table Palette"]
    G --> H["Digital to Analog Converter DAC"]
    D --> G
    H --> I["RGB Amplifiers"]
    I --> J["CRT Electron Beams R G B"]
    E --> K["Horizontal Deflection Yoke"]
    F --> L["Vertical Deflection Yoke"]
    J --> M["Phosphor Screen Pixel Matrix"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#5f1f3a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#3a5f1f,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#5f5f1f,color:#000000
    style H fill:#1f5f5f,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#000000,color:#00ff00
```

> **Block-Level Reading Guide:**
> - The **Frame Buffer** holds the entire pixel image (e.g., **1920 × 1080 × 24 bits ≈ 5.93 MB** for FHD).
> - The **Video Controller** runs a rigid state machine: **pixel clock → fetch → H-sync at line end → V-sync at frame end**.
> - The **CLUT** maps frame buffer indices to true RGB values.
> - The **DAC** converts digital pixel values to analog RGB voltages for the CRT guns (or directly to flat-panel column drivers in modern systems).

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Picture Description"] --> B["Display Type Question"]
    B --> C["Image is Line Oriented?"]
    C -->|Yes| D["Random Scan Display List of Vectors"]
    C -->|No| E["Raster Scan Frame Buffer of Pixels"]
    D --> F["Beam moves along vectors only"]
    E --> G["Beam sweeps entire screen systematically"]
    F --> H["Best for CAD and wireframes"]
    G --> I["Best for realistic shaded scenes and video"]
    style D fill:#3a5f1f,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#5f1f3a,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Random Scan and Raster Scan displays based on: (i) Picture definition, (ii) Refresh mechanism, and (iii) Memory requirement.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Picture Definition:** In a *Random Scan* display, the picture is defined as a set of **line-drawing commands** (vectors) stored in a *Display File*. In a *Raster Scan* display, the picture is defined as a 2D array of **pixel intensity/color values** stored in a *Frame Buffer*.

(ii) **Refresh Mechanism:** Random scan **redraws only the picture vectors** in a cyclic loop driven by a Display Processor; if the image is complex, more time is needed and flicker can occur. Raster scan **sweeps the entire screen** in a fixed left-to-right, top-to-bottom pattern at a constant refresh rate (e.g., **60 Hz**), regardless of image complexity.

(iii) **Memory Requirement:** Random scan memory is proportional to the *number of vectors* — small for simple wireframes. Raster scan memory is proportional to the *total pixel count* — large; e.g., **$1920 \times 1080 \times 24 = \approx 6$ MB per frame**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State and explain the role of the Frame Buffer in a Raster Scan Display System.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **Frame Buffer** (also called the *Refresh Buffer*) is a dedicated, dual-ported random-access memory in a raster display system that holds the **color or intensity value of every pixel** on the screen.

**Role:**
- It serves as the **logical image store**: a one-to-one mapping from memory locations to screen pixels.
- It is **continuously scanned by the video controller** at the pixel clock rate to generate the video signal.
- The CPU (or GPU) writes into it to *modify* the displayed image; the video controller *reads* from it to *refresh* the screen.
- Its size in bits is **$M = N_h \times N_v \times b$**, where $N_h, N_v$ are horizontal and vertical resolutions and $b$ is bits per pixel.

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of a Raster Scan Display System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the functions of the Video Controller, Frame Buffer, and Look-Up Table.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The Raster Scan Display System is built around three core components: the **Frame Buffer**, the **Video Controller**, and the **Color Look-Up Table (CLUT)**.

**1. Frame Buffer (Refresh Buffer):**
A high-speed, dual-ported RAM holding one entry per screen pixel. For resolution $N_h \times N_v$ with $b$ bits per pixel, its size is $M = N_h N_v b$ bits. The CPU/GPU writes the image data; the video controller reads it out sequentially. It is "content-agnostic" — the controller does not interpret what is being drawn.

**2. Video Controller:**
A dedicated timing ASIC that orchestrates the raster scan. It generates:
- The **pixel clock** — base timing for fetching one pixel.
- The **horizontal sync (H-sync)** pulse at the end of each scanline, blanking the beam during horizontal retrace.
- The **vertical sync (V-sync)** pulse at the end of each frame, blanking during vertical retrace.
- Read addresses that walk through the frame buffer in scan order.

**3. Color Look-Up Table (CLUT / Palette):**
A small, fast RAM (e.g., **256 × 24 bits**) that maps small frame buffer indices (say **8 bits**) to larger true-color values (say **24 bits**). This lets the frame buffer hold 256 simultaneous colors chosen from a 16.7 million color palette, drastically reducing memory and bus bandwidth.

**Working Flow:** CPU writes pixels → Frame Buffer stores them → Video Controller reads them in sync with H-sync/V-sync → CLUT expands indices to RGB → DAC produces analog signals → CRT guns/flat-panel drivers display them.

---

**(b) A raster display has a resolution of 1280 × 1024 pixels with 24 bits per pixel. Calculate: (i) Total frame buffer memory in MB, (ii) Pixel clock frequency at 75 Hz refresh, (iii) Required video bandwidth in MB/s.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N_h = 1280$, $N_v = 1024$, $b = 24$ bits, $f_v = 75$ Hz.

**(i) Frame Buffer Memory:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{bits}} &= 1280 \times 1024 \times 24 \\
&= 1{,}310{,}720 \times 24 \\
&= 31{,}457{,}280 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{bytes}} &= \dfrac{31{,}457{,}280}{8} = 3{,}932{,}160 \text{ Bytes} \\
&\approx 3.75 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Pixel Clock Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_p &= N_h \times N_v \times f_v \\
&= 1280 \times 1024 \times 75 \\
&= 1{,}310{,}720 \times 75 \\
&= 98{,}304{,}000 \text{ Hz} \\
&= 98.304 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Video Bandwidth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= f_p \times b = 98{,}304{,}000 \times 24 \\
&= 2{,}359{,}296{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}359{,}296{,}000}{8} \text{ Bytes/sec} \\
&= 294{,}912{,}000 \text{ Bytes/s} \\
&\approx 281.25 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **3.75 MB**, (ii) **98.304 MHz**, (iii) **≈ 281.25 MB/s**.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a Random Scan Display System. What is a Display File?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A **Random Scan Display System** uses a CRT whose electron beam is steered along geometric lines defined by the application. It consists of the following functional blocks:

**1. CPU / Application Program:** Generates high-level drawing primitives.
**2. Display File:** A list of drawing commands (e.g., `MOVE(x,y)`, `LINE(x1,y1,x2,y2)`, `POLY(...)`, `TEXT(s)`) stored in main memory.
**3. Display Processor (DP):** A dedicated controller that cycles through the display file at a fixed rate (typically **30–60 Hz**) and issues low-level signals.
**4. Vector Generator:** Produces analog X and Y deflection voltages that ramp linearly between the endpoints of a `LINE` command.
**5. Character Generator (ROM):** Maps ASCII character codes into a sequence of strokes to draw text.
**6. Attribute Generator:** Sets line style (solid/dashed), color, and intensity.
**7. CRT & Deflection Yokes:** Render the beam traces onto the phosphor screen.

**Working:** The CPU updates the display file when the picture changes. The DP traverses the file at the refresh rate; for each command, the appropriate generator modulates the X, Y, and Z (intensity) inputs of the CRT. The beam is **blanked during jumps** and **brightened during draws**, producing sharp vector graphics.
