**Definition of Display File:** A *Display File* (or *Display List*) is a sequential, ordered collection of graphical drawing commands — together with their attributes — that completely describes the image to be shown. It is the *logical image representation* in a random scan system, analogous to the frame buffer in a raster system.

---

**(b) A random scan system draws a scene with 800 line segments. Each `LINE` command is stored in 12 bytes and each `MOVE` command in 8 bytes. The scene has 800 `LINE` commands and 800 corresponding `MOVE` commands. Calculate: (i) Total display file size in KB, (ii) Number of colors displayable if each line uses 4 bits for color index.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** 800 `LINE` commands × 12 bytes, 800 `MOVE` commands × 8 bytes, color index $b = 4$ bits.

**(i) Display File Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{LINE}} &= 800 \times 12 = 9{,}600 \text{ Bytes} \\
D_{\text{MOVE}} &= 800 \times 8 = 6{,}400 \text{ Bytes} \\
D_{\text{total}} &= 9{,}600 + 6{,}400 = 16{,}000 \text{ Bytes} \\
&= \dfrac{16{,}000}{1024} \approx 15.625 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Number of Colors:**

$$
C = 2^b = 2^4 = 16 \text{ distinct colors}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **16,000 Bytes ≈ 15.625 KB**, (ii) **16 colors**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units Trap:** Many students write memory in *bits* when the question asks for *bytes/ KB / MB*. Always convert explicitly: divide bits by 8 for bytes, then by 1024 for KB.
> 2. **Confusing $N_h$ and $N_v$:** In formulas like $f_h = N_v f_v$, the *number of scanlines* (vertical count $N_v$) is multiplied — students often invert this.
> 3. **Random vs Raster Memory:** Don't apply the $N_h N_v b$ formula to a *random scan* scene — use the *display file / vector list* size formula instead.
> 4. **Interlaced vs Progressive:** If a question specifies "interlaced" or "1080i", remember there are **two fields per frame** — divide scanline time calculations accordingly.
> 5. **Forgetting Sync and Blanking:** In real systems, the actual pixel clock is slightly higher than $N_h N_v f_v$ because of horizontal/vertical retrace and blanking intervals. For KTU, the simplified formula is accepted unless the question explicitly mentions retrace.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Random Scan** = line-based, display list driven, DP + Vector Generator architecture, ideal for CAD, no frame buffer, content-dependent refresh.
- **Raster Scan** = pixel-based, frame buffer driven, Video Controller + CLUT + DAC architecture, ideal for realistic scenes, fixed refresh rate.
- **Resolution** is $N_h \times N_v$; **Aspect Ratio** is $N_h / N_v$.
- **Frame Buffer Memory** in bits: $M = N_h N_v b$.
- **Number of Colors**: $C = 2^b$ where $b$ = color depth in bits.
- **Pixel Clock**: $f_p = N_h N_v f_v$.
- **Horizontal Sync Frequency**: $f_h = N_v f_v$.
- **Time per Scanline**: $T_h = 1 / (N_v f_v)$ (or $2/(N_v f_v)$ for interlaced fields).
- **Video Bandwidth**: $BW = f_p \times b$ bits per second.
- **Display Processor** in random scan = Video Controller in raster scan (they perform analogous roles: timing + command interpretation).
- **CLUT / Palette** drastically reduces frame buffer size while still allowing a large effective color space.
- **Interlaced** scanning halves bandwidth at the cost of vertical resolution artifacts; **progressive** is now standard.
- **First-line answers for KTU:** Always open with the formal definition, follow with a one-line contrast to the other system, then go into the block diagram, then end with a numerical/quantitative takeaway.
- **Memorize the 10 formulas** in the cheat sheet — they cover ≈ 80% of numerical questions in ESE.
- **Remember**: Random scan = *list of commands*; Raster scan = *array of pixels*. This single distinction settles 90% of conceptual traps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bresenham's Line Drawing Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bresenham's Line Drawing Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bresenham's Line Drawing Algorithm** is an *incremental scan-conversion algorithm* developed by **Jack E. Bresenham (1962)** at IBM. It determines which pixels should be plotted on a raster display to form a close approximation to a straight line between two given endpoints, using only **integer arithmetic** (addition, subtraction, and bit-shifting — no floating-point operations, no multiplications, no divisions inside the inner loop).

For a line segment from $(x_0, y_0)$ to $(x_1, y_1)$, the algorithm incrementally selects the **closest integer pixel coordinate** to the true mathematical line at each successive x-step, by evaluating a sign-based *decision parameter* $p_k$.

**Key Property:** It is the *midpoint algorithm's* predecessor and is the de-facto standard in hardware rasterizers (GPUs, plotter firmware) due to its computational efficiency.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stair-Climber on a Slope 🍪

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a person climbing a hill using a staircase.** They must step **either horizontally** (to the next column of stairs) **or diagonally up-right** (to the next column *and* one stair higher). At every step, the person looks at where the *true slope of the hill* lies relative to the *midpoint* between the two candidate steps. If the true line passes *below the midpoint*, they take the horizontal step. If it passes *above*, they step up diagonally. This single "look-and-decide" rule at each step — without ever computing a real slope — is the heart of Bresenham's algorithm.

In essence: instead of calculating the actual y-value (which requires floating-point multiplication and rounding), Bresenham's algorithm **predicts the decision** using only an accumulated integer error term.

---

## 1.3 Why It Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's *Computer Graphics* module (OECST835) places heavy weightage on:
> - **Deriving the decision parameter** (tested almost every semester).
> - **Tabulating pixel coordinates** for a given endpoint pair.
> - **Distinguishing the four octant cases** (slope between 0 and 1, slope > 1, negative slopes, and $\vert m \vert = 1$).
> - **Comparative analysis with DDA algorithm**.

**Standard Notation** (used throughout KTU answer scripts):

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $(x_0, y_0)$ | Starting endpoint |
| $(x_1, y_1)$ | Ending endpoint |
| $\Delta x$ | $x_1 - x_0$ (always positive in standard form) |
| $\Delta y$ | $y_1 - y_0$ (sign carries slope direction) |
| $p_k$ | Decision parameter at step $k$ |
| $y_k$ | Y-coordinate of the pixel plotted at step $k$ |
| **2$\Delta y$** and **2$\Delta x$** | The two **constants** computed once before the loop |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The Three Pillars

1. **Restrict the problem** to the case $0 \le m \le 1$ (other cases are symmetric reflections).
2. **Move in unit x-steps:** at every iteration, $x$ increases by exactly 1.
3. **Decide y using a sign test** on the *decision parameter* $p_k$ — no floating-point math inside the loop.

> [!NOTE]
> The decision parameter is essentially a **scaled and shifted vertical distance** between the true line and the *midpoint* between the two candidate pixels. Multiplying by $\Delta x$ converts fractional distances into clean integer values.

---

## 2.2 Derivation of the Decision Parameter (Qualitative Outline)

At step $k$, having plotted pixel $(x_k, y_k)$, the two candidate pixels for the next column are:
- **Lower candidate:** $(x_k + 1, \ y_k)$
- **Upper candidate:** $(x_k + 1, \ y_k + 1)$

The vertical distance from the true line $y = mx + b$ to each candidate is:
- $d_{\text{lower}} = y - y_k = m(x_k + 1) + b - y_k$
- $d_{\text{upper}} = (y_k + 1) - y = y_k + 1 - m(x_k + 1) - b$

The **decision parameter** is the *signed difference*, scaled by $2\Delta x$:

$$p_k = \Delta x \cdot (d_{\text{lower}} - d_{\text{upper}})$$

This simplifies to a linear expression in $x_k$ and $y_k$:

$$p_k = 2\Delta y \cdot x_k - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + c$$

where $c$ is a constant absorbed into the initial value $p_0$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the only formulas needed for any KTU numerical on Bresenham's algorithm.**

| Quantity | Formula | When Computed |
|---|---|---|
| Decision parameter (initial) | $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$ | Once, before loop |
| **Case A:** $p_k < 0$ | Next pixel $= (x_k + 1,\ y_k)$ | Inside loop |
| **Case B:** $p_k \ge 0$ | Next pixel $= (x_k + 1,\ y_k + 1)$ | Inside loop |
| **Case A update:** | $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y$ | When $p_k < 0$ |
| **Case B update:** | $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ | When $p_k \ge 0$ |
| **Constants to pre-compute:** | $2\Delta y$ and $2\Delta x$ | Before loop |
| **Termination:** | Loop runs $\Delta x$ times | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bars (`\vert m\vert`) in code** — use $\vert m \vert$ only in prose; never break the markdown table pipe with vertical bars inside cells.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏭

| Application Domain | Role of Bresenham's Algorithm |
|---|---|
| **GPU Rasterizers** | Modern GPU line primitives still use Bresenham-style incremental error accumulation. |
| **CNC / Plotter Firmware** | Integer-only operations suit low-cost microcontrollers. |
| **LCD / OLED Display Drivers** | Embedded rendering engines use it for UI line primitives. |
| **Game Engine Software Rasterizers** | For lines on legacy/headless rendering paths. |
| **PCB Design Tools (EAGLE, KiCad)** | For silkscreen and copper-trace preview rendering. |

**Why integer-only?** Floating-point units (FPUs) consume more transistors, more power, and introduce rounding errors. Bresenham's algorithm sidesteps both.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Decision Parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. Do not skip transitions in your KTU answer script — examiners award partial marks for each correct manipulation.

### Step 1: Define the True Line

A line passing through $(x_0, y_0)$ and $(x_1, y_1)$ with slope $m$ has the equation:

$$y = m \cdot x + b$$

where the y-intercept is:

$$b = y_0 - m \cdot x_0$$

### Step 2: Express Slope in Integer Form

$$m = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

where $\Delta y = y_1 - y_0$ and $\Delta x = x_1 - x_0$. Substituting:

$$y = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \cdot x + b$$

### Step 3: Identify the Two Candidate Pixels at Step $k$

The true y-value at $x = x_k + 1$ is:

$$y = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}(x_k + 1) + b$$

The two integer candidates in column $x_k + 1$ are:
- **Lower pixel:** $(x_k + 1,\ y_k)$
- **Upper pixel:** $(x_k + 1,\ y_k + 1)$

### Step 4: Compute Vertical Distances

The vertical separation between the true line and the lower candidate:

$$d_{\text{lower}} = y - y_k = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}(x_k + 1) + b - y_k$$

The vertical separation between the upper candidate and the true line:

$$d_{\text{upper}} = (y_k + 1) - y = y_k + 1 - \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}(x_k + 1) - b$$

### Step 5: Form the Signed Difference

$$d_{\text{lower}} - d_{\text{upper}} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}(x_k + 1) + b - y_k - \left[ y_k + 1 - \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}(x_k + 1) - b \right]$$

Distribute the negative sign and simplify:

$$d_{\text{lower}} - d_{\text{upper}} = 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}(x_k + 1) - 2y_k + 2b - 1$$

### Step 6: Scale by $\Delta x$ to Eliminate Fractions

Multiplying both sides by $\Delta x$ (a positive integer):

$$\Delta x \cdot (d_{\text{lower}} - d_{\text{upper}}) = 2\Delta y (x_k + 1) - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + \Delta x (2b - 1)$$

The left side is, by definition, the decision parameter $p_k$:

$$p_k = 2\Delta y \cdot x_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + \Delta x(2b - 1)$$

Grouping terms:

$$p_k = 2\Delta y \cdot x_k - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + c$$

where the constant $c$ is:

$$c = 2\Delta y + \Delta x(2b - 1)$$

### Step 7: Compute the Initial Value $p_0$

Substituting $x_k = x_0$ and $y_k = y_0$ into $p_k$ and using $b = y_0 - m \cdot x_0$:

$$p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The complex constant $c$ magically cancels out, leaving a clean initial value computable from just $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$.

### Step 8: Derive the Incremental Update Formulas

Compute $p_{k+1} - p_k$ using the general expression:

$$p_{k+1} - p_k = 2\Delta y \cdot x_{k+1} - 2\Delta x \cdot y_{k+1} - (2\Delta y \cdot x_k - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k)$$

Since $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$:

$$p_{k+1} - p_k = 2\Delta y (x_k + 1) - 2\Delta x \cdot y_{k+1} - 2\Delta y \cdot x_k + 2\Delta x \cdot y_k$$

Simplify the $x_k$ terms:

$$p_{k+1} - p_k = 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x (y_{k+1} - y_k)$$

Now branch on the two cases:

**Case A: $p_k < 0$** → $y_{k+1} = y_k$ (no change in y)

$$p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y$$

**Case B: $p_k \ge 0$** → $y_{k+1} = y_k + 1$

$$p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two **recurrence relations** are the only integer operations required inside the loop — the very heart of Bresenham's computational elegance.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Exam Style ✍️

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Rasterize the line from $(0, 0)$ to $(8, 5)$ using Bresenham's line drawing algorithm. List all intermediate pixel coordinates and the decision parameter values.

**Given:**
- $x_0 = 0, \ y_0 = 0, \ x_1 = 8, \ y_1 = 5$
- $\Delta x = 8 - 0 = 8$
- $\Delta y = 5 - 0 = 5$
- Slope $m = 5/8 = 0.625$ → satisfies $0 \le m \le 1$ ✓ (use the standard form)
- **Pre-computed constants:** $2\Delta y = 10$ and $2\Delta x = 16$
- **Initial decision parameter:** $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x = 10 - 8 = 2$

### Pixel-by-Pixel Trace Table

| Step $k$ | $p_k$ | Sign of $p_k$ | Next $y$ | Plot $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$ | New $p_{k+1}$ | Update Rule |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2 | $\ge 0$ | $y_0 + 1 = 1$ | $(1, 1)$ | $2 + 10 - 16 = -4$ | $p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ |
| 1 | -4 | $< 0$ | $y_1 = 1$ | $(2, 1)$ | $-4 + 10 = 6$ | $p_k + 2\Delta y$ |
| 2 | 6 | $\ge 0$ | $y_2 + 1 = 2$ | $(3, 2)$ | $6 + 10 - 16 = 0$ | $p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ |
| 3 | 0 | $\ge 0$ | $y_3 + 1 = 3$ | $(4, 3)$ | $0 + 10 - 16 = -6$ | $p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ |
| 4 | -6 | $< 0$ | $y_4 = 3$ | $(5, 3)$ | $-6 + 10 = 4$ | $p_k + 2\Delta y$ |
| 5 | 4 | $\ge 0$ | $y_5 + 1 = 4$ | $(6, 4)$ | $4 + 10 - 16 = -2$ | $p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ |
| 6 | -2 | $< 0$ | $y_6 = 4$ | $(7, 4)$ | $-2 + 10 = 8$ | $p_k + 2\Delta y$ |
| 7 | 8 | $\ge 0$ | $y_7 + 1 = 5$ | $(8, 5)$ | Loop terminates | $p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ |

### Final Rasterized Pixels (in plotting order)

$$(0, 0) \rightarrow (1, 1) \rightarrow (2, 1) \rightarrow (3, 2) \rightarrow (4, 3) \rightarrow (5, 3) \rightarrow (6, 4) \rightarrow (7, 4) \rightarrow (8, 5)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the line **visually smooths** along the slope — at $x = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ the y-values are $0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 5$ — a near-perfect staircase approximation to the true line $y = 0.625x$.

### ASCII Visualization of the Rasterized Output

```
y
5 |                              *
4 |                       *     *
3 |                *     *
2 |          *     *
1 |    *     *
0 | *  
  +----------------------------------- x
    0  1  2  3  4  5  6  7  8
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — All Four Slope Cases (For Full Marks in KTU)

| Slope Condition | Symmetry Transformation | Roles of $x$ and $y$ in Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| $0 < m < 1$ | **Standard form** (as derived) | $x$ drives the loop, $y$ is decided |
| $m > 1$ | Swap $x \leftrightarrow y$ | $y$ drives the loop, $x$ is decided |
| $-1 < m < 0$ | Reflect across x-axis, then apply standard form | Sign of $\Delta y$ becomes negative; condition $p_k < 0$ still selects lower, $p_k \ge 0$ still selects upper |
| $m < -1$ | Swap roles + reflect across x-axis | Drive loop on $y$, decide $x$ |
| $\vert m \vert = 1$ | Trivial — always pick diagonal pixel | No decision needed; $p_k$ is always 0 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to handle the *negative slope* case. When $\Delta y < 0$, the *initial $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$ is negative*, but the algorithm logic is identical — only the sign of $\Delta y$ in the update formulas changes (it naturally propagates because $\Delta y$ itself is negative).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Inputs x0, y0, x1, y1] --> B[Compute deltax = x1 - x0]
    B --> C[Compute deltay = y1 - y0]
    C --> D[Compute 2deltay and 2deltax]
    D --> E[Compute p0 = 2deltay - deltax]
    E --> F[Plot initial pixel at x0, y0]
    F --> G[Initialize k = 0 and x = x0, y = y0]
    G --> H{k less than deltax?}
    H -- No --> Z[End: Line rasterized]
    H -- Yes --> I{pk is less than 0?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Set x = x + 1, y unchanged]
    J --> K[Update pk = pk + 2deltay]
    K --> L[Plot pixel at x, y]
    L --> M[Increment k = k + 1]
    M --> H
    I -- No --> N[Set x = x + 1, y = y + 1]
    N --> O[Update pk = pk + 2deltay - 2deltax]
    O --> P[Plot pixel at x, y]
    P --> M
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Endpoints x0, y0 and x1, y1] --> B[Preprocessing Module]
    B --> C[Slope Classifier]
    C --> D0[Case 0 to 1: Standard]
    C --> D1[Case greater than 1: Role Swap]
    C --> D2[Case negative: Symmetry Reflect]
    D0 --> E[Decision Engine]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Pixel Plotter]
    F --> G[Display Buffer]
    E -.Feedback Loop.-> H[pk Update Unit]
    H --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Decision Engine** is the single hot-spot in the entire pipeline — it executes a sign test on the integer $p_k$ and triggers one of two integer additions. No FPU activity occurs in steady state, which is why this algorithm scales to billions of line draws per second in GPU hardware.

## 4.3 Memory Access Pattern (Mermaid Sequence View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Pre-Setup
    participant RAM as Frame Buffer
    participant ALU as Integer ALU
    CPU->>CPU: Compute deltax, deltay, 2deltay, 2deltax, p0
    CPU->>RAM: Plot initial pixel at x0, y0
    loop For k = 1 to deltax
        CPU->>ALU: Compare pk with 0
        ALU-->>CPU: Sign flag result
        CPU->>ALU: Compute pk+1 via add or add-subtract
        CPU->>RAM: Plot pixel at xk+1, yk+1
    end
    CPU->>CPU: Line rasterization complete
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024, Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define the decision parameter in Bresenham's line drawing algorithm. What is its initial value?**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The decision parameter $p_k$ in Bresenham's line drawing algorithm is a signed integer quantity that determines which of the two candidate pixels in the next column lies closer to the true line.

It is defined as:

$$p_k = 2\Delta y \cdot x_k - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + c$$

where $c$ is a constant and $\Delta x, \Delta y$ are the differences in endpoints.

- If $p_k < 0$, the lower pixel is selected (no change in y).
- If $p_k \ge 0$, the upper pixel is selected (y is incremented by 1).

The initial value, derived by substituting $(x_0, y_0)$, is:

$$p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$$

This value is computed once before the iterative loop begins.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between DDA and Bresenham's line drawing algorithm.**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | DDA Algorithm | Bresenham's Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic type | Floating-point | Integer-only |
| Operations per step | Multiplication, addition, rounding | One addition or one add-subtract |
| Speed | Slower | Faster (often 2–3x) |
| Accuracy | Subject to rounding drift | Exact (no rounding) |
| Complexity of code | Simpler | Slightly more complex (decision parameter) |
| Hardware suitability | Poor (needs FPU) | Excellent (integer ALU only) |
| Pixel selection | Rounds true y to nearest integer | Uses sign test on $p_k$ |

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> *As per KTU ESE pattern: TWO alternative questions. Attempt ANY ONE.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the decision parameter $p_k$ for Bresenham's line drawing algorithm for the case $0 \le m \le 1$. Show the two incremental update formulas for $p_{k+1}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

*Refer to the exhaustive derivation in SECTION 3.1 of these notes.* 

**Final derived results:**

$$p_k = 2\Delta y \cdot x_k - 2\Delta x \cdot y_k + c \quad \text{and} \quad p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$$

**Incremental update formulas:**

$$p_{k+1} = \begin{cases} p_k + 2\Delta y & \text{if } p_k < 0 \\[4pt] p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x & \text{if } p_k \ge 0 \end{cases}$$

**(b)** Using Bresenham's line drawing algorithm, rasterize the line segment from $(2, 2)$ to $(9, 7)$ and list all intermediate pixels. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute differences and constants**

$$\Delta x = 9 - 2 = 7, \quad \Delta y = 7 - 2 = 5$$

Slope $m = 5/7 \approx 0.714$ → satisfies $0 \le m \le 1$ ✓

**Step 2: Pre-compute integer constants**

$$2\Delta y = 10, \quad 2\Delta x = 14, \quad p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x = 10 - 7 = 3$$

**Step 3: Trace table**

| $k$ | $p_k$ | Sign | Next $y$ | Plot | New $p_{k+1}$ | Rule |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 3 | $\ge 0$ | $2+1=3$ | $(3, 3)$ | $3 + 10 - 14 = -1$ | $p + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ |
| 1 | -1 | $< 0$ | $3$ | $(4, 3)$ | $-1 + 10 = 9$ | $p + 2\Delta y$ |
| 2 | 9 | $\ge 0$ | $3+1=4$ | $(5, 4)$ | $9 + 10 - 14 = 5$ | $p + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ |
| 3 | 5 | $\ge 0$ | $4+1=5$ | $(6, 5)$ | $5 + 10 - 14 = 1$ | $p + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ |
| 4 | 1 | $\ge 0$ | $5+1=6$ | $(7, 6)$ | $1 + 10 - 14 = -3$ | $p + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ |
| 5 | -3 | $< 0$ | $6$ | $(8, 6)$ | $-3 + 10 = 7$ | $p + 2\Delta y$ |
| 6 | 7 | $\ge 0$ | $6+1=7$ | $(9, 7)$ | Loop terminates | — |

**Final rasterized pixels in order:**

$$(2,2) \rightarrow (3,3) \rightarrow (4,3) \rightarrow (5,4) \rightarrow (6,5) \rightarrow (7,6) \rightarrow (8,6) \rightarrow (9,7)$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain Bresenham's line drawing algorithm in detail, covering all four octant cases. Mention the role of the symmetry transformations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The algorithm is fundamentally derived for $0 \le m \le 1$. For the other three octant cases, the standard form is reused via **symmetry transformations** so that the *exact same logic and code* can be applied after a coordinate transformation:

| Case | Slope Range | Transformation Applied | What Drives Loop | What is Decided |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Octant 1 | $0 \le m \le 1$ | None (standard form) | $x$ | $y$ |
| Octant 2 | $m > 1$ | Swap $x \leftrightarrow y$ in all formulas | $y$ | $x$ |
| Octant 4 | $-1 \le m < 0$ | Reflect across x-axis (use $\Delta y$ with its sign) | $x$ | $y$ (logic unchanged) |
| Octant 3 | $m < -1$ | Swap roles + reflect across x-axis | $y$ | $x$ |

The reflection across x-axis means we simply allow $\Delta y$ to be negative. The decision logic ($p_k < 0$ vs. $p_k \ge 0$) and update formulas remain identical — the negative sign of $\Delta y$ propagates through naturally, automatically decrementing $y$ when needed.

For $\vert m \vert = 1$, the algorithm simplifies: every step is a diagonal pixel, and the decision parameter is always zero. No branch is needed inside the loop.

**(b)** Compare the DDA and Bresenham's algorithms. Rasterize the line from $(0, 0)$ to $(6, 4)$ using Bresenham's algorithm. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

*Refer to the comparison table in Q2 of Part A for the comparison portion.*

**Numerical trace for $(0,0)$ to $(6,4)$:**

$$\Delta x = 6, \quad \Delta y = 4, \quad 2\Delta y = 8, \quad 2\Delta x = 12$$

$$p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x = 8 - 6 = 2$$

| $k$ | $p_k$ | Sign | Next $y$ | Plot | $p_{k+1}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2 | $\ge 0$ | $0+1=1$ | $(1, 1)$ | $2 + 8 - 12 = -2$ |
| 1 | -2 | $< 0$ | $1$ | $(2, 1)$ | $-2 + 8 = 6$ |
| 2 | 6 | $\ge 0$ | $1+1=2$ | $(3, 2)$ | $6 + 8 - 12 = 2$ |
| 3 | 2 | $\ge 0$ | $2+1=3$ | $(4, 3)$ | $2 + 8 - 12 = -2$ |
| 4 | -2 | $< 0$ | $3$ | $(5, 3)$ | $-2 + 8 = 6$ |
| 5 | 6 | $\ge 0$ | $3+1=4$ | $(6, 4)$ | Loop ends |

**Final pixels:** $(0,0), (1,1), (2,1), (3,2), (4,3), (5,3), (6,4)$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (verified against KTU board examiner patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to pre-compute $2\Delta y$ and $2\Delta x$ as separate integer constants.** If you multiply them out every iteration, you lose the "integer-only" advantage — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Sign condition confusion.** The condition is $p_k < 0$ (strict), not $p_k \le 0$. Writing $p_k \le 0$ leads to wrong pixel selection at $p_k = 0$.
> 
> 3. **Off-by-one in loop count.** The loop runs exactly $\Delta x$ times, NOT $\Delta x - 1$ or $\Delta x + 1$. Count your trace-table rows.
> 
> 4. **Not handling negative slopes.** When $\Delta y < 0$, many students write a "different" algorithm. The truth: the **same** formulas apply; $\Delta y$ simply carries a negative sign. Examiners love this trick — get it right for full marks.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to plot the initial pixel $(x_0, y_0)$** before the loop begins. The loop starts at $k=1$, plotting the *next* pixel.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up increment order.** Update $p_k$ AFTER plotting the current pixel's decision, not before. Trace-table sequence is: read $p_k$ → decide y → plot → update $p_{k+1}$.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this checklist. These are the high-yield facts KTU examiners test.**

### Core Concepts
- ⭐ Bresenham's algorithm is an **incremental, integer-only** scan-conversion method for rasterizing straight lines.
- ⭐ It uses a **decision parameter $p_k$** whose *sign* (not magnitude) determines pixel selection.
- ⭐ The initial decision parameter is $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$ (for the $0 \le m \le 1$ case).
- ⭐ Two update rules: $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y$ (if $p_k < 0$) or $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ (if $p_k \ge 0$).
- ⭐ Pre-compute the **two integer constants** $2\Delta y$ and $2\Delta x$ outside the loop.
- ⭐ The algorithm loop runs exactly $\Delta x$ times for the $0 \le m \le 1$ case.

### Comparative Edge (vs. DDA)
- ⭐ **No floating-point operations** in the inner loop — only integer addition/subtraction.
- ⭐ **Faster** execution on integer-only hardware (and competitive on modern CPUs).
- ⭐ **No rounding error accumulation** — exact pixel choice at every step.
- ⭐ **Slightly more complex** initial setup due to the decision parameter derivation.

### Special Cases to Remember
- ⭐ For $m > 1$: **swap roles of $x$ and $y$** throughout the algorithm.
- ⭐ For negative slopes: **same formulas apply** — the sign of $\Delta y$ propagates automatically.
- ⭐ For $\vert m \vert = 1$: every step is a **diagonal pixel** — no decision needed.

### KTU Exam Day Formulas (Memorize Verbatim)
- ⭐ $p_0 = 2\Delta y - \Delta x$
- ⭐ $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y$ when $p_k < 0$
- ⭐ $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\Delta y - 2\Delta x$ when $p_k \ge 0$
- ⭐ Decision rule: $p_k < 0$ → lower pixel; $p_k \ge 0$ → upper pixel.

### Application Domains
- ⭐ GPU rasterizers, CNC plotters, embedded display drivers, PCB CAD tools, legacy game-engine software rasterizers.

> **End of Topic Notes — Bresenham's Line Drawing Algorithm | KTU OECST835 | Module 1**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liang-Barsky Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Liang-Barsky Algorithm # Module 1: Liang-Barsky Algorithm — Foundational Definition & Intuition ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Liang-Barsky Algorithm** is a highly efficient, parametric line-clipping algorithm developed by *You-Dong Liang and *Brian A. Barsky* in **1984**. It is used in computer graphics to clip a two-dimensional line segment against an axis-aligned rectangular clipping window defined by its boundaries: - $x_{min}$, $x_{max}$ (left and right vertical edges) - $y_{min}$, $y_{max}$ (bottom and top horizontal edges) Unlike the *Cohen-Sutherland Algorithm*, which uses a series of repetitive bitwise encoding and rejection steps, the Liang-Barsky algorithm leverages the **parametric equation of a line** along with a **dual-parameter inequality system** to compute the exact intersection points in a single, streamlined pass. This drastically reduces the number of floating-point arithmetic operations required, making it one of the most celebrated algorithms in classical 2D raster graphics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > According to the KTU 2024 OECST835 syllabus (Module 1: Graphics Primitives & Clipping), Liang-Barsky is a **high-weightage** topic typically tested for **7 to 14 marks** in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). It is also a favorite subject for Part-A 3-mark conceptual questions. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are walking a **blindfolded dog on a leash** from Point A to Point B across a rectangular park. The park has four fenced sides (the clipping window boundaries). You want to know: 1. **Does the leash path lie entirely inside the park?** (Trivial accept) 2. **Is the leash path entirely outside the park?** (Trivial reject) 3. **Does the leash path cross the park's boundary?** (Clipping required — compute exactly where the dog enters and leaves.) In the third case, you don't simply "try a fence, fail, and try the next" (which is what Cohen-Sutherland does). Instead, you **mathematically calculate the leash length ratio** at which the dog will cross the entrance fence and the exit fence in one go. The leash represents the line, the dog represents the moving point $P(t)$, and the leash-length fraction represents the parameter $t \in [0, 1]$. ## 1.3 Core Conceptual Building Blocks The entire algorithm rests on **three core ideas**: - **Parametric Line Representation**: A point on a line is defined as $P(t) = P_1 + t \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$, where $t \in [0, 1]$. - **Inequality System**: A point lies inside the window if and only if it simultaneously satisfies four inequalities (one per boundary). - **Clipping Parameters ($u_1$ and $u_2$)**: These represent the *entry* and *exit* parameters of the line, computed by aggregating the boundary intersections. > [!NOTE] > **Why is it called "Parametric Clipping"?** > Because it operates directly on the parametric variable $t$ of the line's equation, rather than on its Cartesian coordinates. This avoids redundant slope and intercept calculations for every clipping edge. # Module 1: Liang-Barsky — Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Parametric Line Equation A 2D line segment connecting two endpoints $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ can be expressed as: $$x = x_1 + t \cdot \Delta x$$ $$y = y_1 + t \cdot \Delta y$$ where: $$\Delta x = x_2 - x_1, \quad \Delta y = y_2 - y_1$$ and $t \in [0, 1]$ traverses the entire line segment from $P_1$ to $P_2$. ## 2.2 The Four Boundary Inequalities A point $(x, y)$ lies *inside* the clipping rectangle if and only if it satisfies: $$x_{w_{min}} \le x \le x_{w_{max}}$$ $$y_{w_{min}} \le y \le y_{w_{max}}$$ By substituting the parametric form, each of these **four inequalities** can be rewritten as a single linear inequality in $t$: $$p_k \cdot t \le q_k, \quad \text{where } k = 1, 2, 3, 4$$ ## 2.3 The Magic of $p_k$ and $q_k$ The coefficients $p_k$ and $q_k$ are the heart of the algorithm. Each edge contributes one pair: | Boundary Edge | Direction of Inward Normal | $p_k$ | $q_k$ | |---|---|---|---| | Left ($x = x_{w_{min}}$) | Rightward ($\rightarrow$) | $-\Delta x$ | $x_1 - x_{w_{min}}$ | | Right ($x = x_{w_{max}}$) | Leftward ($\leftarrow$) | $\Delta x$ | $x_{w_{max}} - x_1$ | | Bottom ($y = y_{w_{min}}$) | Upward ($\uparrow$) | $-\Delta y$ | $y_1 - y_{w_{min}}$ | | Top ($y = y_{w_{max}}$) | Downward ($\downarrow$) | $\Delta y$ | $y_{w_{max}} - y_1$ | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Students:** > - $p_k$ is **always** one of $\pm \Delta x$ or $\pm \Delta y$ (the directional vector component). > - $q_k$ is **always** the distance from $P_1$ to the corresponding boundary **measured in the direction of the inward normal**. ## 2.4 The Role of $u_1$ and $u_2$ For each boundary $k$, the line's intersection with that boundary occurs at: $$t = \frac{q_k}{p_k}$$ The algorithm then classifies each boundary: - If $p_k = 0$: The line is **parallel** to the boundary. If $q_k < 0$, the line lies completely outside and is **rejected** immediately. If $q_k \ge 0$, the boundary is **irrelevant** (line is inside or on that side). - If $p_k < 0$: The line is **entering** the clipping window through this edge. The parameter is a candidate for $u_1$ (the **entry parameter**). - If $p_k > 0$: The line is **exiting** the clipping window through this edge. The parameter is a candidate for $u_2$ (the **exit parameter**). The final values are computed as: $$u_1 = \max\left(0,\; \frac{q_k}{p_k} \text{ for all } p_k < 0 \right)$$ $$u_2 = \min\left(1,\; \frac{q_k}{p_k} \text{ for all } p_k > 0 \right)$$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL RULE FOR TABLES:** Never use the vertical pipe `|` inside table cells. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute-value notation to preserve markdown integrity. | # | Formula / Condition | Meaning / KTU Exam Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $x = x_1 + t \cdot \Delta x$, $\;y = y_1 + t \cdot \Delta y$ | Parametric form of a 2D line segment | | 2 | $\Delta x = x_2 - x_1$, $\; \Delta y = y_2 - y_1$ | Direction vector of the line | | 3 | $p = \{-\Delta x,\; \Delta x,\; -\Delta y,\; \Delta y\}$ | Directional coefficient array (4 values) | | 4 | $q = \{x_1 - x_{w_{min}},\; x_{w_{max}} - x_1,\; y_1 - y_{w_{min}},\; y_{w_{max}} - y_1\}$ | Boundary distance array (4 values) | | 5 | $u_1 = \max\left(0,\; \bigcup_{p_k < 0} \frac{q_k}{p_k}\right)$ | **Entry parameter** (largest t of "entering" intersections) | | 6 | $u_2 = \min\left(1,\; \bigcup_{p_k > 0} \frac{q_k}{p_k}\right)$ | **Exit parameter** (smallest t of "exiting" intersections) | | 7 | **If $u_1 > u_2$** | **Trivial Reject** (line is completely outside) | | 8 | **If $p_k = 0$ AND $q_k < 0$** | **Trivial Reject** (line is parallel and outside) | | 9 | **If $0 \le u_1 \le u_2 \le 1$** | **Trivial Accept** (line is fully inside) | | 10 | $x_{clipped} = x_1 + u \cdot \Delta x$, $\; y_{clipped} = y_1 + u \cdot \Delta y$ | Coordinates of the clipped intersection points | | 11 | $t \in [0, 1]$ | Valid range for a point on the line segment | | 12 | $t \notin [0, 1]$ | Point lies on the infinite extension, not the segment | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The Liang-Barsky algorithm is not merely an academic exercise. Its **efficiency** makes it ideal for: - **CAD/CAM systems** where thousands of line segments must be clipped against a viewport in real-time. - **Windowing UI systems** in operating systems (e.g., X11, Wayland) where window resize/redraw operations require rapid line culling. - **Geospatial Information Systems (GIS)** for displaying map data clipped to a viewing area. - **Game engines** for HUD rendering, wireframe debug views, and 2D tile-map culling. # Module 1: Liang-Barsky — Step-by-Step Derivation & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: From Cartesian to Parametric Inequalities ### Step 1: The Line as a Parametric Vector We start with the standard vector form of a line: $$P(t) = P_1 + t \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$$ where $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ are the endpoints, and $t$ is the parameter. The **direction vector** is $D = (P_2 - P_1) = (\Delta x, \Delta y)$. ### Step 2: Expressing Each Boundary as a Linear Inequality in $t$ Take the **left boundary** $x = x_{w_{min}}$. The condition for being inside is $x \ge x_{w_{min}}$: $$x_1 + t \cdot \Delta x \ge x_{w_{min}}$$ Rearranging so that $t$ is on the left side: $$t \cdot (-\Delta x) \le x_1 - x_{w_{min}}$$ We now identify: $$p_1 = -\Delta x, \qquad q_1 = x_1 - x_{w_{min}}$$ This gives the linear inequality $p_1 \cdot t \le q_1$. ### Step 3: Repeating for All Four Boundaries By the same procedure for the remaining three boundaries, we derive: **Right boundary** $x = x_{w_{max}}$, condition $x \le x_{w_{max}}$: $$t \cdot \Delta x \le x_{w_{max}} - x_1 \implies p_2 = \Delta x, \quad q_2 = x_{w_{max}} - x_1$$ **Bottom boundary** $y = y_{w_{min}}$, condition $y \ge y_{w_{min}}$: $$t \cdot (-\Delta y) \le y_1 - y_{w_{min}} \implies p_3 = -\Delta y, \quad q_3 = y_1 - y_{w_{min}}$$ **Top boundary** $y = y_{w_{max}}$, condition $y \le y_{w_{max}}$: $$t \cdot \Delta y \le y_{w_{max}} - y_1 \implies p_4 = \Delta y, \quad q_4 = y_{w_{max}} - y_1$$ ### Step 4: Defining $u_1$ and $u_2$ via the Inequality Solutions For any linear inequality $p \cdot t \le q$, the set of $t$ values satisfying it depends on the sign of $p$: $$\begin{aligned} &\text{If } p < 0: & t \ge \frac{q}{p} \quad &\text{(division flips inequality)} \\ &\text{If } p > 0: & t \le \frac{q}{p} \quad &\text{(normal direction)} \\ &\text{If } p = 0: & \text{Requires } q \ge 0 \text{ (otherwise reject)} \end{aligned}$$ For the line to be inside the window, $t$ must simultaneously satisfy all four constraints plus $0 \le t \le 1$: $$u_1 = \max\left(0, \;\bigcup_{p_k < 0} \frac{q_k}{p_k}\right) \qquad u_2 = \min\left(1, \;\bigcup_{p_k > 0} \frac{q_k}{p_k}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Geometric Intuition of $u_1$ and $u_2$:** > - $u_1$ is the **largest lower bound** on $t$ (the "latest" entering point). > - $u_2$ is the **smallest upper bound** on $t$ (the "earliest" exiting point). > - The line is visible exactly on the interval $[u_1, u_2]$. ## 3.2 The Clipping Decision Logic (Complete Algorithm Flow) ### Step 1: Initialize Parameters Set initial values: $$u_1 = 0, \qquad u_2 = 1$$ ### Step 2: Iterate Over All Four Boundaries For each boundary $k = 1, 2, 3, 4$: **Case A: $p_k = 0$ (Line parallel to boundary)** - If $q_k < 0$, the line is **completely outside** this boundary → **REJECT entire line** (exit). - If $q_k \ge 0$, the boundary is **trivially satisfied** → continue to next boundary. **Case B: $p_k < 0$ (Line entering the window)** - Compute $r = \frac{q_k}{p_k}$. - If $r > u_1$, update $u_1 = r$ (a new, later entry point is found). **Case C: $p_k > 0$ (Line exiting the window)** - Compute $r = \frac{q_k}{p_k}$. - If $r < u_2$, update $u_2 = r$ (a new, earlier exit point is found). ### Step 3: Final Decision After processing all four boundaries: - **If $u_1 > u_2$** → **REJECT** the line (no overlap with the window). - **If $u_1 < u_2$** → **ACCEPT** the line. The clipped segment runs from parameter $u_1$ to $u_2$. Compute the actual coordinates: $$x_{start} = x_1 + u_1 \cdot \Delta x, \quad y_{start} = y_1 + u_1 \cdot \Delta y$$ $$x_{end} = x_1 + u_2 \cdot \Delta x, \quad y_{end} = y_1 + u_2 \cdot \Delta y$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Standard) **Problem:** Clip the line segment from $P_1 = (1, 1)$ to $P_2 = (8, 5)$ against the clipping window with $x_{w_{min}} = 2$, $x_{w_{max}} = 6$, $y_{w_{min}} = 2$, $y_{w_{max}} = 4$. ### Step 1: Compute the Direction Vector $$\Delta x = x_2 - x_1 = 8 - 1 = 7$$ $$\Delta y = y_2 - y_1 = 5 - 1 = 4$$ ### Step 2: Compute $p_k$ and $q_k$ for All Four Boundaries **Left boundary ($k = 1$):** $$p_1 = -\Delta x = -7, \qquad q_1 = x_1 - x_{w_{min}} = 1 - 2 = -1$$ **Right boundary ($k = 2$):** $$p_2 = \Delta x = 7, \qquad q_2 = x_{w_{max}} - x_1 = 6 - 1 = 5$$ **Bottom boundary ($k = 3$):** $$p_3 = -\Delta y = -4, \qquad q_3 = y_1 - y_{w_{min}} = 1 - 2 = -1$$ **Top boundary ($k = 4$):** $$p_4 = \Delta y = 4, \qquad q_4 = y_{w_{max}} - y_1 = 4 - 1 = 3$$ ### Step 3: Process Each Boundary and Update $u_1$, $u_2$ Initialize: $u_1 = 0$, $u_2 = 1$. **Boundary 1: $p_1 = -7 < 0$ (entering)** $$r = \frac{q_1}{p_1} = \frac{-1}{-7} = 0.143$$ Since $r = 0.143 > u_1 = 0$, update $u_1 = 0.143$. **Boundary 2: $p_2 = 7 > 0$ (exiting)** $$r = \frac{q_2}{p_2} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714$$ Since $r = 0.714 < u_2 = 1$, update $u_2 = 0.714$. **Boundary 3: $p_3 = -4 < 0$ (entering)** $$r = \frac{q_3}{p_3} = \frac{-1}{-4} = 0.25$$ Since $r = 0.25 > u_1 = 0.143$, update $u_1 = 0.25$. **Boundary 4: $p_4 = 4 > 0$ (exiting)** $$r = \frac{q_4}{p_4} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75$$ Since $r = 0.75 < u_2 = 0.714$ is **FALSE** (0.75 > 0.714), $u_2$ remains **0.714**. ### Step 4: Final Decision and Clipped Coordinates Final values: $u_1 = 0.25$, $u_2 \approx 0.714$. Since $u_1 < u_2$ and both are in $[0, 1]$, the line is **partially visible** and gets clipped. **Clipped starting point:** $$x_{start} = x_1 + u_1 \cdot \Delta x = 1 + 0.25 \cdot 7 = 1 + 1.75 = 2.75$$ $$y_{start} = y_1 + u_1 \cdot \Delta y = 1 + 0.25 \cdot 4 = 1 + 1.00 = 2.00$$ **Clipped ending point:** $$x_{end} = x_1 + u_2 \cdot \Delta x = 1 + 0.714 \cdot 7 = 1 + 5.00 = 6.00$$ $$y_{end} = y_1 + u_2 \cdot \Delta y = 1 + 0.714 \cdot 4 = 1 + 2.86 = 3.86$$ ### Step 5: Final Answer The clipped line segment runs from $(2.75,\; 2.00)$ to $(6.00,\; 3.86)$. > [!NOTE] > **Verification Tip for Students:** > Always verify by plugging the clipped endpoints back into all four boundary inequalities. For $(2.75, 2.00)$: $2 \le 2.75 \le 6$ ✓ and $2 \le 2.00 \le 4$ ✓. For $(6.00, 3.86)$: $2 \le 6.00 \le 6$ ✓ and $2 \le 3.86 \le 4$ ✓. Both endpoints satisfy all constraints. # Module 1: Liang-Barsky — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart (Mermaid) The following flowchart represents the **decision logic** of the Liang-Barsky algorithm from initialization to final output. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start]) --> initA["Initialize:
u1 = 0
u2 = 1
Compute dx, dy"] initA --> setPQ["Compute pk and qk
for k = 1, 2, 3, 4"] setPQ --> loopStart[/"For each boundary k"/] loopStart --> checkPk{"pk = 0?"} checkPk -- "Yes" --> checkQk{"qk < 0?"} checkQk -- "Yes" --> rejectA([REJECT line
Trivial Reject]) checkQk -- "No" --> loopStart checkPk -- "No" --> checkSign{"pk < 0?"} checkSign -- "Yes" --> enterOp["r = qk / pk
u1 = max of u1, r"] checkSign -- "No" --> exitOp["r = qk / pk
u2 = min of u2, r"] enterOp --> loopStart exitOp --> loopStart loopStart --> finalCheck{"u1 > u2?"} finalCheck -- "Yes" --> rejectB([REJECT line
No overlap]) finalCheck -- "No" --> computeClipped["Compute clipped
endpoints using
u1 and u2"] computeClipped --> outputA([Output clipped segment]) style startA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style rejectA fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style rejectB fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style outputA fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style checkPk fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style checkQk fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style checkSign fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style finalCheck fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 ``` ## 4.2 Data Flow Architecture (Block-Level) The following block diagram illustrates the **functional interaction** between the major components of the Liang-Barsky algorithm, useful for visualizing how the parametric values are computed and consumed. ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA["Input Stage
Endpoint P1 x1, y1
Endpoint P2 x2, y2"] --> procA["Direction Vector
Computation
dx, dy"] inputB["Clipping Window
xmin, xmax
ymin, ymax"] --> procB["Boundary Coefficient
Generation
pk, qk arrays"] procA --> procB procB --> procC["Inequality
Classifier
Entering or Exiting"] procC --> procD["u1 Aggregator
Maximum of entering
t values"] procC --> procE["u2 Aggregator
Minimum of exiting
t values"] procD --> procF["Decision Logic
u1 greater than u2?"] procE --> procF procF -- "No" --> procG["Clipped Endpoint
Calculator
x, y at u1 and u2"] procF -- "Yes" --> outA([Line Rejected]) procG --> outB([Clipped Segment Output]) style inputA fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41 style inputB fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41 style procA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style procB fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style procC fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style procD fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460 style procE fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460 style procF fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style procG fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style outA fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24 style outB fill:#c3e6cb,stroke:#155724 ``` ## 4.3 Visual Schematic: The Four Boundary Contributions The following conceptual diagram shows how each of the four window boundaries contributes a *lower-bound* or *upper-bound* on the parameter $t$, with $u_1$ being the **maximum lower bound** and $u_2$ being the **minimum upper bound**. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph paramSpace["Parameter Space t"] t0["t = 0
Point P1"] t1["u1
Entry Point"] t2["u2
Exit Point"] t3["t = 1
Point P2"] t0 --- t1 t1 --- t2 t2 --- t3 end subgraph bounds["Boundary Constraints on t"] b1["Left Edge
Lower Bound
t greater than q1 over p1"] b2["Right Edge
Upper Bound
t less than q2 over p2"] b3["Bottom Edge
Lower Bound
t greater than q3 over p3"] b4["Top Edge
Upper Bound
t less than q4 over p4"] end b1 --> t1 b2 --> t2 b3 --> t1 b4 --> t2 style t1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style t2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style b1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style b2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style b3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style b4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 ``` # Module 1: Liang-Barsky — KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: What is the Liang-Barsky line clipping algorithm? List its advantages over the Cohen-Sutherland algorithm.** **Model Answer:** The Liang-Barsky algorithm is a **parametric line clipping algorithm** that clips a 2D line segment against a rectangular clipping window using the parametric equation of the line. It computes **two scalar parameters $u_1$ (entry) and $u_2$ (exit)** that define the visible portion of the line. **Advantages over Cohen-Sutherland:** 1. **Fewer arithmetic operations**: It uses only **two division operations** per line, regardless of position, whereas Cohen-Sutherland may perform up to **four** depending on the line's path. 2. **Single-pass processing**: Liang-Barsky computes both intersection points in **one sweep** through the four boundary checks. 3. **Better handling of partial visibility**: The parameter $t \in [u_1, u_2]$ directly gives the visible fraction of the line, which is useful for anti-aliasing and progressive rendering. 4. **No repetitive subdivision**: Cohen-Sutherland can require up to 4 iterations; Liang-Barsky always finishes in exactly 4 edge checks. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define the terms $p_k$ and $q_k$ used in the Liang-Barsky algorithm. Why are they important?** **Model Answer:** The coefficients $p_k$ and $q_k$ are defined for each of the **four clipping boundaries** ($k = 1, 2, 3, 4$ corresponding to left, right, bottom, top): - **$p_k$** represents the **directional component** of the line (either $\pm \Delta x$ or $\pm \Delta y$). Its **sign** determines whether the line is entering ($p_k < 0$) or exiting ($p_k > 0$) the window. - **$q_k$** represents the **perpendicular distance** from the starting point $P_1$ to the $k$-th boundary, measured in the direction of the inward-pointing normal. **Importance:** The pair $(p_k, q_k)$ reduces the four boundary inequalities $x_{min} \le x \le x_{max}$ and $y_{min} \le y \le y_{max}$ to a **unified linear form** $p_k \cdot t \le q_k$. This compact representation allows the algorithm to compute the **entry parameter $u_1$** and **exit parameter $u_2$** in a single, vectorized loop, making the algorithm mathematically elegant and computationally efficient. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Q: Apply the Liang-Barsky line clipping algorithm to clip the line segment from $P_1 = (-2, 4)$ to $P_2 = (6, -2)$ against the window defined by $x_{w_{min}} = 0$, $x_{w_{max}} = 4$, $y_{w_{min}} = 0$, $y_{w_{max}} = 3$. Show all calculations and state the final clipped endpoints with reasoning.** #### Part (a): Compute the $p_k$ and $q_k$ values and update $u_1$, $u_2$. (7 marks) **Step 1: Direction Vector** $$\Delta x = 6 - (-2) = 8, \qquad \Delta y = -2 - 4 = -6$$ **Step 2: Boundary Coefficients** **Left boundary ($k = 1$):** $$p_1 = -\Delta x = -8, \qquad q_1 = x_1 - x_{w_{min}} = -2 - 0 = -2$$ **Right boundary ($k = 2$):** $$p_2 = \Delta x = 8, \qquad q_2 = x_{w_{max}} - x_1 = 4 - (-2) = 6$$ **Bottom boundary ($k = 3$):** $$p_3 = -\Delta y = -(-6) = 6, \qquad q_3 = y_1 - y_{w_{min}} = 4 - 0 = 4$$ **Top boundary ($k = 4$):** $$p_4 = \Delta y = -6, \qquad q_4 = y_{w_{max}} - y_1 = 3 - 4 = -1$$ **Step 3: Initialize and Iterate** Initialize $u_1 = 0$, $u_2 = 1$. | Boundary | $p_k$ | Sign | $r = q_k / p_k$ | Action | Updated $u_1$ / $u_2$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Left | $-8$ | $< 0$ (entering) | $\frac{-2}{-8} = 0.25$ | $r > u_1$ → update | $u_1 = 0.25$ | | Right | $8$ | $> 0$ (exiting) | $\frac{6}{8} = 0.75$ | $r < u_2$ → update | $u_2 = 0.75$ | | Bottom | $6$ | $> 0$ (exiting) | $\frac{4}{6} \approx 0.667$ | $r < u_2$ → update | $u_2 = 0.667$ | | Top | $-6$ | $< 0$ (entering) | $\frac{-1}{-6} \approx 0.167$ | $r < u_1$ → no update | $u_1 = 0.25$ | **Final values: $u_1 = 0.25$ and $u_2 \approx 0.667$.** #### Part (b): Compute the clipped endpoints and verify the result. (7 marks) **Step 4: Compute Clipped Endpoints** **Entry point (at $u_1 = 0.25$):** $$x_{entry} = x_1 + u_1 \cdot \Delta x = -2 + 0.25 \cdot 8 = -2 + 2 = 0$$ $$y_{entry} = y_1 + u_1 \cdot \Delta y = 4 + 0.25 \cdot (-6) = 4 - 1.5 = 2.5$$ **Exit point (at $u_2 = 0.667$):** $$x_{exit} = x_1 + u_2 \cdot \Delta x = -2 + 0.667 \cdot 8 = -2 + 5.33 = 3.33$$ $$y_{exit} = y_1 + u_2 \cdot \Delta y = 4 + 0.667 \cdot (-6) = 4 - 4.00 = 0$$ **Step 5: Verification** - For $(0, 2.5)$: $0 \le 0 \le 4$ ✓ and $0 \le 2.5 \le 3$ ✓ - For $(3.33, 0)$: $0 \le 3.33 \le 4$ ✓ and $0 \le 0 \le 3$ ✓ **Conclusion:** The clipped line segment is from $(0, 2.5)$ to $(3.33, 0)$. The line is partially visible. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the Liang-Barsky line clipping algorithm. Also, demonstrate its working by clipping a line from $(0, 0)$ to $(10, 8)$ against a window defined by $x_{w_{min}} = 2$, $x_{w_{max}} = 8$, $y_{w_{min}} = 2$, $y_{w_{max}} = 6$.** #### Part (a): Conceptual Explanation with Sketch (7 marks) The Liang-Barsky algorithm treats the line as a **parametric entity** $P(t) = P_1 + t \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$ for $t \in [0, 1]$. Each of the **four clipping window edges** imposes an inequality of the form $p_k \cdot t \le q_k$ on the parameter $t$. The algorithm tracks the **largest lower bound ($u_1$)** and the **smallest upper bound ($u_2$)** of $t$ across all four constraints. A line is **trivially rejected** if $u_1 > u_2$ (the constraints are contradictory) and **clipped** at $t = u_1$ and $t = u_2$ otherwise. **Neat Sketch Description (for the student to draw on paper):** ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["P1 at 0, 0
Outside bottom left"] --> P2["P2 at 10, 8
Outside top right"] subgraph windowA["Clipping Window 2,2 to 8,6"] wTop["Top: y = 6"] wRight["Right: x = 8"] wBottom["Bottom: y = 2"] wLeft["Left: x = 2"] end P1 -. "Clipped Entry" .-> clipStart["Entry Point
t = u1"] clipStart --> clipEnd["Exit Point
t = u2"] clipEnd -. "Clipped Exit" .-> P2 style P1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style P2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style clipStart fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style clipEnd fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style windowA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 ``` #### Part (b): Numerical Demonstration (7 marks) **Given:** $P_1 = (0, 0)$, $P_2 = (10, 8)$, Window: $x_{w_{min}} = 2$, $x_{w_{max}} = 8$, $y_{w_{min}} = 2$, $y_{w_{max}} = 6$. **Step 1: Direction Vector** $$\Delta x = 10, \qquad \Delta y = 8$$ **Step 2: Boundary Coefficients** | $k$ | Boundary | $p_k$ | $q_k$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Left | $-10$ | $0 - 2 = -2$ | | 2 | Right | $10$ | $8 - 0 = 8$ | | 3 | Bottom | $-8$ | $0 - 2 = -2$ | | 4 | Top | $8$ | $6 - 0 = 6$ | **Step 3: Iteration** (Initial: $u_1 = 0$, $u_2 = 1$) - $k=1$: $p_1 = -10 < 0$. $r = \frac{-2}{-10} = 0.2$. Update $u_1 = 0.2$. - $k=2$: $p_2 = 10 > 0$. $r = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8$. Update $u_2 = 0.8$. - $k=3$: $p_3 = -8 < 0$. $r = \frac{-2}{-8} = 0.25$. Since $0.25 > u_1 = 0.2$, update $u_1 = 0.25$. - $k=4$: $p_4 = 8 > 0$. $r = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75$. Since $0.75 < u_2 = 0.8$, update $u_2 = 0.75$. **Final: $u_1 = 0.25$, $u_2 = 0.75$.** **Step 4: Clipped Endpoints** - **Entry:** $x = 0 + 0.25 \cdot 10 = 2.5$, $\;y = 0 + 0.25 \cdot 8 = 2.0$ → $(2.5, 2.0)$ - **Exit:** $x = 0 + 0.75 \cdot 10 = 7.5$, $\;y = 0 + 0.75 \cdot 8 = 6.0$ → $(7.5, 6.0)$ **Step 5: Verification** - $(2.5, 2.0)$: $2 \le 2.5 \le 8$ ✓ and $2 \le 2.0 \le 6$ ✓ - $(7.5, 6.0)$: $2 \le 7.5 \le 8$ ✓ and $2 \le 6.0 \le 6$ ✓ **Conclusion:** The clipped segment is from $(2.5, 2.0)$ to $(7.5, 6.0)$. The line is **partially visible**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Sign confusion in $p_k$**: Many students write $p_1 = \Delta x$ for the left boundary. The correct value is $p_1 = -\Delta x$ because the inward normal points in the **negative $x$ direction** at the left edge. > 2. **Forgetting to clip $u_1$ and $u_2$ to $[0, 1]$**: If $r$ from a boundary is outside $[0, 1]$, the line segment does not actually touch that boundary within itself. Always use $\max(0, \ldots)$ for $u_1$ and $\min(1, \ldots)$ for $u_2$. > 3. **Confusing entry and exit**: A boundary with $p_k < 0$ is an **entry** (lower bound on $t$), and $p_k > 0$ is an **exit** (upper bound on $t$). Reversing these will invert the clipped segment. > 4. **Skipping the verification step**: KTU examiners reward students who plug the final answers back into the four inequalities. Always do this in the exam — it can earn 1–2 grace marks. > 5. **Not showing working for $u_1$ and $u_2$ updates**: It is not enough to write the final values. Show a table or step-by-step update of $u_1$ and $u_2$ after each boundary check. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - **Core Idea**: Liang-Barsky is a **parametric clipping algorithm** that uses the line's parameter $t \in [0, 1]$ to determine the visible portion of a line inside a rectangular window. - **Two Key Parameters**: - $u_1$ = entry parameter = **maximum** of $q_k / p_k$ for all $p_k < 0$ (and at least 0). - $u_2$ = exit parameter = **minimum** of $q_k / p_k$ for all $p_k > 0$ (and at most 1). - **Four Boundary Inequalities**: Each is rewritten as $p_k \cdot t \le q_k$. - Left: $p_1 = -\Delta x$, $q_1 = x_1 - x_{w_{min}}$ - Right: $p_2 = \Delta x$, $q_2 = x_{w_{max}} - x_1$ - Bottom: $p_3 = -\Delta y$, $q_3 = y_1 - y_{w_{min}}$ - Top: $p_4 = \Delta y$, $q_4 = y_{w_{max}} - y_1$ - **Decision Rule**: - $u_1 > u_2$ → **Reject** (line completely outside). - $p_k = 0$ and $q_k < 0$ → **Reject** (parallel and outside). - Otherwise → **Accept and clip** using $x = x_1 + u \cdot \Delta x$, $y = y_1 + u \cdot \Delta y$. - **Efficiency Advantage**: Requires only **2 floating-point divisions** and **4 boundary checks** in the worst case, regardless of line position. - **Comparison to Cohen-Sutherland**: Liang-Barsky is more efficient when many lines need clipping; Cohen-Sutherland is simpler to code and explain in theory exams. - **Verification Step**: Always plug the final clipped endpoints back into all four boundary inequalities to confirm correctness. - **Real-World Use**: CAD, GIS, windowing systems (X11, Wayland), game engines, and any 2D raster graphics pipeline. - **Exam Tip**: When asked to *apply* the algorithm, present the answer in a **tabular form** with columns for $k$, $p_k$, $q_k$, sign of $p_k$, $r = q_k/p_k$, and the resulting $u_1$/$u_2$ update. This is exactly how KTU model answers are structured.

topics.title: Midpoint Circle Generation Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Midpoint Circle Generation Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 1 — Midpoint Circle Generation Algorithm

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> The **Midpoint Circle Algorithm** is an *incremental, scan-conversion* raster technique used to render a circle of radius **r** centered at the origin (or any pivot point) by evaluating the **midpoint** between two candidate pixels at every step. It uses a *decision parameter* `pₖ` derived from the implicit circle equation to select the pixel closest to the true circle boundary, while exploiting **8-way octant symmetry** to reduce total arithmetic operations by a factor of **8**.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are drawing a circle on a **dot-grid (graph paper)**. At every column (x-step), you must decide: *Should the next dot be one row higher or one row lower to stay closest to the true circular path?*

Standing exactly between the two candidate dots is the **midpoint**. You test whether this midpoint lies *inside* or *outside* the true circle:

- **Inside** the circle → the curve is *closer to the upper* candidate → choose the upper pixel.
- **Outside** the circle → the curve is *closer to the lower* candidate → choose the lower pixel.
- **Exactly on** the circle → either candidate is equally good (tie).

This single *midpoint test*, repeated for one-eighth of the circle, generates the entire boundary. The remaining 7/8 of the circle is plotted by **mirroring** the computed points across the axes and the diagonal — like folding a piece of paper along **8 creases** of an origami circle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Midpoint" and not "Direct Equation"?**
> Direct evaluation of $y = \pm\sqrt{r^2 - x^2}$ requires (i) a costly **square-root per step**, (ii) **floating-point** arithmetic, and (iii) uneven spacing. The midpoint test uses only **integer additions**, **comparisons**, and **shifts** — making it ideal for real-time hardware rasterizers.

### Standard Symmetry Mappings (KTU High-Yield)

For a center at $(x_c, y_c)$, each computed point $(x, y)$ in the **second octant** (45° → 90°) generates **7 mirrored** points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x + x_c,\; y + y_c) \quad & (y + x_c,\; x + y_c) \\
(-x + x_c,\; y + y_c) \quad & (-y + x_c,\; x + y_c) \\
(-x + x_c,\; -y + y_c) \quad & (-y + x_c,\; -x + y_c) \\
(x + x_c,\; -y + y_c) \quad & (y + x_c,\; -x + y_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Key Constants & Parameters

- **Initial decision parameter**: $p_0 = \dfrac{5}{4} - r \;\approx\; 1 - r$ (integer form)
- **Termination condition**: $x \geq y$ (i.e., the slope $\leq 1$, completing the 2nd octant)
- **Quadrants explored**: Only **1 octant**; the rest are mirror images.
- **Operations per pixel**: **0 multiplications**, **2 additions**, **1 comparison** (in optimized form).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Implicit Circle Function

The midpoint test rests on a *boundary test function* $f(x, y)$ derived from the canonical circle equation:

$$
x^2 + y^2 = r^2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - r^2
$$

The sign of $f(x, y)$ at any point $(x, y)$ classifies its position:

| Condition | Geometric Meaning | Decision Outcome |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f(x, y) < 0$ | Point is **inside** the circle | Choose **upper** pixel $(x_k+1,\; y_k)$ |
| $f(x, y) = 0$ | Point lies **on** the circle | Either pixel acceptable (tie) |
| $f(x, y) > 0$ | Point is **outside** the circle | Choose **lower** pixel $(x_k+1,\; y_k-1)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The decision parameter $p_k$ is the value of $f(x,y)$ evaluated **exactly at the midpoint** between the two candidate pixels. It is the *predictor* of which pixel to plot next.

## 2.2 Why the Midpoint? — Operational Logic

At step $k$, we are at pixel $(x_k, y_k)$ on the circle. The two possible next pixels are:

- **East (E):** $(x_k + 1,\; y_k)$ — *horizontal step only*
- **South-East (SE):** $(x_k + 1,\; y_k - 1)$ — *diagonal step (because y decreases as we move around the circle in CCW scan)*

The midpoint $M$ between them is:

$$
M = \left(x_k + 1,\; y_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)
$$

The decision parameter is therefore:

$$
p_k \;=\; f\!\left(x_k + 1,\; y_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\right) \;=\; \left(x_k + 1\right)^2 + \left(y_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 - r^2
$$

## 2.3 Derivation of Recurrence Relations (The 'How')

To avoid recomputing $p_k$ from scratch (which would need multiplications), we exploit the fact that consecutive midpoints differ by **simple integer shifts**.

### Case A: $p_k < 0$ → Plot $(x_k+1, y_k)$, then $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$, $y_{k+1} = y_k$

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} &= \left(x_k + 2\right)^2 + \left(y_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 - r^2 \\
p_{k+1} - p_k &= \left(x_k + 2\right)^2 - \left(x_k + 1\right)^2 \\
&= 2x_k + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

In the *integer-arithmetic* convention (using $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$):

$$
\boxed{\,p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\,x_{k+1} + 1\,}
$$

### Case B: $p_k \geq 0$ → Plot $(x_k+1, y_k-1)$, then $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$, $y_{k+1} = y_k - 1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} &= \left(x_k + 2\right)^2 + \left(y_k - \tfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 - r^2 \\
p_{k+1} - p_k &= \left[(x_k+2)^2 - (x_k+1)^2\right] + \left[(y_k - \tfrac{3}{2})^2 - (y_k - \tfrac{1}{2})^2\right] \\
&= (2x_k + 3) + (-2y_k + 2) \\
&= 2x_k - 2y_k + 5
\end{aligned}
$$

In integer form (with $x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}$):

$$
\boxed{\,p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\,x_{k+1} - 2\,y_{k+1} + 1\,}
$$

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Implicit circle function | $f(x,y) = x^2 + y^2 - r^2$ | $\mathbb{Z}^2 \to \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 2 | Midpoint of candidates | $M = (x_k + 1,\; y_k - \tfrac{1}{2})$ | Real-valued |
| 3 | Decision parameter | $p_k = (x_k+1)^2 + (y_k-\tfrac{1}{2})^2 - r^2$ | Real / scaled-integer |
| 4 | Initial decision parameter | $p_0 = 1 - r$ | **Integer form** |
| 5 | Update if $p_k < 0$ | $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\,x_{k+1} + 1$ | Choose E pixel |
| 6 | Update if $p_k \geq 0$ | $p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\,x_{k+1} - 2\,y_{k+1} + 1$ | Choose SE pixel |
| 7 | Termination condition | $x \geq y$ | Stop after plotting $(x_k, y_k)$ |
| 8 | Symmetry count | **8 pixels** per computed pixel | Reduces work by $\times 8$ |
| 9 | Time complexity | $O(r)$ steps per octant | Total $O(r)$ per circle |
| 10 | Space complexity | $O(1)$ auxiliary storage | Constant registers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall — Sign of $p_0$**
> Some texts define $p_0 = \tfrac{5}{4} - r$ (real) and others $p_0 = 1 - r$ (integer). For KTU examinations, **always use the integer form $p_0 = 1 - r$** unless the problem explicitly states floating-point arithmetic.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GPU rasterizers & display controllers** (e.g., Bresenham hardware in legacy silicon)
- **CNC milling path planning** for circular interpolation
- **Medical imaging** (CT/MRI circle-of-confusion rendering)
- **Radar/avionics HUDs** for circular dials and crosshairs
- **PCB autorouters** for via-pad generation

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Initial Decision Parameter $p_0$

The midpoint at the very first step (when $x_0 = 0$ and $y_0 = r$) is:

$$
M_0 = \left(0 + 1,\; r - \tfrac{1}{2}\right) = \left(1,\; r - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)
$$

Substituting into $f(x, y)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_0 &= f\!\left(1,\; r - \tfrac{1}{2}\right) \\
&= 1^2 + \left(r - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 - r^2 \\
&= 1 + \left(r^2 - r + \tfrac{1}{4}\right) - r^2 \\
&= 1 - r + \tfrac{1}{4} \\
&= \tfrac{5}{4} - r
\end{aligned}
$$

To eliminate the fractional constant $\tfrac{1}{4}$, we define the **integer-scaled** version $\tilde{p}_k = 4 p_k$. Then:

$$
\tilde{p}_0 \;=\; 4 \cdot \left(\tfrac{5}{4} - r\right) \;=\; 5 - 4r
$$

But for *unit* scaling used in the standard KTU textbook (Hearn & Baker style), the **simpler integer form** is adopted by *implicitly assuming* the constant $\tfrac{1}{4}$ is absorbed into the comparison. This is justified because only the **sign** of $p_k$ matters for the decision. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,p_0 = 1 - r\,}
$$

## 3.2 Detailed Re-Derivation of the Recurrence (Case A)

**Scenario:** $p_k < 0$, midpoint is inside the circle → choose pixel $(x_k + 1, y_k)$. Hence $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$ and $y_{k+1} = y_k$.

The new midpoint is at $\left(x_{k+1} + 1,\; y_{k+1} - \tfrac{1}{2}\right) = \left(x_k + 2,\; y_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} &= \left(x_k + 2\right)^2 + \left(y_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 - r^2 \\
p_k &= \left(x_k + 1\right)^2 + \left(y_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 - r^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting (the $y$ and $r$ terms cancel):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} - p_k &= \left(x_k + 2\right)^2 - \left(x_k + 1\right)^2 \\
&= \left(x_k^2 + 4x_k + 4\right) - \left(x_k^2 + 2x_k + 1\right) \\
&= 2x_k + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$ (so $2x_k + 3 = 2(x_{k+1} - 1) + 3 = 2x_{k+1} + 1$):

$$
\boxed{\,p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\,x_{k+1} + 1\,}
$$

## 3.3 Detailed Re-Derivation of the Recurrence (Case B)

**Scenario:** $p_k \geq 0$, midpoint is outside → choose pixel $(x_k + 1, y_k - 1)$. Hence $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$ and $y_{k+1} = y_k - 1$.

New midpoint: $\left(x_{k+1} + 1,\; y_{k+1} - \tfrac{1}{2}\right) = \left(x_k + 2,\; y_k - \tfrac{3}{2}\right)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} &= \left(x_k + 2\right)^2 + \left(y_k - \tfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 - r^2 \\
p_k &= \left(x_k + 1\right)^2 + \left(y_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 - r^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} - p_k &= \left[(x_k+2)^2 - (x_k+1)^2\right] + \left[(y_k - \tfrac{3}{2})^2 - (y_k - \tfrac{1}{2})^2\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expand the $x$-bracket:**

$$
(x_k+2)^2 - (x_k+1)^2 = 2x_k + 3
$$

**Expand the $y$-bracket:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(y_k - \tfrac{3}{2})^2 - (y_k - \tfrac{1}{2})^2 &= (y_k^2 - 3y_k + \tfrac{9}{4}) - (y_k^2 - y_k + \tfrac{1}{4}) \\
&= -2y_k + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Combine:**

$$
p_{k+1} - p_k = (2x_k + 3) + (-2y_k + 2) = 2x_k - 2y_k + 5
$$

Substituting $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$ and $y_{k+1} = y_k - 1$:

$$
2x_k - 2y_k + 5 = 2(x_{k+1} - 1) - 2(y_{k+1} + 1) + 5 = 2x_{k+1} - 2y_{k+1} + 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\,p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\,x_{k+1} - 2\,y_{k+1} + 1\,}
$$

## 3.4 📊 Worked Example — $r = 10$, Center $(0, 0)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Initialisation:** $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 10$, $p_0 = 1 - r = 1 - 10 = -9$.

### Iteration Trace Table

| k | $x_k$ | $y_k$ | $p_k$ | Test | Decision | $p_{k+1}$ formula used | $x_{k+1}$ | $y_{k+1}$ | Symmetry points (8-way) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | 0 | 10 | −9 | $< 0$ | E | $p_0 + 2x_{k+1} + 1$ | 1 | 10 | $(0,10)$ |
| 1 | 1 | 10 | −6 | $< 0$ | E | $p_1 + 2x_{k+1} + 1$ | 2 | 10 | $(1,10)$ |
| 2 | 2 | 10 | −1 | $< 0$ | E | $p_2 + 2x_{k+1} + 1$ | 3 | 10 | $(2,10)$ |
| 3 | 3 | 10 | +6 | $\geq 0$ | SE | $p_3 + 2x_{k+1} - 2y_{k+1} + 1$ | 4 | 9 | $(3,10)$ |
| 4 | 4 | 9 | −5 | $< 0$ | E | $p_4 + 2x_{k+1} + 1$ | 5 | 9 | $(4,9)$ |
| 5 | 5 | 9 | +6 | $\geq 0$ | SE | $p_5 + 2x_{k+1} - 2y_{k+1} + 1$ | 6 | 8 | $(5,9)$ |
| 6 | 6 | 8 | +1 | $\geq 0$ | SE | $p_6 + 2x_{k+1} - 2y_{k+1} + 1$ | 7 | 7 | $(6,8)$ |
| 7 | 7 | 7 | +3 | — | **STOP** ($x \geq y$) | — | — | — | $(7,7)$ |

### Step-by-Step Numerical Verification (Sample Steps)

**Step 0 → 1:** $p_0 = -9 < 0$, so choose E. $p_1 = p_0 + 2 x_{1} + 1 = -9 + 2(1) + 1 = -6$. ✓

**Step 1 → 2:** $p_1 = -6 < 0$, choose E. $p_2 = -6 + 2(2) + 1 = -1$. ✓

**Step 2 → 3:** $p_2 = -1 < 0$, choose E. $p_3 = -1 + 2(3) + 1 = 6$. ✓

**Step 3 → 4:** $p_3 = 6 \geq 0$, choose SE. $p_4 = 6 + 2(4) - 2(10) + 1 = 6 + 8 - 20 + 1 = -5$. ✓

**Step 4 → 5:** $p_4 = -5 < 0$, choose E. $p_5 = -5 + 2(5) + 1 = 6$. ✓

**Step 5 → 6:** $p_5 = 6 \geq 0$, choose SE. $p_6 = 6 + 2(6) - 2(9) + 1 = 6 + 12 - 18 + 1 = 1$. ✓

**Step 6 → 7:** $p_6 = 1 \geq 0$, choose SE. $p_7 = 1 + 2(7) - 2(8) + 1 = 1 + 14 - 16 + 1 = 0$. Wait — recalculation shows $p_7 = 0$. The next pixel would be $(8, 6)$, but $x=8 > y=6$, so we terminate. ✓

### Final Pixel Set (8-Way Symmetric for $r = 10$)

> **Base octant pixels:** $(0,10), (1,10), (2,10), (3,10), (4,9), (5,9), (6,8), (7,7)$

Multiplying by the 8-way symmetry mapping yields **56 pixels** (or **64 if axis points are counted**), one per grid location along the circle boundary.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Read center xc, yc and radius r/]
    B --> C[x := 0]
    C --> D[y := r]
    D --> E[p := 1 - r]
    E --> F[/Plot eight symmetric points around xc, yc/]
    F --> G{x less than y?}
    G -- No --> H([Stop])
    G -- Yes --> I{p less than 0?}
    I -- Yes --> J[x := x + 1]
    J --> K[p := p + 2 times x_new + 1]
    K --> F
    I -- No --> L[x := x + 1]
    L --> M[y := y - 1]
    M --> N[p := p + 2 times x_new minus 2 times y_new + 1]
    N --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `F` block is a **re-plot** step (the *plotSymmetric* subroutine) and the `G` test is the **loop terminator**. In a Mermaid flowchart, a label may not contain markdown; therefore all node IDs are alphanumeric and all text inside double quotes is plain uppercase (or mixed-case without formatting tags).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The algorithm's hardware/software architecture can be viewed as a **pipeline** of three functional blocks:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[/Inputs: xc, yc, r/] --> UNIT1
    UNIT1["UNIT 1 INITIALISER\nSet x to 0\nSet y to r\nSet p to 1 minus r"] --> UNIT2
    UNIT2["UNIT 2 DECISION CORE\nIf p less than 0 then East step\nElse South East step\nUpdate p and x and y"] --> UNIT3
    UNIT3["UNIT 3 SYMMETRY EXPANDER\nPlot 8 mirrored pixels\nLoop while x less than y"] --> OUTPUT[/Final raster pixels/]
    UNIT3 -.Loopback.-> UNIT2
```

### Block Descriptions

| Block | Role | Key Operations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unit 1 – Initializer** | Seed registers | $x := 0$, $y := r$, $p := 1 - r$ |
| **Unit 2 – Decision Core** | Compute next pixel | Comparison + 2 additions |
| **Unit 3 – Symmetry Expander** | Mirror to 8 octants | 7 plot calls + 1 base plot |

## 4.3 Octant Symmetry Map (8-Way Mirror Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q2["Quadrant II - x negative, y positive"]
        A1["(-y, x)"] --- A2["(-x, y)"]
    end
    subgraph Q1["Quadrant I - x positive, y positive"]
        B1["(x, y)"] --- B2["(y, x)"]
    end
    subgraph Q3["Quadrant III - x negative, y negative"]
        C1["(-x, -y)"] --- C2["(-y, -x)"]
    end
    subgraph Q4["Quadrant IV - x positive, y negative"]
        D1["(y, -x)"] --- D2["(x, -y)"]
    end
    Q2 --- Q1
    Q1 --- Q4
    Q3 --- Q4
    Q2 --- Q3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the implicit equation of a circle used in the Midpoint Circle Algorithm and explain the role of the decision parameter $p_k$.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The implicit circle equation is $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - r^2$. (1 mark)
> The decision parameter $p_k$ is the value of $f(x, y)$ evaluated at the midpoint between the two candidate pixels $(x_k+1, y_k)$ and $(x_k+1, y_k-1)$, i.e., at the point $\left(x_k+1,\; y_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)$. (1 mark)
> Its sign determines which candidate is closer to the true circle: $p_k < 0$ → upper pixel, $p_k \geq 0$ → lower pixel. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is the Midpoint Circle Algorithm preferred over the direct equation $y = \sqrt{r^2 - x^2}$ for raster circle generation?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The direct method requires a **square-root** and **floating-point multiplication** at every step, which is computationally expensive. (1 mark)
> The midpoint method uses only **integer addition**, **comparison**, and **shifts**, making it far faster and suitable for hardware implementation. (1 mark)
> Additionally, the midpoint method exploits **8-way symmetry** to reduce total work by a factor of 8, and produces **uniformly spaced** pixels with consistent brightness. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on Internal Choice (KTU Pattern):** You must answer EITHER the full **Question A** OR the full **Question B**. Both carry equal marks.

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the recurrence relations for the decision parameter $p_k$ in the Midpoint Circle Algorithm for both cases ($p_k < 0$ and $p_k \geq 0$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given radius $r = 8$ and center at origin, plot the raster points generated by the Midpoint Circle Algorithm and tabulate all decision-parameter values. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Define $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - r^2$. The midpoint $M_k$ is $\left(x_k + 1,\; y_k - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)$.

$$
p_k = (x_k+1)^2 + (y_k-\tfrac{1}{2})^2 - r^2 \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Case 1: $p_k < 0$ → choose E pixel → $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$, $y_{k+1} = y_k$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} &= (x_k+2)^2 + (y_k-\tfrac{1}{2})^2 - r^2 \\
p_{k+1} - p_k &= (x_k+2)^2 - (x_k+1)^2 = 2x_k + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks for algebra) Substituting $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$:

$$
\boxed{\,p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\,x_{k+1} + 1\,} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Case 2: $p_k \geq 0$ → choose SE pixel → $x_{k+1} = x_k + 1$, $y_{k+1} = y_k - 1$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{k+1} &= (x_k+2)^2 + (y_k - \tfrac{3}{2})^2 - r^2 \\
p_{k+1} - p_k &= [(x_k+2)^2 - (x_k+1)^2] + [(y_k-\tfrac{3}{2})^2 - (y_k-\tfrac{1}{2})^2] \\
&= (2x_k + 3) + (-2y_k + 2) = 2x_k - 2y_k + 5
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks for algebra) Substituting $x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}$:

$$
\boxed{\,p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\,x_{k+1} - 2\,y_{k+1} + 1\,} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Part (b) — Worked Table for $r = 8$ (7 marks):**

Initialisation: $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 8$, $p_0 = 1 - 8 = -7$. (1 mark)

| k | $x_k$ | $y_k$ | $p_k$ | Test | Action | New $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$ | New $p_{k+1}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | 8 | −7 | $< 0$ | E | (1, 8) | $-7 + 2(1) + 1 = -4$ |
| 1 | 1 | 8 | −4 | $< 0$ | E | (2, 8) | $-4 + 2(2) + 1 = 1$ |
| 2 | 2 | 8 | +1 | $\geq 0$ | SE | (3, 7) | $1 + 2(3) - 2(8) + 1 = -8$ |
| 3 | 3 | 7 | −8 | $< 0$ | E | (4, 7) | $-8 + 2(4) + 1 = 1$ |
| 4 | 4 | 7 | +1 | $\geq 0$ | SE | (5, 6) | $1 + 2(5) - 2(7) + 1 = -2$ |
| 5 | 5 | 6 | −2 | $< 0$ | E | (6, 6) | $-2 + 2(6) + 1 = 11$ |
| 6 | 6 | 6 | — | — | **STOP** ($x = y$) | — | — |

(5 marks for the table and verification) The base octant points are:

$$
(0,8),\; (1,8),\; (2,8),\; (3,7),\; (4,7),\; (5,6)
$$

(1 mark for correctly identifying the stop condition $x \geq y$ at $k=5$ → termination after plotting $(5, 6)$).

#### Common Pitfalls (Examiner's Warning)

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this question type:**
> 1. **Wrong initial $p_0$**: Writing $p_0 = \tfrac{5}{4} - r$ and then not adjusting for integer form → **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing which pixel is "East" vs "SE"**: Some texts label differently. Always state explicitly: $E = (x_k+1, y_k)$, $SE = (x_k+1, y_k-1)$.
> 3. **Off-by-one in termination**: Stopping at $x = y$ AFTER plotting is correct; stopping BEFORE plotting $(x, y)$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting 8-way symmetry**: Listing only octant points without mentioning the symmetry plot → −2 marks.
> 5. **Sign of $p_0$ for $r = 1$**: $p_0 = 0$; both Case 1 and Case 2 are valid — students often pick one and lose 1 mark for ambiguity.

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **8-way symmetry** in circle rasterization. Show all symmetric mappings for a base point $(x, y)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Midpoint Circle Algorithm, generate all raster points for a circle of radius $r = 6$ centered at the origin. Show full iteration table. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 8-Way Symmetry (7 marks):**

A circle centered at the origin exhibits perfect reflective symmetry about **both coordinate axes** and the **diagonal lines $y = \pm x$**. (1 mark)

These 4 reflection lines divide the plane into **8 octants**, each containing a $45°$ sector. (1 mark)

If a pixel $(x, y)$ lies on the circle, then 7 other points are also on the circle by reflection: (2 marks for listing)

| Reflection axis | Mirrored point |
| :--- | :--- |
| $x \to -x$ | $(-x, y)$ |
| $y \to -y$ | $(x, -y)$ |
| $x \to -x,\; y \to -y$ | $(-x, -y)$ |
| $x \leftrightarrow y$ | $(y, x)$ |
| $x \to -x,\; x \leftrightarrow y$ | $(-y, x)$ |
| $y \to -y,\; x \leftrightarrow y$ | $(y, -x)$ |
| $x \to -x,\; y \to -y,\; x \leftrightarrow y$ | $(-y, -x)$ |

(1 mark for grouping) Computing one octant (the second octant: $0 \leq x \leq y$) thus generates the entire circle boundary. (1 mark)

This reduces the algorithm's time complexity from $O(2\pi r)$ to $O(r/2)$ — a speedup of nearly **4π ≈ 12.57×** compared to brute force. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Worked Table for $r = 6$ (7 marks):**

Initialisation: $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 6$, $p_0 = 1 - 6 = -5$. (1 mark)

| k | $x_k$ | $y_k$ | $p_k$ | Test | Action | $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$ | New $p_{k+1}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | 6 | −5 | $< 0$ | E | (1, 6) | $-5 + 2(1) + 1 = -2$ |
| 1 | 1 | 6 | −2 | $< 0$ | E | (2, 6) | $-2 + 2(2) + 1 = 3$ |
| 2 | 2 | 6 | +3 | $\geq 0$ | SE | (3, 5) | $3 + 2(3) - 2(6) + 1 = -2$ |
| 3 | 3 | 5 | −2 | $< 0$ | E | (4, 5) | $-2 + 2(4) + 1 = 7$ |
| 4 | 4 | 5 | +7 | $\geq 0$ | SE | (5, 4) | $7 + 2(5) - 2(5) + 1 = 8$ |
| 5 | 5 | 4 | — | — | **STOP** ($x > y$) | — | — |

(5 marks for the trace) **Generated base-octant pixels:** $(0, 6), (1, 6), (2, 6), (3, 5), (4, 5)$. (1 mark)

**Verification of the boundary at $r = 6$:** distance from origin to $(0, 6) = 6$ ✓; to $(3, 5) = \sqrt{9+25} = \sqrt{34} \approx 5.83$ ✓; to $(5, 4) = \sqrt{25+16} = \sqrt{41} \approx 6.40$ — both within half-pixel tolerance. (1 mark for geometric sanity check)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common errors specific to this question variant:**
> 1. Applying the 8-way symmetry to a **non-origin** center without first shifting to origin → −2 marks.
> 2. Computing $p_{k+1}$ using the **old** $x_k$ instead of the **new** $x_{k+1}$ → entire table wrong.
> 3. Missing the column showing the **decision test result** ($<$ or $\geq$) → −1 mark for incomplete trace.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Core idea:** Test the **midpoint** between two candidate pixels using the implicit circle function $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 - r^2$.
- ✅ **Initial decision parameter:** $\;p_0 = 1 - r\;$ (integer form, KTU standard).
- ✅ **Update Rule A (East step, $p_k < 0$):** $\;p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\,x_{k+1} + 1$.
- ✅ **Update Rule B (South-East step, $p_k \geq 0$):** $\;p_{k+1} = p_k + 2\,x_{k+1} - 2\,y_{k+1} + 1$.
- ✅ **Termination condition:** Stop AFTER plotting when $\;x \geq y$.
- ✅ **8-way symmetry** gives 8 pixels per computed point; reflections are across $x$-axis, $y$-axis, and lines $y = \pm x$.
