# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse-Slope Recurrence

For a straight edge with endpoints $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ where $y_2 > y_1$:

$$
\text{slope} \;=\; m \;=\; \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{1}{m} \;=\; \frac{x_2 - x_1}{y_2 - y_1} \;\equiv\; \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}
$$

At scan line $y_1$, the intersection is at $(x_1, y_1)$. At the next scan line $y_1 + 1$, the intersection x is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2' &= x_1 + (y - y_1) \cdot \frac{1}{m} \\
\Rightarrow\quad x(y+1) &= x_1 + (y + 1 - y_1) \cdot \frac{1}{m} \\
&= \bigl[\,x_1 + (y - y_1)\tfrac{1}{m}\,\bigr] + \tfrac{1}{m} \\
&= x(y) + \tfrac{1}{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields the elegant **recurrence**:

$$
\boxed{\,x_{k+1} \;=\; x_k + \dfrac{1}{m}\,}
$$

which is precisely why the algorithm is fast — it avoids recomputing line equations from scratch at every pixel.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Complete Trace (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### Polygon Definition

Consider a polygon with the following vertex list (given in counter-clockwise order, KTU standard convention):

| Vertex | $x$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 2 |
| B | 8 | 2 |
| C | 10 | 5 |
| D | 8 | 8 |
| E | 2 | 8 |
| F | 4 | 5 |

### Step 1 — Identify Edges & Compute Per-Edge Data

| Edge | $(x_1, y_1) \to (x_2, y_2)$ | $y_{min}$ | $y_{max}$ | $x$ at $y_{min}$ | $\dfrac{1}{m} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta y}$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AB | (2,2) → (8,2) | 2 | 2 | — | — | **Horizontal → ignore** |
| BC | (8,2) → (10,5) | 2 | 5 | 8 | $\dfrac{10-8}{5-2} = \dfrac{2}{3}$ | Active |
| CD | (10,5) → (8,8) | 5 | 8 | 10 | $\dfrac{8-10}{8-5} = -\dfrac{2}{3}$ | Active |
| DE | (8,8) → (2,8) | 8 | 8 | — | — | **Horizontal → ignore** |
| EF | (2,8) → (4,5) | 5 | 8 | 2 | $\dfrac{4-2}{5-8} = -\dfrac{2}{3}$ | Active |
| FA | (4,5) → (2,2) | 2 | 5 | 2 | $\dfrac{2-4}{2-5} = \dfrac{2}{3}$ | Active |

### Step 2 — Build the Sorted Edge Table (SET)

The ET is a **bucket array** indexed by y_min. Each entry stores edges with that y_min in increasing x order.

| y-bucket | Edge entries $(y_{max},\; x_{y_{min}},\; 1/m)$ |
|---|---|
| 2 | (5, 8, 2/3), (5, 2, 2/3) |
| 3 | — |
| 4 | — |
| 5 | (8, 10, −2/3), (8, 2, −2/3) |
| 6 | — |
| 7 | — |

### Step 3 — Active Edge List (AEL) Trace, Scan Line by Scan Line

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The AEL is shown **after** insertion of new edges, **after** removal of expired edges, **after** x-update, and **after** sorting by x. The shaded spans are the pixels filled at that scan line.

#### Scan line y = 2

- AEL before: [ ]
- Add ET[2]: (5, 8, 2/3) and (5, 2, 2/3)
- AEL (sorted by x): (5, **2**, 2/3), (5, **8**, 2/3)
- **Fill span: x ∈ [2, 8]**

#### Scan line y = 3

- No addition, no removal
- Update x: 2 + 2/3 = 2.67; 8 + 2/3 = 8.67
- AEL: (5, **2.67**, 2/3), (5, **8.67**, 2/3)
- **Fill span: x ∈ [2.67, 8.67]**

#### Scan line y = 4

- No addition, no removal
- Update x: 2.67 + 2/3 = 3.33; 8.67 + 2/3 = 9.33
- AEL: (5, **3.33**, 2/3), (5, **9.33**, 2/3)
- **Fill span: x ∈ [3.33, 9.33]**

#### Scan line y = 5

- **Remove** both edges (y_max = 5 reached).
- **Add** ET[5]: (8, 10, −2/3) and (8, 2, −2/3)
- AEL (sorted): (8, **2**, −2/3), (8, **10**, −2/3)
- **Fill span: x ∈ [2, 10]**

#### Scan line y = 6

- No addition, no removal
- Update x: 2 − 2/3 = 1.33; 10 − 2/3 = 9.33
- AEL: (8, **1.33**, −2/3), (8, **9.33**, −2/3)
- **Fill span: x ∈ [1.33, 9.33]**

#### Scan line y = 7

- No addition, no removal
- Update x: 1.33 − 2/3 = 0.67; 9.33 − 2/3 = 8.67
- AEL: (8, **0.67**, −2/3), (8, **8.67**, −2/3)
- **Fill span: x ∈ [0.67, 8.67]**

#### Scan line y = 8

- **Remove** both edges (y_max = 8 reached). AEL becomes [ ].
- ET is empty → **Algorithm terminates.**

### Step 4 — Visualisation of Filled Spans

```
y = 8  |--------- span [0.67, 8.67] ---------|         (D, E at top — but y=8 not drawn)
y = 7  |  span [0.67, 8.67]  |
y = 6  | span [1.33, 9.33]   |
y = 5  |------ span [2, 10] ------|                  (C, F — narrowest? widest waist)
y = 4  |  span [3.33, 9.33]   |
y = 3  |  span [2.67, 8.67]   |
y = 2  |----- span [2, 8] -----|                     (A, B at bottom)
```

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Quick Reference for Solvers)

| Situation Encountered | Correct Action | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| Edge is **horizontal** | **Drop it** from the ET entirely. | Counting it gives infinite intersections. |
| Edge is **vertical** | $1/m = 0$ is invalid. Special-case: $x$ stays constant. | Dividing by 0 in code. |
| Scan line touches a **local min vertex** | Count the edge intersection as **1** (parity flips). | Counting both edges separately (gives wrong parity). |
| Scan line touches a **pass-through vertex** | Count as **2** (parity preserved). | Counting as 1 (parity flips incorrectly). |
| Two edges meet at scan line with **same x** | Sort by **larger 1/m first** to pick the correct active edge. | Random sort → wrong pairing. |
| AEL becomes empty **mid-polygon** | Algorithm may exit prematurely — verify ET is fully drained. | Missing edges in deeper y-buckets. |
| **Spans overlap** in non-simple polygons | Algorithm naturally handles even-odd fill rule. | Manually resolving overlaps. |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start]) --> A1[Read polygon vertex list]
    A1 --> A2[Discard horizontal edges]
    A2 --> A3[For each remaining edge compute y_min, y_max, x_at_ymin, 1/m]
    A3 --> A4[Build Sorted Edge Table SET bucket by y_min]
    A4 --> A5[Initialise Active Edge List AEL to empty]
    A5 --> A6[Set y = smallest y_min in SET]
    A6 --> A7{AEL or SET non empty?}
    A7 -->|No| A99([Stop])
    A7 -->|Yes| B1[Add all edges from SET y to AEL]
    B1 --> B2[Remove edges with y_max = y from AEL]
    B2 --> B3[Sort AEL by current x ascending]
    B3 --> B4[Pair consecutive AEL entries and fill spans]
    B4 --> B5[For every edge in AEL update x = x + 1/m]
    B5 --> B6[Increment y = y + 1]
    B6 --> A7
```

## 4.2 Edge Table Construction — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Input Layer]
        P[Polygon Vertex List V1 V2 ... Vn]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Processing Layer]
        E1[Edge Segment Extractor]
        E2[Slope and Inverse Slope Calculator 1 by m]
        E3[Horizontal Edge Filter]
        E4[Bucket Sorter by y_min]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        T0[SET y is 0]
        T1[SET y is 1]
        T2[SET y is 2]
        Tdots[...]
        TN[SET y is n]
    end
    P --> E1 --> E3
    E3 -->|non-horizontal| E2 --> E4
    E4 --> T0
    E4 --> T1
    E4 --> T2
    E4 --> Tdots
    E4 --> TN
    E3 -->|horizontal| DROP[Discarded]
```

## 4.3 Active Edge List State Machine (Per Scan Line)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EmptyAEL
    EmptyAEL --> AddingEdges: y reaches new bucket
    AddingEdges --> RemovingExpired: filter y_max = y
    RemovingExpired --> SortingByX: ascending x order
    SortingByX --> FillingSpans: pair edges and shade
    FillingSpans --> UpdatingX: x = x + 1/m
    UpdatingX --> IncrementingY: y = y + 1
    IncrementingY --> AddingEdges: if SET has new bucket
    IncrementingY --> EmptyAEL: if SET drained and AEL drained
    EmptyAEL --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Memory-Mapped Pipeline (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU_SIDE[CPU Preprocessing]
        VLIST[Vertex Buffer]
        EBUILD[Edge Table Builder]
        SLOPECALC[Inverse Slope Calculator]
    end
    subgraph DATASTRUCT[Core Data Structures]
        ETMEM[(Sorted Edge Table SET)]
        AELMEM[(Active Edge List AEL)]
    end
    subgraph PERSCAN[Per Scan Line Pipeline]
        INS[Edge Inserter]
        REM[Edge Remover y_max]
        SORT[Bucket Sorter by x]
        FILL[Span Filler - Pixel Painter]
        XUPD[X Updater x plus 1 over m]
    end
    subgraph FRAMEBUF[Frame Buffer]
        FB[Pixel Array on Display]
    end
    VLIST --> EBUILD
    EBUILD --> SLOPECALC
    SLOPECALC --> ETMEM
    ETMEM --> INS
    AELMEM --> INS
    INS --> REM
    REM --> SORT
    SORT --> FILL
    FILL --> XUPD
    XUPD --> AELMEM
    FILL --> FB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams depict the **functional flow** of the algorithm. In raster hardware, the *Span Filler* and *X Updater* blocks are typically pipelined to operate in lockstep with the CRT/LED/LCD horizontal sweep.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, May 2024]**

**Q1. Define the Scan-Line Polygon Fill algorithm. Mention the role of the Edge Table (ET) and Active Edge List (AEL).**
*(Module Outcome: CO2 — Apply rasterisation algorithms.)*

**Model Answer:**

The Scan-Line Polygon Fill algorithm fills a polygon by sweeping a horizontal scan line from the top of the polygon to the bottom. For every scan line y, it finds the intersection x-coordinates with all polygon edges. Pixels between consecutive pairs of intersections are shaded.

- **Edge Table (ET)**: A static, bucket-sorted list of all non-horizontal edges, indexed by their **y_min**. It holds $(y_{max}, x_{y_{min}}, 1/m)$ for each edge.
- **Active Edge List (AEL)**: A dynamic list of those edges that are **currently intersected** by the active scan line. At each step the AEL is sorted by x, pairs of entries define fill spans, and x is incremented by $1/m$ for the next scan line.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Dec 2023]**

**Q2. State the Odd–Even (Parity) Rule used in the scan-line fill algorithm. Why are horizontal edges ignored?**
*(Module Outcome: CO2 — Understand fill rules.)*

**Model Answer:**

The Odd–Even Rule (Parity Rule) states: a point $(x, y)$ is **inside** the polygon if a horizontal ray cast to the right from $(x, y)$ crosses the polygon boundary an **odd** number of times; otherwise it is **outside**.

Horizontal edges are ignored because the scan line lies exactly **on** such an edge, producing an **infinite number of intersections** (theoretically), which would corrupt the parity count. By removing them and treating adjacent non-horizontal edges at the shared vertex, a correct finite parity is obtained.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Algorithm Trace

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, ESE Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Scan-Line Polygon Fill algorithm. Define Edge Table and Active Edge List, and describe how the **inverse slope $1/m$** is used to update the intersection x-coordinate from one scan line to the next.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply the Scan-Line Polygon Fill algorithm to fill the polygon with vertices:

$$
A(2,2),\; B(8,2),\; C(10,5),\; D(8,8),\; E(2,8),\; F(4,5)
$$

Construct the Edge Table and trace the Active Edge List for every scan line from $y = 2$ to $y = 7$. List the x-coordinate spans filled at each scan line.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Edge Table Construction.** For each non-horizontal edge, compute:
- $y_{min} = \min(y_1, y_2)$
- $y_{max} = \max(y_1, y_2)$
- $x_{y_{min}} = x$ at the lower endpoint
- $1/m = \Delta x / \Delta y$

Edges are then **bucket-sorted** into the Edge Table at the row indexed by $y_{min}$.

**Step 2 — Active Edge List Updates.** At every scan line $y$ the AEL undergoes three operations:

1. **Insertion** — All edges with $y_{min} = y$ (taken from ET[y]) are added to the AEL.
2. **Deletion** — Edges whose $y_{max} = y$ are removed.
3. **Sort** — The AEL is sorted in **ascending x** (ties broken by larger $1/m$ first).

**Step 3 — Inverse Slope Recurrence.** For a straight line $y = m x + c$:

$$
x \;=\; \frac{y - c}{m} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{dx}{dy} \;=\; \frac{1}{m}
$$

So if at scan line $y$ the intersection is at $x_k$, at scan line $y+1$ it is at:

$$
\boxed{\,x_{k+1} \;=\; x_k + \dfrac{1}{m}\,}
$$

This is the **coherence property** that makes scan-line filling linear in the polygon area rather than quadratic.

**Step 4 — Filling.** Pairs of consecutive AEL entries $(x_{2i-1}, x_{2i})$ define fill spans; all pixels in these spans are shaded.

#### Model Solution to (b)

The complete derivation is shown in **Section 3.2 of these notes** (Worked Example). The result:

| Scan Line y | AEL entries (x values) | Fill Span |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 2.00, 8.00 | [2, 8] |
| 3 | 2.67, 8.67 | [2.67, 8.67] |
| 4 | 3.33, 9.33 | [3.33, 9.33] |
| 5 | 2.00, 10.00 | [2, 10] |
| 6 | 1.33, 9.33 | [1.33, 9.33] |
| 7 | 0.67, 8.67 | [0.67, 8.67] |

At $y = 8$ the algorithm terminates since the AEL and ET are both empty.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Conceptual + Trace)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **coherence properties** exploited by the scan-line polygon fill algorithm. How do they contribute to computational efficiency compared to a naïve per-pixel inside/outside test?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Trace the scan-line algorithm for the polygon with vertices:

$$
P_1(1,1),\; P_2(7,1),\; P_3(9,4),\; P_4(7,7),\; P_5(3,7),\; P_6(1,4)
$$

#### Model Solution to (a)

Three coherence properties are exploited:

1. **Scan-Line Coherence** — The fill pattern of scan line $y$ is highly similar to that of scan line $y+1$. Most edge intersections shift only slightly. This avoids recomputing the entire parity test per pixel.

2. **Edge Coherence** — The x-intersection of an edge with successive scan lines changes by a constant amount $1/m$ (the inverse slope). Therefore:

$$
x(y+1) \;=\; x(y) + \dfrac{1}{m}
$$

This is a single floating-point add per edge per scan line — extremely fast.

3. **Span Coherence** — Once the entry and exit x-coordinates of a scan line are known, the entire span between them is filled without further testing.

**Efficiency comparison**: A naïve inside/outside test for a polygon of $n$ edges and an image of $N$ pixels is $O(nN)$. The scan-line algorithm is $O(n + N)$ — for typical polygons this is **orders of magnitude** faster, which is why all real-world rasterisers use it.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Edges & per-edge data:**

| Edge | $y_{min}$ | $y_{max}$ | $x$ at $y_{min}$ | $1/m$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1P_2$ | 1 | 1 | — | — (horizontal, ignore) |
| $P_2P_3$ | 1 | 4 | 7 | $(9-7)/(4-1) = 2/3$ |
| $P_3P_4$ | 4 | 7 | 9 | $(7-9)/(7-4) = -2/3$ |
| $P_4P_5$ | 7 | 7 | — | — (horizontal, ignore) |
| $P_5P_6$ | 4 | 7 | 3 | $(1-3)/(4-7) = 2/3$ |
| $P_6P_1$ | 1 | 4 | 1 | $(1-1)/(1-4) = 0$ |

**Edge Table:**

| y | Entries |
|---|---|
| 1 | (4, 7, 2/3), (4, 1, 0) |
| 4 | (7, 9, −2/3), (7, 3, 2/3) |

**AEL Trace:**

| y | AEL (sorted by x) | Fill Span |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (4, 1, 0), (4, 7, 2/3) | [1, 7] |
| 2 | (4, 1+0, 0), (4, 7+2/3, 2/3) = (4, 1, 0), (4, 7.67, 2/3) | [1, 7.67] |
| 3 | (4, 1, 0), (4, 8.33, 2/3) | [1, 8.33] |
| 4 | Remove (y_max=4). Add ET[4]: (7,9,−2/3), (7,3,2/3). Sort: (7, 3, 2/3), (7, 9, −2/3) | [3, 9] |
| 5 | Update: (7, 3+2/3, 2/3), (7, 9−2/3, −2/3) = (7, 3.67, 2/3), (7, 8.33, −2/3) | [3.67, 8.33] |
| 6 | Update: (7, 4.33, 2/3), (7, 7.67, −2/3) | [4.33, 7.67] |
| 7 | Remove (y_max=7). AEL empty, ET empty. **Stop.** | — |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop horizontal edges** → costs 1–2 marks per sub-question. Always state explicitly which edges are horizontal and excluded.
> 2. **Not showing x-update using 1/m** → you must show the numerical increment of x for every active edge on every scan line. Skipping this costs up to **3 marks**.
> 3. **Failing to sort the AEL by x** → wrong fill spans. The AEL must be sorted **at every scan line** before pairing.
> 4. **Including edges with $y_{min} = y_{max}$ in the AEL** → these are removed in the deletion step; do not process them.
> 5. **Not pairing AEL entries in (left, right) pairs** → if the polygon has an odd number of active edges at a scan line, there is a definition error (or self-intersecting polygon) — KTU expects the student to **state this observation**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Scan-Line Polygon Fill**

- ✅ The algorithm is driven by **horizontal scan lines** sweeping top-to-bottom across the polygon.
- ✅ The **Edge Table (ET)** is a **bucket array indexed by y_min**; built once.
- ✅ The **Active Edge List (AEL)** is updated at every scan line via **insert → delete → sort → fill → x-update**.
- ✅ **Horizontal edges are dropped**; **vertical edges have $1/m = 0$** (or are special-cased).
- ✅ The **inverse slope $1/m = \Delta x / \Delta y$** is the workhorse — it lets x advance by one cheap addition per scan line.
- ✅ Each AEL entry stores the tuple $\bigl(y_{max},\; x_{current},\; 1/m\bigr)$.
- ✅ **Sort the AEL by current x at every scan line**; pair consecutive entries to get fill spans.
- ✅ **Remove** AEL entries when $y = y_{max}$ (the bottom of the edge has been reached).
- ✅ **Odd–Even / Parity Rule** governs the inside/outside test — count rightward crossings modulo 2.
- ✅ **Coherence properties** (scan-line, edge, span) reduce complexity from $O(nN)$ to $O(n + N)$.
- ✅ Real-world applications: **GPU rasterisation, GIS maps, PCB layout, CAD fill, medical imaging**.
- ✅ Typical KTU 14-marker structure: **(a) 7 marks theory + (b) 7 marks numerical trace**; show ET, AEL, and fill spans for every scan line.
- ✅ Watch the **vertex pairing rule** — at a shared vertex, sort by larger $1/m$ first.
- ✅ End condition: **AEL empty AND ET empty**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor for the Exam Hall** — *ET is the warehouse, AEL is the conveyor belt, the scan line is the quality inspector, and 1/m is the conveyor's stepping motor.* If you keep this picture in mind, the algorithm writes itself.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Filling and Flood Filling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boundary Filling and Flood Filling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Boundary Fill Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The **Boundary Fill Algorithm** is a recursive/iterative area-filling technique used in raster graphics where the algorithm starts from an interior point (called the *seed point*) inside a region and paints the pixel using a *fill color*, recursively moving to neighboring pixels (4-connected or 8-connected) until it encounters the *boundary color*. The process terminates when the boundary is reached, ensuring the interior is completely filled without leaking outside.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Bucket of Paint Inside a Closed Wall"

Imagine you are standing **inside a closed circular brick wall**. You have a bucket of **blue paint** and a brush. You pour the blue paint at your current location (the seed point). The paint spreads to the **floor tiles touching your tile** — front, back, left, right (or even diagonals). You keep pouring blue on every white tile you can reach, but you **stop the moment your brush touches a red brick (boundary)**. The result: every reachable white tile inside the wall becomes blue.

That is *exactly* what boundary fill does in a pixel grid. The brick wall = boundary color, the white floor = interior pixels, the blue paint = fill color, and your starting position = the seed point $(x, y)$.

## 1.2 Flood Fill Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The **Flood Fill Algorithm** is an area-filling algorithm that starts from a seed pixel and replaces the *target color* (the original color of the seed and its connected region) with a new *fill color*. Unlike boundary fill, it does NOT rely on a boundary color. Instead, it continues spreading as long as the neighboring pixels match the original target color. It is most often used in paint-bucket tools in image editors (e.g., MS Paint, Photoshop magic wand).

### Intuitive Analogy — "Flooding a Colored Pond with a New Color"

Imagine a shallow pond of **green water** surrounded by dry land. You stand at a green pixel (a single drop) and release **red dye**. The red dye spreads to every green drop it touches (4 or 8 neighbors) and converts them red. It does **not stop at a wall** — it stops only when there is **no more green water nearby** to convert. This is flood fill: it is "color-replacement by connectivity," not "stop-at-boundary."

## 1.3 The Critical Distinction (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Fill** requires a *closed boundary* of a specific color. **Flood Fill** requires a uniform *target color* to replace. The two algorithms are structurally similar but conceptually different in their stop condition.

| Property | Boundary Fill | Flood Fill |
|---|---|---|
| **Stop Condition** | Pixel color = boundary color | Pixel color ≠ target color |
| **Requires Boundary?** | Yes (closed, uniform) | No |
| **Seed Point Location** | Must be strictly interior | Anywhere in target region |
| **Most Common Use** | Filling polygons, shapes | MS Paint bucket, image editing |
| **Risk** | Boundary leak if not closed | Infinite fill on similar colors |

## 1.4 The Two Connectivity Models

Both algorithms operate using one of two neighbor sets:

- **4-Connected (4-Neighbor):** Considers only the **4 orthogonal neighbors** — North, South, East, West.
  $$\text{Neighbors}_4(x,y) = \{(x+1,y),\ (x-1,y),\ (x,y+1),\ (x,y-1)\}$$

- **8-Connected (8-Neighbor):** Considers the **4 orthogonal + 4 diagonal** neighbors.
  $$\text{Neighbors}_8(x,y) = \text{Neighbors}_4 \cup \{(x+1,y+1),\ (x+1,y-1),\ (x-1,y+1),\ (x-1,y-1)\}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Boundary Fill — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The algorithm works on a **stack-based** (iterative) or **recursive** model. The recursive model is the one KTU typically tests.

1. **Initialize** the seed point $(x, y)$ and the fill color $C_{fill}$ and the boundary color $C_{boundary}$.
2. **Test** the current pixel:
   - If $\text{getPixel}(x, y) = C_{boundary}$ → **STOP** (return).
   - If $\text{getPixel}(x, y) = C_{fill}$ → **STOP** (already filled, avoid cycles).
   - Otherwise → set the pixel to $C_{fill}$.
3. **Recurse** on each valid neighbor (4 or 8).
4. **Base case:** the recursion unwinds when no more valid neighbors exist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack Overflow Risk:** The recursive boundary fill can cause **stack overflow** for very large regions because each recursive call consumes one stack frame. The iterative version using an explicit stack is preferred in production systems.

## 2.2 Flood Fill — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Initialize** the seed point $(x, y)$, the target color $C_{old}$ (read from the seed), and the new fill color $C_{new}$.
2. **Test** the current pixel:
   - If $\text{getPixel}(x, y) \neq C_{old}$ → **STOP** (no more region to fill).
   - If $\text{getPixel}(x, y) = C_{new}$ → **STOP** (avoid infinite loops).
   - Otherwise → set the pixel to $C_{new}$.
3. **Recurse** on each valid neighbor.
4. **Termination** when the entire connected component of $C_{old}$ pixels is exhausted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Flood fill is *boundary-free*. It can leak across image regions if the target color appears outside the intended area. The 4-connected version is more conservative; the 8-connected version is faster but can cross thin 1-pixel gaps (the "leaking" problem).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes every essential formula, condition, and complexity metric required for KTU 2024 examinations on this topic.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **4-Neighbor Set** | $N_4 = \{(x\pm1, y),\ (x, y\pm1)\}$ | Cardinal direction pixels |
| **8-Neighbor Set** | $N_8 = N_4 \cup \{(x\pm1, y\pm1)\}$ | Cardinal + diagonal pixels |
| **Boundary Stop** | $\text{getPixel}(p) = C_{boundary}$ | $p$ = current pixel, $C$ = color |
| **Flood Stop** | $\text{getPixel}(p) \neq C_{old}$ | Mismatch → terminate |
| **Already-Filled Guard** | $\text{getPixel}(p) = C_{fill}$ | Prevents infinite recursion |
| **Recursive Depth (worst case)** | $D_{max} = m \times n$ | $m, n$ = image dimensions |
| **Time Complexity** | $T(n) = O(N)$ | $N$ = pixels in region |
| **Space Complexity (recursive)** | $S(n) = O(D_{max}) = O(m \cdot n)$ | Stack frames |
| **Space Complexity (iterative)** | $S(n) = O(D_{max})$ | Explicit stack memory |
| **Diagonal Distance (Chebyshev)** | $d = \max(\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert, \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert)$ | Used in 8-connected traversal |
| **Cardinal Distance (Manhattan)** | $d = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$ | Used in 4-connected traversal |

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **CAD/CAM Systems:** Boundary fill is the standard for filling closed vector shapes on raster displays.
- **Medical Imaging:** Flood fill is used for **segmenting tumors or organs** in MRI/CT scans by replacing a target tissue color.
- **Game Development:** Map editors (e.g., Warcraft III, Age of Empires) use flood fill to detect connected terrain regions (continent detection).
- **GIS (Geographic Information Systems):** Flood fill identifies connected flood zones, lake boundaries, and landmass clustering.
- **PCB Design:** Boundary fill applies solder masks to closed copper regions on printed circuit boards.

## 2.5 Failure Modes & Diagnostic Comparison

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls to Identify in Exam Answers:**

- **Boundary fill on an unclosed boundary** → fills leak to the entire screen.
- **Flood fill on a noisy image** → "speckle" effect because isolated same-colored pixels each become separate regions.
- **4-connected vs 8-connected inconsistency** → if boundary is drawn with 1-pixel diagonals, 4-connected flood fill will leak through the diagonal gap, but 8-connected will not. The reverse is also true for thin diagonal lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Number of Pixels Visited

Let a region $R$ contain $N$ pixels. Each pixel is processed **at most once** because once painted with $C_{fill}$ (boundary fill) or $C_{new}$ (flood fill), it is no longer eligible for repainting due to the **already-filled guard**. Hence, the total work is bounded by the number of pixels in the region.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Work}_{\text{fill}} &= \sum_{p \in R} \big[ \text{getPixel}(p) \neq C_{stop} \big] + \sum_{p \in R} \big[ \text{setPixel}(p,\ C_{new}) \big] \\
&= N \cdot O(1) + N \cdot O(1) \\
&= 2N \cdot O(1) \\
&= O(N)
\end{aligned}
$$

The summation runs over **every pixel $p$ in the connected region $R$**. Each pixel is read once and written once, giving the linear time bound.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Recursion Depth for a Rectangular Region

Consider a rectangle of dimensions $m \times n$ pixels. The deepest possible recursion stack occurs when the fill travels along a single column, processing one row at a time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{max} &= m \quad \text{(for a single column of height } m\text{)} \\
D_{max} &\leq m \cdot n \quad \text{(absolute worst case, the entire region)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, a depth-first recursion will visit pixels in an L-shaped order, so the **typical depth** is closer to $\max(m, n)$ for rectangular regions.

## 3.3 Derivation: 4-Connected vs 8-Connected Coverage

For a region with $N$ pixels:
- **4-connected traversal** may fail to reach some pixels if the region has diagonal-only connectivity (e.g., a checkerboard pattern of two different colors). In that case, **two 4-connected regions exist** where one 8-connected region would be perceived.
- **8-connected traversal** treats diagonal adjacency as valid, so it captures the visually-coherent region.

The **8-connected closure** of a region $R_4$ satisfies:
$$R_8 = R_4 \cup \{\, p \mid p \text{ is diagonal neighbor of any } q \in R_4 \text{ and } p \notin R_4 \,\}$$

For a checkerboard of side $s$:
$$N_{R_4} = 2 \cdot \left(\frac{s}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{s^2}{2}, \quad N_{R_8} = s^2$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Boundary Fill on a 6×6 Grid

**Setup:** A 6×6 grid has a closed square boundary from $(1,1)$ to $(4,4)$ drawn in color $C_{boundary}$ (say, RED). All interior pixels are WHITE. Fill color is BLUE. Seed point is $(2, 2)$.

We trace the first few recursive calls (4-connected):

**Step 1:** Call `BoundaryFill(2, 2)`
- getPixel(2, 2) = WHITE ≠ RED and ≠ BLUE → setPixel(2, 2, BLUE) ✓
- Recurse on 4 neighbors.

**Step 2:** Call `BoundaryFill(2, 3)` → WHITE → BLUE ✓ → recurse.
**Step 3:** Call `BoundaryFill(3, 2)` → WHITE → BLUE ✓ → recurse.
**Step 4:** Call `BoundaryFill(1, 2)` → RED → **STOP** (boundary hit).
**Step 5:** Call `BoundaryFill(2, 1)` → RED → **STOP** (boundary hit).

The recursion tree is **balanced**, and the algorithm fills the entire 3×3 interior in $9$ setPixel operations and at most $4 \times 9 = 36$ recursive calls before the boundary is hit on every branch.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Flood Fill on a 5×5 Grid

**Setup:** A 5×5 grid. The central 3×3 region is GREEN. The seed is at $(2, 2)$, and the target color $C_{old}$ = GREEN. New color = YELLOW. There is no boundary color.

**Trace (4-connected):**

1. getPixel(2, 2) = GREEN = $C_{old}$ → setPixel(2, 2, YELLOW) ✓
2. Recurse: (3, 2), (1, 2), (2, 3), (2, 1) — all GREEN → all become YELLOW.
3. Continue until all 9 GREEN pixels are YELLOW.
4. When the recursion tries to enter a non-GREEN pixel, it **stops** — no boundary needed.

**Total operations:** 9 setPixel + 36 getPixel checks. Time = $O(9) = O(1)$ for this fixed region.

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix (Exhaustive)

| Criterion | Boundary Fill (4-conn) | Boundary Fill (8-conn) | Flood Fill (4-conn) | Flood Fill (8-conn) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Boundary required** | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **Stop condition** | Boundary color | Boundary color | Color mismatch | Color mismatch |
| **Diagonal handling** | Ignored | Treated as connected | Ignored | Treated as connected |
| **Leak risk** | Medium | Low | High (thin gaps) | Very High |
| **Speed** | Slowest | Faster | Slow | Fastest |
| **Recursion depth** | High | High | High | High |
| **Use case** | Polygons | Filled shapes | Paint bucket | Magic wand |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Boundary Fill Algorithm (Recursive, 4-Connected)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start BoundaryFill x, y, Fcolor, Bcolor] --> B{getPixel x, y = Bcolor}
    B -- Yes --> Z1[Return STOP at boundary]
    B -- No --> C{getPixel x, y = Fcolor}
    C -- Yes --> Z2[Return STOP already filled]
    C -- No --> D[setPixel x, y with Fcolor]
    D --> E1[Recurse BoundaryFill x plus 1, y]
    D --> E2[Recurse BoundaryFill x minus 1, y]
    D --> E3[Recurse BoundaryFill x, y plus 1]
    D --> E4[Recurse BoundaryFill x, y minus 1]
    E1 --> F{Any active call left}
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F -- No --> Z3[End]
    F -- Yes --> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Flood Fill Algorithm (Recursive, 4-Connected)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start FloodFill x, y, OldColor, NewColor] --> B{getPixel x, y equals OldColor}
    B -- No --> Z1[Return STOP color mismatch]
    B -- Yes --> C[setPixel x, y with NewColor]
    C --> D1[Recurse FloodFill x plus 1, y, OldColor, NewColor]
    C --> D2[Recurse FloodFill x minus 1, y, OldColor, NewColor]
    C --> D3[Recurse FloodFill x, y plus 1, OldColor, NewColor]
    C --> D4[Recurse FloodFill x, y minus 1, OldColor, NewColor]
    D1 --> E{Any active call left}
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E -- No --> Z2[End all region pixels replaced]
    E -- Yes --> B
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Fill Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        ipA[Seed Coordinates x, y]
        ipB[Fill Color Fcolor]
        ipC[Boundary Color Bcolor or Target Color Tcolor]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[CONTROL UNIT]
        ctr1[Mode Selector: Boundary vs Flood]
        ctr2[Connectivity Selector: 4 vs 8]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[PIXEL PROCESSING ENGINE]
        eng1[Frame Buffer Read getPixel]
        eng2[Color Comparator Logic]
        eng3[Frame Buffer Write setPixel]
    end

    subgraph MEMORY[MEMORY SUBSYSTEM]
        mem1[Explicit Stack for Iterative Mode]
        mem2[System Call Stack for Recursive Mode]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        out1[Filled Region on Display]
        out2[Termination Signal]
    end

    ipA --> ctr1
    ipB --> ctr1
    ipC --> ctr1
    ctr1 --> ctr2
    ctr2 --> eng1
    eng1 --> eng2
    eng2 --> eng3
    eng2 --> mem1
    eng2 --> mem2
    eng3 --> out1
    mem1 --> out2
    mem2 --> out2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — 8-Connected Neighborhood Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Current Pixel x, y] --> N1[N1: x, y plus 1]
    P --> N2[N2: x, y minus 1]
    P --> N3[N3: x plus 1, y]
    P --> N4[N4: x minus 1, y]
    P --> N5[N5: x plus 1, y plus 1]
    P --> N6[N6: x plus 1, y minus 1]
    P --> N7[N7: x minus 1, y plus 1]
    P --> N8[N8: x minus 1, y minus 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The central node `P` is the current pixel. The 8 surrounding nodes `N1` through `N8` are its neighbors. For a **4-connected** algorithm, only `N1`, `N2`, `N3`, `N4` are processed. For an **8-connected** algorithm, all 8 nodes are processed.

## 4.5 Mermaid Decision Tree — Boundary vs Flood Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Are you filling a closed shape with a known boundary color} -- Yes --> A1[Use Boundary Fill Algorithm]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Do you want to replace a uniform target color region}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Use Flood Fill Algorithm]
    Q2 -- No --> A3[Re-evaluate problem requirements]
    A1 --> S1{Connectivity 4 or 8}
    A2 --> S2{Connectivity 4 or 8}
    S1 --> EXE1[Execute with appropriate guard conditions]
    S2 --> EXE2[Execute with color mismatch stop]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Boundary Fill Algorithm. Why is it called a "boundary-based" fill?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Boundary Fill Algorithm is an area-filling procedure that begins at a seed point located *inside* a closed region and recursively paints neighboring pixels with a fill color, stopping only when it encounters pixels of the *boundary color*. It is called "boundary-based" because the **stopping condition depends entirely on the presence of a pre-defined boundary color** surrounding the region. Without a closed, uniformly-colored boundary, the algorithm cannot guarantee containment. The algorithm uses either 4-connected or 8-connected adjacency. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Boundary Fill and Flood Fill algorithms in three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Boundary Fill | Flood Fill |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Requires a pre-defined *boundary color* to terminate. | Requires only a *target color* to be replaced. |
| 2 | Used to fill closed shapes (polygons, circles). | Used to replace connected color regions (paint-bucket). |
| 3 | Cannot proceed if the boundary is not closed. | Can leak across the image if target color is widespread. |

**(3 marks)** — Award 1 mark per correct differentiation point.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **Boundary Fill Algorithm** in detail with a suitable algorithm. Discuss the difference between 4-connected and 8-connected approaches. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (Boundary Fill, 4-Connected, Recursive):**
```
procedure boundaryFill(x, y, fillColor, boundaryColor)
    int current = getPixel(x, y)
    if (current ≠ boundaryColor) AND (current ≠ fillColor) then
        setPixel(x, y, fillColor)
        boundaryFill(x + 1, y, fillColor, boundaryColor)
        boundaryFill(x - 1, y, fillColor, boundaryColor)
        boundaryFill(x, y + 1, fillColor, boundaryColor)
        boundaryFill(x, y - 1, fillColor, boundaryColor)
    end if
end procedure
```

**Key Steps Explained:**
1. **Input:** Seed point $(x, y)$, fill color, boundary color.
2. **Termination checks:** Two guards — stop if pixel is already the fill color (avoids infinite recursion) or if it matches the boundary color (stops the spread).
3. **Recursion:** Four recursive calls for 4-connected; eight calls for 8-connected.
4. **Output:** The interior of the closed region becomes uniformly filled.

**4-Connected vs 8-Connected Difference:**

| Aspect | 4-Connected | 8-Connected |
|---|---|---|
| Neighbors considered | 4 (N, S, E, W) | 8 (N, S, E, W, NE, NW, SE, SW) |
| Speed | Slower for diagonal regions | Faster for diagonal regions |
| Boundary requirement | Stricter (must close 4-conn) | Stricter (must close 8-conn) |
| Risk of leak | Diagonal gaps leak | No diagonal leak |

**4-connected** may *leak* through a single diagonal gap in the boundary because it does not consider diagonal pixels as connected. **8-connected** treats diagonal pixels as neighbors, so it cannot leak through such gaps. **(7 marks — 4 marks for algorithm, 3 marks for connectivity comparison)**

---

**(b)** Explain the **Flood Fill Algorithm** with a procedure and mention its real-world applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (Flood Fill, 4-Connected, Recursive):**
```
procedure floodFill(x, y, oldColor, newColor)
    int current = getPixel(x, y)
    if (current = oldColor) then
        setPixel(x, y, newColor)
        floodFill(x + 1, y, oldColor, newColor)
        floodFill(x - 1, y, oldColor, newColor)
        floodFill(x, y + 1, oldColor, newColor)
        floodFill(x, y - 1, oldColor, newColor)
    end if
end procedure
```

**Explanation of Working:**
1. The function reads the color of the seed pixel $(x, y)$, which becomes the `oldColor`.
2. If the current pixel still matches `oldColor`, it is repainted with `newColor`.
3. The recursion continues in 4 directions, terminating naturally when all pixels in the connected component have been converted (i.e., when the function encounters a pixel that is *not* `oldColor`).
4. **No boundary is required** — the algorithm is bounded only by the extent of the target color.

**Real-World Applications:**
- **Image editing software:** The "paint bucket" tool in MS Paint, GIMP, Photoshop uses flood fill.
- **Map editors:** Detecting connected landmasses, lakes, or regions in 2D tile maps (used in games like Civilization).
- **Medical imaging:** Segmenting tumors or anatomical structures by threshold-based region growing.
- **PCB design & GIS:** Identifying contiguous zones for masking, analysis, or rendering.
- **Computer vision:** Pre-processing step for blob detection and connected component labeling.

**(7 marks — 3 marks for procedure, 2 marks for explanation, 2 marks for applications)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare **Boundary Fill** and **Flood Fill** algorithms. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other? Justify with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Detailed Comparison:**

| Parameter | Boundary Fill | Flood Fill |
|---|---|---|
| **Algorithm name origin** | Stops at boundary | Spreads like a flood |
| **Stopping condition** | Hits a boundary color | Runs out of target color |
| **Input requirements** | Boundary color, fill color, seed | Old color, new color, seed |
| **Implementation complexity** | Two guard conditions | One guard condition |
| **Region guarantee** | Always interior of a closed shape | Any connected color region |
| **Memory overhead** | Identical | Identical |
| **Error sensitivity** | Breaks if boundary is open | Breaks if old color is widespread |

**When to Prefer Boundary Fill:**
- **Prefer** boundary fill when the region to be filled is a **closed, well-defined shape** (e.g., a polygon, circle, ellipse) with a known boundary color. For example, in a CAD system where you draw a triangle outline in black and want to fill its interior with cyan.

**When to Prefer Flood Fill:**
- **Prefer** flood fill when the goal is to **replace one specific color** in an image or map. For example, recoloring the green grass area of a photo to yellow, or identifying all contiguous forest tiles in a strategy game.

**Justification by Example:**
- Drawing a **clip-art** of a filled sun: Boundary fill — the sun's circular outline is the boundary.
- Changing the **background color of a portrait**: Flood fill — the background is a single connected color region with no predefined boundary.
- Filling a **polygon in OpenGL legacy mode**: Boundary fill — the polygon's edges are the boundary.

**(7 marks — 3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for preferences, 2 marks for examples)**

---

**(b)** A closed triangular region is defined by vertices $A(2, 2)$, $B(8, 2)$, $C(5, 7)$ in a raster grid. The boundary is drawn in RED (color value = 1) and the interior is WHITE (color value = 0). Using **4-connected Boundary Fill**, trace the first **8 pixels** that will be filled if the seed is at $(4, 3)$ and the fill color is BLUE (color value = 2). Show each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We maintain a **visit order list** (depth-first, processing neighbors in the order: East, West, North, South). We assume the triangle's interior at the start includes the seed and its 4-connected neighbors. We label each step.

| Step | Pixel (x, y) | Original Color | New Color | Reason / Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(4, 3)$ | WHITE (0) | BLUE (2) | Seed point; passed both guards. |
| 2 | $(5, 3)$ | WHITE (0) | BLUE (2) | East neighbor of (4,3). |
| 3 | $(6, 3)$ | WHITE (0) | BLUE (2) | East neighbor of (5,3). |
| 4 | $(7, 3)$ | WHITE (0) | BLUE (2) | East neighbor of (6,3). |
| 5 | $(7, 2)$ | RED (1) | **STOP** | Hits boundary — recursion halts. |
| 6 | (backtrack) $(6, 2)$ | RED (1) | **STOP** | Hits boundary. |
| 7 | (backtrack) $(5, 2)$ | RED (1) | **STOP** | Hits boundary. |
| 8 | (backtrack) $(4, 2)$ | RED (1) | **STOP** | Hits boundary. |

**Continuation Note:** After the East branch exhausts, the algorithm backtracks to $(4, 3)$ and processes the West neighbor $(3, 3)$, then North neighbors going up the triangle, etc.

**Key Observations:**
- The algorithm fills pixels in a **depth-first** order along the East direction first.
- When it hits the RED boundary, it **does not** paint the boundary pixel; it simply returns from that recursive call.
- The filled pixels form a **horizontal scan** along $y = 3$ from $x = 4$ to $x = 7$ before backtracking.
- Total pixels filled in the first 8 *step events*: 4 pixels painted + 4 stop events at the boundary.

**(7 marks — 1 mark per step row, plus 1 mark for the conceptual explanation of the trace order)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Boundary/Flood Fill questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "already-filled" guard** — without it, the recursive call enters an infinite loop. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this omission.
> 2. **Confusing 4-conn vs 8-conn** — if asked for 4-connected, listing 8 neighbors in the procedure loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the boundary color check** in flood fill (or vice versa) — the two algorithms are commonly interchanged, costing **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Not specifying the seed point** in pseudocode — KTU expects `setPixel(x, y, fillColor)` and recurse; a missing seed definition loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the stop conditions** — both `if` conditions (boundary and already-filled) are mandatory; missing one loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Ignoring time/space complexity** — Part B questions of 14 marks often include a 1-mark sub-question on complexity. Omit it = lose 1 mark.
> 7. **Writing only "boundary fill is used for polygons"** — be specific: mention *closed* polygons, *specific* boundary color, *recursive* spread. Vague answers lose **1–2 marks** for lack of precision.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Boundary Fill:** Stops at a **boundary color**; requires a **closed** region. Stop conditions = `(color = boundary) OR (color = fill)`.
- **Flood Fill:** Stops when **color mismatch** occurs; does **not** require a boundary. Stop condition = `(color ≠ oldColor)`.
- **4-connected** = 4 neighbors (N, S, E, W); **8-connected** = 4 + 4 diagonals.
- **8-connected** is faster but more prone to leaking in flood fill; **4-connected** is more conservative.
- **Time complexity** of both algorithms = $O(N)$ where $N$ = number of pixels in the region.
- **Space complexity (recursive)** = $O(D_{max})$ where $D_{max}$ = max recursion depth.
- **Iterative version** uses an explicit stack to avoid stack overflow on large regions.
- **Boundary fill leaks** if the boundary is not closed; **flood fill leaks** if the target color appears outside the intended region.
- **Real-world uses:** CAD (boundary), MS Paint bucket (flood), medical image segmentation (flood), GIS (flood), PCB solder masks (boundary).
- **Diagonal gap rule:** A 1-pixel diagonal gap in a 4-connected boundary allows leakage; 8-connected boundary is immune to diagonal gaps.
- **Always include the "already-painted" guard** to prevent infinite recursion — this is a **mandatory** element for full marks.
- **Pseudocode must specify** the seed point input, both guard conditions, and the recursion on neighbors in a **specific order** (East, West, North, South is KTU-standard).
- **Applications worth memorizing:** CAD polygon fill, MS Paint bucket tool, MRI tumor segmentation, game map continent detection, PCB solder-mask generation.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Boundary Filling and Flood Filling**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformations and Clipping Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transformations and Clipping Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Geometric Transformations — Formal Definition

In Computer Graphics, a **geometric transformation** is a mathematical operation that maps every point $P(x, y)$ of a graphical object to a new point $P'(x', y')$ by applying a structured rule, typically represented as a matrix multiplication in **homogeneous coordinate space**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for OECST835 (Module 3), the fundamental 2D transformations covered are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Transformation (KTU Formal Definition)**
> A geometric transformation $T$ is a bijective (or sometimes non-bijective) function $T: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ that repositions, resizes, or reorients a graphical primitive (point, line, polygon) by manipulating its vertex coordinates through an affine matrix equation of the form $P' = M \cdot P$, where $M$ is a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous transformation matrix and $P$ is a column vector in homogeneous form $(x, y, 1)^T$.

The five canonical 2D transformations are:
1. **Translation** — shifting a point by $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$
2. **Scaling** — stretching/compressing along axes by factors $s_x, s_y$
3. **Rotation** — pivoting about the origin by angle $\theta$
4. **Reflection** — mirroring across an axis or line
5. **Shearing** — skewing parallel to an axis

### 1.2 Clipping Algorithms — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clipping (KTU Formal Definition)**
> Clipping is the process of identifying and retaining only those portions of a graphical object (point, line segment, polygon) that lie **inside** a specified region called the **clip window** (typically a rectangular window with boundaries $x_{\min}, x_{\max}, y_{\min}, y_{\max}$), while discarding the portions that lie outside.

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically requires mastery of:
- **Point Clipping** (trivial: range check)
- **Line Clipping** — Cohen-Sutherland algorithm and Liang-Barsky algorithm
- **Polygon Clipping** — Sutherland-Hodgman algorithm

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies — Why Do We Need These?

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Transformations = "Moving Furniture on a Blueprint"**
> Imagine you are an architect looking at a 2D blueprint of a house. *Translation* is sliding the entire house 5 cm to the right on the paper. *Scaling* is photocopying the house at 50% size. *Rotation* is turning the house 90° so its long side faces north. *Reflection* is placing a mirror along one edge of the paper. *Shearing* is pushing the top of the house sideways while keeping the bottom fixed (like a parallelogram leaning over). All these operations are described compactly by **one single 3×3 matrix**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Clipping = "Photographing Through a Window"**
> Imagine a beautiful tree outside your room, but you can only see it through a rectangular window frame. The frame *clips* your view: only the part of the tree visible through the rectangle appears in the picture. **Cohen-Sutherland** is like marking each endpoint of a branch with a 4-bit region code (above, below, right, left of the window) to instantly decide if the entire branch is inside, entirely outside, or partially visible. **Liang-Barsky** is like using a parametric slider ($t = 0$ at one end, $t = 1$ at the other) to find the exact points where the branch enters and exits the window — extremely efficient because it often finishes in just one pass.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta$ | Rotation angle | **radians** (math) or **degrees** (UI) |
| $s_x, s_y$ | Scaling factors | unitless ratios |
| $t_x, t_y$ | Translation offsets | pixels or world units |
| $sh_x, sh_y$ | Shear factors | unitless |
| Clip window | $x_w^{\min}, x_w^{\max}, y_w^{\min}, y_w^{\max}$ | **device coordinates** |
| Outcode | 4-bit code $(c_1 c_2 c_3 c_4)$ | **0–15** (decimal) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Homogeneous Coordinate System — The Backbone of CG

A 2D point $(x, y)$ is represented as $(x_h, y_h, w)$ with the actual Cartesian coordinates recovered as $x = x_h / w$, $y = y_h / w$. Choosing $w = 1$ gives the standard **augmented vector**:

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This allows all five transformations (including translation, which is not linear) to be unified as a **single $3 \times 3$ matrix multiplication**.

### 2.2 The Five Fundamental 2D Transformation Matrices

**Why $3 \times 3$?** Because translation requires addition ($\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{bmatrix}$), and pure matrix multiplication is linear — it cannot add constants. We add a third row/column to make translation representable as multiplication.

#### (a) Translation Matrix $T(t_x, t_y)$

$$
T(t_x, t_y) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### (b) Scaling Matrix $S(s_x, s_y)$ (about origin)

$$
S(s_x, s_y) = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### (c) Rotation Matrix $R(\theta)$ (counter-clockwise about origin)

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### (d) Reflection Matrices

$$
\text{About } x\text{-axis: } M_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad\quad
\text{About } y\text{-axis: } M_y = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\text{About line } y = x: M_{yx} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad\quad
\text{About origin: } M_O = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### (e) Shear Matrices

$$
\text{X-Shear: } Sh_x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & sh_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad\quad
\text{Y-Shear: } Sh_y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ sh_y & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.3 Composite Transformations — The Composition Order Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Multiplication Rule (CRITICAL for KTU Board Exams)**
> Matrix multiplication is **NOT commutative** ($AB \neq BA$ in general). The transformation applied **first** in the geometric sequence appears **rightmost** in the matrix product. If the intended geometric sequence is: *first scale, then rotate, then translate*, the composite matrix is:
> $$C = T \cdot R \cdot S$$

### 2.4 Pivot-Point Rotation and Fixed-Point Scaling

To rotate about an arbitrary point $(x_p, y_p)$ instead of the origin, the composite matrix is:

$$
R_p = T(x_p, y_p) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p)
$$

To scale about a fixed point $(x_f, y_f)$:

$$
S_f = T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Transformation | Composite Matrix Form | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Translation $(t_x, t_y)$ | $T(t_x, t_y)$ | $t_x, t_y \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | Scaling about origin | $S(s_x, s_y)$ | $s_x, s_y \neq 0$ |
| 3 | Rotation about origin (CCW) | $R(\theta)$ | $\theta$ in radians (board) |
| 4 | Reflection across X-axis | $M_x$ | flips $y$-sign |
| 5 | Reflection across Y-axis | $M_y$ | flips $x$-sign |
| 6 | Reflection across $y = x$ | $M_{yx}$ | swaps coordinates |
| 7 | X-Shear | $Sh_x$ | $y' = y$, $x'$ shifted |
| 8 | Y-Shear | $Sh_y$ | $x' = x$, $y'$ shifted |
| 9 | Rotation about $(x_p, y_p)$ | $T(x_p, y_p) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p)$ | 3-step process |
| 10 | Scaling about $(x_f, y_f)$ | $T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$ | 3-step process |
| 11 | General 2D affine | $\begin{bmatrix} a & b & c \\ d & e & f \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | last row always $(0,0,1)$ |

### 2.6 Cohen-Sutherland Line Clipping — Region Codes

Each endpoint of a line is assigned a **4-bit outcode** based on its position relative to the clip window:

$$
\text{bit 4 (LEFT)} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x < x_{\min} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \quad\quad
\text{bit 3 (RIGHT)} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > x_{\max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{bit 2 (BOTTOM)} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } y < y_{\min} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \quad\quad
\text{bit 1 (TOP)} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } y > y_{\max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

| Condition | Action |
|---|---|
| $\text{code}_1 \lor \text{code}_2 = 0$ | **Trivial Accept** (line fully inside) |
| $\text{code}_1 \land \text{code}_2 \neq 0$ | **Trivial Reject** (line fully outside) |
| Otherwise | Subdivide and clip against a boundary |

### 2.7 Liang-Barsky Parametric Line Clipping

A line segment is parametrized as $P(t) = P_1 + t(P_2 - P_1)$, $0 \le t \le 1$, giving:

$$
x(t) = x_1 + t \cdot \Delta x, \quad y(t) = y_1 + t \cdot \Delta y, \quad \Delta x = x_2 - x_1, \quad \Delta y = y_2 - y_1
$$

The four inequalities $x_{\min} \le x(t) \le x_{\max}$ and $y_{\min} \le y(t) \le y_{\max}$ yield:

$$
p_1 = -\Delta x, \quad q_1 = x_1 - x_{\min} \quad\quad p_2 = \Delta x, \quad q_2 = x_{\max} - x_1
$$

$$
p_3 = -\Delta y, \quad q_3 = y_1 - y_{\min} \quad\quad p_4 = \Delta y, \quad q_4 = y_{\max} - y_1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of These Algorithms**
> - **Transformations** drive every animation, game engine, CAD modeler, and VR headset (e.g., a 3D object in Unreal Engine is rotated/scaled 60 times per second using exactly these matrices).
> - **Cohen-Sutherland** is used in graphics hardware for fast screen-space rejection of off-screen primitives.
> - **Liang-Barsky** is more efficient (faster on average) because it computes both clipping points in one pass — preferred in printer drivers and high-resolution plotting.
> - **Sutherland-Hodgman** is the standard for polygon clipping in OpenGL/Vulkan-based renderers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 2D Rotation Matrix

We want to rotate a point $P(x, y)$ by an angle $\theta$ counter-clockwise about the origin to obtain $P'(x', y')$. Express the original point in polar form:

$$
x = r \cos\phi, \quad y = r \sin\phi
$$

After rotation by $\theta$, the new angle becomes $\phi + \theta$:

$$
x' = r \cos(\phi + \theta), \quad y' = r \sin(\phi + \theta)
$$

Apply the trigonometric addition identities:

$$
x' = r(\cos\phi \cos\theta - \sin\phi \sin\theta)
$$

$$
y' = r(\sin\phi \cos\theta + \cos\phi \sin\theta)
$$

Substitute back $r\cos\phi = x$ and $r\sin\phi = y$:

$$
x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta
$$

$$
y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta
$$

Express this system as a matrix equation (in homogeneous form):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Sanity Check:** At $\theta = 0$, we get the identity matrix (no change). At $\theta = 90° = \pi/2$, $\cos 90° = 0$ and $\sin 90° = 1$, giving $(x', y') = (-y, x)$ — which is the correct geometric result. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation of Composite Rotation about a Pivot Point $(x_p, y_p)$

To rotate a point $(x, y)$ about $(x_p, y_p)$ by $\theta$ CCW, we decompose the operation into three sequential steps:

**Step 1** — Translate the pivot point to the origin: $T(-x_p, -y_p)$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ y_1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_p \\ 0 & 1 & -y_p \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \implies \begin{cases} x_1 = x - x_p \\ y_1 = y - y_p \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2** — Apply standard rotation: $R(\theta)$

$$
\begin{cases} x_2 = x_1\cos\theta - y_1\sin\theta \\ y_2 = x_1\sin\theta + y_1\cos\theta \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3** — Translate back: $T(x_p, y_p)$

$$
\begin{cases} x' = x_2 + x_p \\ y' = y_2 + y_p \end{cases}
$$

**Composite Matrix** $C = T(x_p, y_p) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p)$:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x_p \\ 0 & 1 & y_p \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -x_p \\ 0 & 1 & -y_p \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

First multiply $R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p)$:

$$
R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & -x_p\cos\theta + y_p\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & -x_p\sin\theta - y_p\cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now multiply $T(x_p, y_p) \cdot$ (result above):

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_p(1 - \cos\theta) + y_p\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_p(1 - \cos\theta) - x_p\sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Expanded transformation equations:**

$$
x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta + x_p(1 - \cos\theta) + y_p\sin\theta
$$

$$
y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta + y_p(1 - \cos\theta) - x_p\sin\theta
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the X-Shear Matrix

X-shear shifts $x$-coordinate in proportion to $y$:

$$
x' = x + sh_x \cdot y, \quad y' = y
$$

In matrix form (homogeneous):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & sh_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Inverse X-Shear** (for undoing the operation):

$$
Sh_x^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -sh_x & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Composite Transformation

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A triangle has vertices $A(1,1)$, $B(4,1)$, $C(1,5)$. Apply the following sequence of operations and find the final coordinates:
> 1. Scale by $S(2, 0.5)$ about origin
> 2. Rotate by $90°$ CCW about origin
> 3. Translate by $T(3, -2)$

**Step 1: Build the composite matrix $C = T(3, -2) \cdot R(90°) \cdot S(2, 0.5)$**

Scaling matrix:

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Rotation matrix (90° CCW: $\cos 90° = 0$, $\sin 90° = 1$):

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Translation matrix:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $R \cdot S$ first**

$$
R \cdot S = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $C = T \cdot (R \cdot S)$**

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -0.5 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Apply to each vertex**

For $A(1, 1)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x'_A \\ y'_A \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -0.5 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0(1) + (-0.5)(1) + 3(1) \\ 2(1) + 0(1) + (-2)(1) \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $A' = (2.5, 0)$.

For $B(4, 1)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x'_B \\ y'_B \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -0.5 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 - 0.5 + 3 \\ 8 + 0 - 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 6 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $B' = (2.5, 6)$.

For $C(1, 5)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x'_C \\ y'_C \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -0.5 & 3 \\ 2 & 0 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 - 2.5 + 3 \\ 2 + 0 - 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

So $C' = (0.5, 0)$.

**Final transformed triangle vertices:** $A'(2.5, 0)$, $B'(2.5, 6)$, $C'(0.5, 0)$.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Cohen-Sutherland Algorithm Walkthrough

> **Problem:** Clip the line $P_1(2, 5)$ to $P_2(8, 2)$ against the window $x_{\min}=3$, $x_{\max}=7$, $y_{\min}=2$, $y_{\max}=6$.

**Step 1: Compute outcode for $P_1(2, 5)$**

$x = 2 < 3$ → LEFT = 1
$x = 2 < 7$ → RIGHT = 0
$y = 5 > 2$ → BOTTOM = 0
$y = 5 < 6$ → TOP = 0
$\text{code}_1 = 0001$ (binary) = **1** (decimal)

**Step 2: Compute outcode for $P_2(8, 2)$**

$x = 8 > 3$ → LEFT = 0
$x = 8 > 7$ → RIGHT = 1
$y = 2 \not< 2$ → BOTTOM = 0
$y = 2 \not> 6$ → TOP = 0
$\text{code}_2 = 0100$ (binary) = **4** (decimal)

**Step 3: Test trivial conditions**

$\text{code}_1 \lor \text{code}_2 = 0001 \lor 0100 = 0101 \neq 0$ → **Not trivially accepted**
$\text{code}_1 \land \text{code}_2 = 0001 \land 0100 = 0000 = 0$ → **Not trivially rejected**

**Step 4: Subdivision — clip against LEFT boundary ($x = 3$)**

For $P_1$ (LEFT bit is 1, i.e., outside left):

$$
y = y_1 + \frac{(y_2 - y_1)(x_{\min} - x_1)}{x_2 - x_1} = 5 + \frac{(2 - 5)(3 - 2)}{8 - 2} = 5 + \frac{-3 \cdot 1}{6} = 5 - 0.5 = 4.5
$$

Replace $P_1$ with $(3, 4.5)$. Recompute outcode: $3 \not< 3$, $3 \not> 7$, $4.5 \not< 2$, $4.5 \not> 6$ → new $\text{code}_1 = 0000$.

**Step 5: Re-test**

$\text{code}_1 = 0000$, $\text{code}_2 = 0100$. AND = 0 (not rejected). OR $\neq 0$ (not accepted). Subdivide again.

Clip against RIGHT boundary for $P_2$ (RIGHT bit = 1):

$$
y = 4.5 + \frac{(2 - 4.5)(7 - 3)}{8 - 3} = 4.5 + \frac{(-2.5)(4)}{5} = 4.5 - 2 = 2.5
$$

Replace $P_2$ with $(7, 2.5)$. Recompute outcode: 0000.

**Step 6: Trivial accept**

Both codes are 0000. **Clipped line: $(3, 4.5)$ to $(7, 2.5)$.**

### 3.6 Liang-Barsky Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Clip the line from $P_1(2, -1)$ to $P_2(6, 5)$ against window $x \in [1, 5]$, $y \in [0, 4]$.

**Step 1: Compute $\Delta x$, $\Delta y$**

$\Delta x = 6 - 2 = 4$, $\Delta y = 5 - (-1) = 6$

**Step 2: Compute $p$ and $q$ values**

$p_1 = -\Delta x = -4$, $q_1 = x_1 - x_{\min} = 2 - 1 = 1$
$p_2 = \Delta x = 4$, $q_2 = x_{\max} - x_1 = 5 - 2 = 3$
$p_3 = -\Delta y = -6$, $q_3 = y_1 - y_{\min} = -1 - 0 = -1$
$p_4 = \Delta y = 6$, $q_4 = y_{\max} - y_1 = 4 - (-1) = 5$

**Step 3: Initialize $t_{\text{enter}} = 0$, $t_{\text{leave}} = 1$**

| Edge $i$ | $p_i$ | $q_i$ | $r_i = q_i / p_i$ | Updated $(t_E, t_L)$ | Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Left) | $-4$ | $1$ | $-0.25$ | — | $p < 0$, $q \ge 0$ → entering |
| | | | | $t_E = \max(0, -0.25) = 0$ | |
| 2 (Right) | $4$ | $3$ | $0.75$ | — | $p > 0$, $q \ge 0$ → leaving |
| | | | | $t_L = \min(1, 0.75) = 0.75$ | |
| 3 (Bottom) | $-6$ | $-1$ | $0.1667$ | $p < 0$, $q < 0$ → outside, reject line? |
| | | | | No — only if $r > t_L$ does it reject | |

Since $p_3 < 0$ and $q_3 < 0$, the line is **entering from outside the bottom**. So we need to update $t_E = \max(t_E, q_i / p_i) = \max(0, 0.1667) = 0.1667$.

But $t_E = 0.1667 > t_L = 0.75$? No, $0.1667 < 0.75$, so line is still potentially inside.

Edge 4 (Top): $p_4 = 6 > 0$, $q_4 = 5 \ge 0$, $r_4 = 5/6 = 0.8333$. Update $t_L = \min(0.75, 0.8333) = 0.75$.

Final: $t_E = 0.1667$, $t_L = 0.75$. Since $t_E \le t_L$, line is **partially inside**.

**Step 4: Compute clipped endpoints**

$x'_1 = 2 + 0.1667 \cdot 4 = 2.667$, $y'_1 = -1 + 0.1667 \cdot 6 = 0$
$x'_2 = 2 + 0.75 \cdot 4 = 5$, $y'_2 = -1 + 0.75 \cdot 6 = 3.5$

**Clipped line:** $(2.667, 0)$ to $(5, 3.5)$.

### 3.7 Sutherland-Hodgman Polygon Clipping — The Algorithm

For a polygon with vertices $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_n$, the algorithm processes one clip boundary at a time. For each edge of the polygon (from $S$ to $P$):

| Case | $S$ Position | $P$ Position | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inside | Inside | $P$ |
| 2 | Inside | Outside | Intersection $I$ |
| 3 | Outside | Inside | $I$, then $P$ |
| 4 | Outside | Outside | Nothing |

This is applied **four times** — once for each window edge (left, right, bottom, top). The output of one pass becomes the input polygon for the next.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Graphics Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OBJECT COORDINATES] --> B[MODELING TRANSFORM]
    B --> C[WORLD COORDINATES]
    C --> D[VIEWING TRANSFORM]
    D --> E[VIEW COORDINATES]
    E --> F[PROJECTION TRANSFORM]
    F --> G[NORMALIZED DEVICE COORDS]
    G --> H[VIEWPORT TRANSFORM]
    H --> I[DEVICE COORDINATES]
    I --> J[CLIPPING STAGE]
    J --> K[RASTERIZATION]
    K --> L[FRAME BUFFER]
```

### 4.2 Cohen-Sutherland Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START] --> nodeB[Read Line Endpoints P1 P2]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Outcode for P1]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute Outcode for P2]
    nodeD --> nodeE{code1 OR code2 equals 0}
    nodeE -->|YES TRUE| nodeF[TRIVIAL ACCEPT]
    nodeE -->|NO| nodeG{code1 AND code2 equals 0}
    nodeG -->|NO FALSE| nodeH[TRIVIAL REJECT]
    nodeG -->|YES TRUE| nodeI[Pick Endpoint Outside]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Clip Against Boundary Edge]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Replace Endpoint with Intersection]
    nodeK --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeL[OUTPUT CLIPPED LINE]
    nodeH --> nodeL
```

### 4.3 Liang-Barsky Parametric Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[INITIALIZE tE equals 0 tL equals 1] --> stepB[For Each of 4 Edges]
    stepB --> stepC{Is p equals 0}
    stepC -->|YES| stepD{Is q less than 0}
    stepD -->|YES| stepE[REJECT LINE PARALLEL AND OUTSIDE]
    stepD -->|NO| stepB
    stepC -->|NO| stepF{Is p less than 0}
    stepF -->|YES ENTERING| stepG[r equals q divided by p]
    stepG --> stepH[Update tE equals maximum of tE and r]
    stepH --> stepI{Is tE greater than tL}
    stepI -->|YES| stepE
    stepI -->|NO| stepB
    stepF -->|NO LEAVING| stepJ[r equals q divided by p]
    stepJ --> stepK[Update tL equals minimum of tL and r]
    stepK --> stepL{Is tE greater than tL}
    stepL -->|YES| stepE
    stepL -->|NO| stepB
    stepB -->|ALL EDGES DONE| stepM{Is tE less than or equal tL}
    stepM -->|YES| stepN[ACCEPT Compute New Endpoints]
    stepM -->|NO| stepE
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture — Composite Transformation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[INPUT VERTEX x y] --> inB[APPLY SCALING MATRIX 3x3]
    inB --> inC[APPLY ROTATION MATRIX 3x3]
    inC --> inD[APPLY TRANSLATION MATRIX 3x3]
    inD --> inE[OUTPUT VERTEX x PRIME y PRIME]
    inF[MATRIX STACK STORES ORDER] --> inB
    inF --> inC
    inF --> inD
```

### 4.5 Block Architecture — Sutherland-Hodgman Polygon Clipper

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    poly1[INPUT POLYGON VERTEX LIST] --> pass1[CLIP AGAINST LEFT EDGE]
    pass1 --> pass2[CLIP AGAINST RIGHT EDGE]
    pass2 --> pass3[CLIP AGAINST BOTTOM EDGE]
    pass3 --> pass4[CLIP AGAINST TOP EDGE]
    pass4 --> poly2[OUTPUT CLIPPED POLYGON]
    rule1[TEST EACH EDGE INSIDE OUTSIDE] --> pass1
    rule1 --> pass2
    rule1 --> pass3
    rule1 --> pass4
```

### 4.6 Region Code Assignment Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[POINT x y] --> q1{TEST x LESS THAN XMIN}
    q1 -->|YES| r1[SET LEFT BIT 1]
    q1 -->|NO| r2[SET LEFT BIT 0]
    p1 --> q2{TEST x GREATER THAN XMAX}
    q2 -->|YES| r3[SET RIGHT BIT 1]
    q2 -->|NO| r4[SET RIGHT BIT 0]
    p1 --> q3{TEST y LESS THAN YMIN}
    q3 -->|YES| r5[SET BOTTOM BIT 1]
    q3 -->|NO| r6[SET BOTTOM BIT 0]
    p1 --> q4{TEST y GREATER THAN YMAX}
    q4 -->|YES| r7[SET TOP BIT 1]
    q4 -->|NO| r8[SET TOP BIT 0]
    r1 --> fin[COMBINE 4 BITS INTO OUTCODE]
    r2 --> fin
    r3 --> fin
    r4 --> fin
    r5 --> fin
    r6 --> fin
    r7 --> fin
    r8 --> fin
```

---
