## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define homogeneous coordinates. Why are they used in computer graphics?*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Homogeneous coordinates** represent an n-dimensional point as an (n+1)-dimensional vector. A 2D point $(x, y)$ is written as $(x, y, w)$ where the actual Cartesian coordinates are $x/w$ and $y/w$. For most uses, $w = 1$.
>
> **Why used in CG (1 mark each point):**
> 1. **Unified matrix representation** — All five 2D transformations (translation, scaling, rotation, reflection, shear) can be expressed as a single $3 \times 3$ matrix multiplication $P' = M \cdot P$. Without homogeneous form, translation (which is additive) cannot be represented as a $2 \times 2$ matrix multiplication.
> 2. **Composition is matrix multiplication** — Multiple transformations can be combined simply by multiplying their matrices, which is the foundation of the graphics transformation pipeline.
> 3. **Perspective division** — When $w \neq 1$ (e.g., in 3D perspective projection), homogeneous coordinates naturally handle the perspective foreshortening.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Differentiate between Cohen-Sutherland and Liang-Barsky line clipping algorithms.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cohen-Sutherland | Liang-Barsky |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | **Subdivision-based** (encode regions, clip incrementally) | **Parametric** (uses $t \in [0, 1]$) |
| Trivial accept/reject | Yes — fast checks via outcodes | Yes — via $p$ and $q$ analysis |
| Number of iterations | May need **up to 4 passes** (one per edge) | **Single pass** in most cases |
| Floating-point ops per line | More (re-evaluates outcodes repeatedly) | Fewer (computes both $t_E$, $t_L$ at once) |
| Best suited for | Lines with many trivial cases | Lines that need precise parametric clipping |
| Clipped point count | Can be 0, 1 (degenerate), or 2 | Always 0, 1, or 2 (cleaner logic) |

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Composite Transformations + Cohen-Sutherland**

> **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** A triangle has vertices $A(1, 0)$, $B(3, 0)$, $C(1, 3)$.
> Perform the following sequence of transformations in the given order:
> (i) Scale by $(3, 2)$ about origin
> (ii) Rotate by $30°$ counter-clockwise about origin
> (iii) Translate by $(4, -1)$
> Find the coordinates of the final triangle. Show all matrix multiplications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Apply the Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm to clip the line segment from $P_1(-2, 6)$ to $P_2(8, -1)$ against the clipping window with $x_{\min} = 0$, $x_{\max} = 6$, $y_{\min} = 0$, $y_{\max} = 4$. Show outcode calculations at each step. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Build individual transformation matrices**

Scaling:

$$
S(3, 2) = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Rotation (with $\cos 30° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$, $\sin 30° = 0.5$):

$$
R(30°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Translation:

$$
T(4, -1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Build composite matrix $C = T \cdot R \cdot S$**

First compute $M_1 = R \cdot S$:

$$
M_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.598 & -1.0 & 0 \\ 1.5 & 1.732 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now compute $C = T \cdot M_1$:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.598 & -1.0 & 0 \\ 1.5 & 1.732 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} (2.598 + 4) & (-1.0 + 0) & (0 + 4) \\ (1.5 + 0) & (1.732 + 0) & (0 - 1) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.598 & -1.0 & 4 \\ 1.5 & 1.732 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Apply to each vertex**

For $A(1, 0, 1)$:

$$
A' = \begin{bmatrix} 6.598 & -1.0 & 4 \\ 1.5 & 1.732 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.598 + 0 + 4 \\ 1.5 + 0 - 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10.598 \\ 0.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$A' = (10.598, 0.5)$

For $B(3, 0, 1)$:

$$
B' = \begin{bmatrix} 6.598 & -1.0 & 4 \\ 1.5 & 1.732 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 19.794 + 0 + 4 \\ 4.5 + 0 - 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 23.794 \\ 3.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$B' = (23.794, 3.5)$

For $C(1, 3, 1)$:

$$
C' = \begin{bmatrix} 6.598 & -1.0 & 4 \\ 1.5 & 1.732 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.598 - 3 + 4 \\ 1.5 + 5.196 - 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7.598 \\ 5.696 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$C' = (7.598, 5.696)$

**Final transformed triangle:** $A'(10.598, 0.5)$, $B'(23.794, 3.5)$, $C'(7.598, 5.696)$

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Outcode for $P_1(-2, 6)$**

$x = -2 < 0 = x_{\min}$ → LEFT = 1
$x = -2 < 6$ → RIGHT = 0
$y = 6 > 0$ → BOTTOM = 0
$y = 6 > 4$ → TOP = 1
$\text{code}_1 = 1001$ (binary) = **9**

**Step 2: Outcode for $P_2(8, -1)$**

$x = 8 > 0$ → LEFT = 0
$x = 8 > 6$ → RIGHT = 1
$y = -1 < 0$ → BOTTOM = 1
$y = -1 < 4$ → TOP = 0
$\text{code}_2 = 0110$ (binary) = **6**

**Step 3: Trivial check**

OR = $1001 \lor 0110 = 1111 \neq 0$ → not trivially accepted
AND = $1001 \land 0110 = 0000 = 0$ → not trivially rejected → must subdivide

**Step 4: First subdivision — clip $P_1$ against TOP edge ($y = 4$)**

For $P_1$ (TOP bit is 1):

$$
x = x_1 + \frac{(x_2 - x_1)(y_{\max} - y_1)}{y_2 - y_1} = -2 + \frac{(8 - (-2))(4 - 6)}{-1 - 6} = -2 + \frac{10 \cdot (-2)}{-7} = -2 + \frac{-20}{-7} = -2 + 2.857 = 0.857
$$

Replace $P_1$ with $(0.857, 4)$. New outcode: $x=0.857$ is not less than 0, not greater than 6, $y=4$ is not greater than 4. So $\text{code}_1 = 0000$.

**Step 5: Second subdivision — clip $P_2$ against BOTTOM edge ($y = 0$)**

For $P_2$ (BOTTOM bit is 1):

$$
x = 0.857 + \frac{(8 - 0.857)(0 - 4)}{-1 - 4} = 0.857 + \frac{7.143 \cdot (-4)}{-5} = 0.857 + \frac{-28.572}{-5} = 0.857 + 5.714 = 6.571
$$

Replace $P_2$ with $(6.571, 0)$. New outcode: $x=6.571$ is greater than 6 → RIGHT = 1. So $\text{code}_2 = 0100$.

**Step 6: Third subdivision — clip $P_2$ against RIGHT edge ($x = 6$)**

For $P_2$ (RIGHT bit is 1):

$$
y = 4 + \frac{(-1 - 4)(6 - 0.857)}{8 - 0.857} = 4 + \frac{(-5)(5.143)}{7.143} = 4 + \frac{-25.714}{7.143} = 4 - 3.6 = 0.4
$$

Replace $P_2$ with $(6, 0.4)$. New outcode: 0000.

**Step 7: Trivial accept**

Both codes are 0000. **Clipped line: $(0.857, 4)$ to $(6, 0.4)$.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Liang-Barsky + Reflections and Shear**

> **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Using the Liang-Barsky line clipping algorithm, clip the line segment from $P_1(1, 4)$ to $P_2(7, -2)$ against the rectangular window with $x_{\min} = 2$, $x_{\max} = 6$, $y_{\min} = 1$, $y_{\max} = 5$. Show all four $p_i$ and $q_i$ computations and the final clipped line. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the matrix for reflection of a 2D point about the line $y = x$. Also find the matrix for reflection about the line $y = -x$. Show the geometric effect on a sample triangle with vertices $(1, 2)$, $(3, 2)$, $(1, 4)$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Compute $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$**

$\Delta x = 7 - 1 = 6$, $\Delta y = -2 - 4 = -6$

**Step 2: Compute $p_i$ and $q_i$ for all four edges**

$p_1 = -\Delta x = -6$, $q_1 = x_1 - x_{\min} = 1 - 2 = -1$
$p_2 = \Delta x = 6$, $q_2 = x_{\max} - x_1 = 6 - 1 = 5$
$p_3 = -\Delta y = 6$, $q_3 = y_1 - y_{\min} = 4 - 1 = 3$
$p_4 = \Delta y = -6$, $q_4 = y_{\max} - y_1 = 5 - 4 = 1$

**Step 3: Initialize $t_E = 0$, $t_L = 1$**

**Edge 1 (Left):** $p_1 = -6 < 0$, $q_1 = -1 < 0$ → entering, update $t_E$

$$
r_1 = q_1 / p_1 = -1 / -6 = 0.1667
$$

$t_E = \max(0, 0.1667) = 0.1667$

**Edge 2 (Right):** $p_2 = 6 > 0$, $q_2 = 5 \ge 0$ → leaving, update $t_L$

$$
r_2 = q_2 / p_2 = 5 / 6 = 0.8333
$$

$t_L = \min(1, 0.8333) = 0.8333$

**Edge 3 (Bottom):** $p_3 = 6 > 0$, $q_3 = 3 \ge 0$ → leaving, update $t_L$

$$
r_3 = q_3 / p_3 = 3 / 6 = 0.5
$$

$t_L = \min(0.8333, 0.5) = 0.5$

**Edge 4 (Top):** $p_4 = -6 < 0$, $q_4 = 1 \ge 0$ → entering, update $t_E$

$$
r_4 = q_4 / p_4 = 1 / -6 = -0.1667
$$

$t_E = \max(0.1667, -0.1667) = 0.1667$

**Step 4: Final check**

$t_E = 0.1667 \le t_L = 0.5$ → **Line is partially inside**

**Step 5: Compute clipped endpoints**

$x'_1 = 1 + 0.1667 \cdot 6 = 1 + 1.0 = 2.0$
$y'_1 = 4 + 0.1667 \cdot (-6) = 4 - 1.0 = 3.0$

$x'_2 = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 6 = 1 + 3 = 4.0$
$y'_2 = 4 + 0.5 \cdot (-6) = 4 - 3 = 1.0$

**Clipped line:** $(2, 3)$ to $(4, 1)$.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Reflection about $y = x$**

For a point $(x, y)$, reflection across the line $y = x$ swaps the coordinates: $(x, y) \to (y, x)$.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
M_{y=x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Reflection about $y = -x$**

For a point $(x, y)$, reflection across the line $y = -x$ maps to $(-y, -x)$:

$$
M_{y=-x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Apply $M_{y=x}$ to triangle vertices**

For $(1, 2)$: $(x', y') = (2, 1)$
For $(3, 2)$: $(x', y') = (2, 3)$
For $(1, 4)$: $(x', y') = (4, 1)$

**Reflected triangle vertices:** $(2, 1)$, $(2, 3)$, $(4, 1)$.

**Step 4: Apply $M_{y=-x}$ to triangle vertices**

For $(1, 2)$: $(x', y') = (-2, -1)$
For $(3, 2)$: $(x', y') = (-2, -3)$
For $(1, 4)$: $(x', y') = (-4, -1)$

**Reflected triangle vertices:** $(-2, -1)$, $(-2, -3)$, $(-4, -1)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Order of Matrix Multiplication Reversed (2-mark penalty):** Students often write $C = S \cdot R \cdot T$ when the sequence is "first scale, then rotate, then translate." The correct composite is $C = T \cdot R \cdot S$. The first transformation in sequence is **rightmost** in the matrix product.
> 2. **Outcode Bit Assignment Error (1-mark penalty):** Students confuse the bit order. The KTU standard is LEFT = bit 4 (most significant), RIGHT = bit 3, BOTTOM = bit 2, TOP = bit 1 (least significant). Getting this wrong invalidates all subsequent trivial accept/reject tests.
> 3. **Trigonometric Sign Error in Rotation (1-mark penalty):** Forgetting the negative sign on $-\sin\theta$ in the $(0, 0)$ position of the rotation matrix. Memorize: $\begin{bmatrix} +\cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ +\sin\theta & +\cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$.
> 4. **Liang-Barsky Direction Confusion (1.5-mark penalty):** Mixing up the entering and leaving cases. Rule: $p < 0$ means the line is moving from outside to inside as $t$ increases (entering); $p > 0$ means moving from inside to outside (leaving).
> 5. **Missing Homogeneous Coordinate:** Failing to use a $3 \times 3$ matrix form for 2D transformations — the third row/column $(0, 0, 1)$ is **mandatory** for translation to work via matrix multiplication.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**
>
> **Transformations:**
> - All 2D points become column vectors $(x, y, 1)^T$ in homogeneous form.
> - Rotation matrix (CCW): $\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — remember the **minus sign**.
> - Scale matrix: $\begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & s_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
> - Translate matrix: $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
> - Reflection about $y = x$: swap coordinates. About $y = -x$: negate and swap.
> - **Composite transformations** = matrix product in **reverse order** of execution: $C = T_n \cdots T_2 T_1$.
> - **Pivot rotation** about $(x_p, y_p)$: $T(x_p, y_p) \cdot R(\theta) \cdot T(-x_p, -y_p)$.
> - **Fixed-point scaling** about $(x_f, y_f)$: $T(x_f, y_f) \cdot S(s_x, s_y) \cdot T(-x_f, -y_f)$.
>
> **Clipping Algorithms:**
> - **Cohen-Sutherland:** 4-bit outcode (TBRL or LRBT). Trivial accept if OR = 0; trivial reject if AND $\neq 0$. Subdivide iteratively.
> - **Liang-Barsky:** Parametric $t \in [0, 1]$. Compute four $(p_i, q_i)$ pairs. Update $t_E$ when $p < 0$, update $t_L$ when $p > 0$. Reject if $t_E > t_L$.
> - **Sutherland-Hodgman (Polygon):** Four-edge sequential clipping — Left → Right → Bottom → Top. Each vertex classified as inside/outside; output determined by the four-case table.
> - **Clipping** is essential for viewport mapping and to avoid drawing off-screen pixels (performance optimization).
> - **Order of clipping in the graphics pipeline** is: 1) Clipping in world/view space, 2) Projection, 3) Clipping in homogeneous coordinates, 4) Perspective division, 5) Viewport mapping.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Window to Viewport Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Window to Viewport Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In computer graphics, the **Window-to-Viewport Transformation** is a fundamental **2D geometric mapping operation** that converts object coordinates defined in the **World Coordinate System (WCS)** — referred to as the *window* — into corresponding display coordinates in the **Device Coordinate System (DCS)** — referred to as the *viewport*. This transformation forms a critical stage of the **Viewing Pipeline**, sitting precisely between the *World Coordinate Stage* and the *Device Coordinate Stage*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A **window** is a rectangular region in **world coordinates** that selects what portion of a scene is to be displayed. A **viewport** is a rectangular region on the **output device** (display screen / display surface) onto which the selected window contents are mapped. The **window-to-viewport mapping** is an affine transformation (a combination of translation, scaling, and translation) that establishes a one-to-one correspondence between the points of the window and the points of the viewport.

**Mathematical Re-statement:** Given a window defined by corners $(x_{w_{\min}}, y_{w_{\min}})$ and $(x_{w_{\max}}, y_{w_{\max}})$, and a viewport defined by corners $(x_{v_{\min}}, y_{v_{\min}})$ and $(x_{v_{\max}}, y_{v_{\max}})$, every world point $(x_w, y_w)$ inside the window is mapped to a unique device point $(x_v, y_v)$ inside the viewport via the linear fractional transformation stated in Section 2.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are a **photographer** standing in front of a giant landscape painting (this is your *world*).

- You raise a **rectangular cardboard frame** (this is your *window*) and look only at the part of the painting visible through that frame.
- You then take that framed portion of the painting and **stretch it to fit onto a 4×6 inch photo print** (this is your *viewport*).
- The cardboard frame decides **WHAT** part of the world to capture. The photo print size decides **WHERE and HOW BIG** it will appear on paper.

That is *exactly* the Window-to-Viewport Transformation:
- **Window** → decides *what* to show (selection in world space).
- **Viewport** → decides *where* and *at what size* to show it (display mapping).

> [!NOTE]
> The world can be infinite, but the screen is finite. The window-to-viewport mapping is essentially the **bridge that fits an arbitrarily large (or small, or skewed) piece of the world into the bounded display surface**, without altering the *relative* geometry of the selected scene.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - The mapping is a **two-step affine** operation: **Translation → Scaling → Translation**.
> - The scaling factors along the X and Y axes are **independent**.
> - Aspect ratio preservation requires $S_x = S_y$ (similarity transformation).
> - Default device coordinates usually lie in the range **$[0, 1023] \times [0, 767]$** for a $1024 \times 768$ VGA display, or normalized to **$[0, 1] \times [0, 1]$** in NDC.
> - All mappings assume **clipping has already been performed**, so only points strictly inside the window need to be remapped.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Decomposed Into Logical Steps

The window-to-viewport transformation is conceptually a **three-stage affine pipeline** applied independently along the X and Y axes. The procedure is:

1. **Step 1 — Translate the window origin to $(0,0)$:**  
   Subtract $(x_{w_{\min}}, y_{w_{\min}})$ from every world point so that the lower-left corner of the window coincides with the origin of a new local coordinate system. This isolates the window's *internal geometry* from its absolute world position.

2. **Step 2 — Scale the unitless $[0,1] \times [0,1]$ region into the viewport size:**  
   Multiply the translated coordinates by the scaling factors $S_x$ and $S_y$. The factor $S_x$ stretches/shrinks the window width to match the viewport width, and similarly $S_y$ does so for the height. This is the *only* step that may introduce non-uniformity.

3. **Step 3 — Translate the scaled region to the viewport's lower-left corner:**  
   Add $(x_{v_{\min}}, y_{v_{\min}})$ to position the now-correctly-sized region exactly inside the display's viewport rectangle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the three-step decomposition?**  
> Because matrix multiplication and affine geometry demand that we keep the **operations composable**. The combined transformation matrix is:
> $$T = T(x_{v_{\min}}, y_{v_{\min}}) \cdot S(S_x, S_y) \cdot T(-x_{w_{\min}}, -y_{w_{\min}})$$

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Mapping

- **Why do we need this mapping?**  
  Because the world is described in *floating-point world units* (e.g., meters, kilometers, light-years, abstract CAD units) while the screen is a *finite grid of pixels*. We need a deterministic function that decides, for *any* world point inside the window, *exactly which pixel* on the screen to light up.

- **How does the mapping preserve geometry?**  
  By using a *linear fractional* relationship. Since both X and Y are mapped via identical-form equations, straight lines in the world remain straight lines in the viewport, and parallel lines remain parallel. This is what makes the transformation an **affine map** (not a projective map).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains **every equation you will be tested on** for the Window-to-Viewport Transformation in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| **Symbol / Term** | **Definition / Formula** | **Units / Range** |
|---|---|---|
| Window X range | $\left[x_{w_{\min}},\; x_{w_{\max}}\right]$ | World units |
| Window Y range | $\left[y_{w_{\min}},\; y_{w_{\max}}\right]$ | World units |
| Viewport X range | $\left[x_{v_{\min}},\; x_{v_{\max}}\right]$ | Device units (pixels/NDC) |
| Viewport Y range | $\left[y_{v_{\min}},\; y_{v_{\max}}\right]$ | Device units (pixels/NDC) |
| X-axis scaling factor | $S_x \;=\; \dfrac{x_{v_{\max}} - x_{v_{\min}}}{x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Y-axis scaling factor | $S_y \;=\; \dfrac{y_{v_{\max}} - y_{v_{\min}}}{y_{w_{\max}} - y_{w_{\min}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Mapped X coordinate | $x_v \;=\; S_x \left(x_w - x_{w_{\min}}\right) + x_{v_{\min}}$ | Device units |
| Mapped Y coordinate | $y_v \;=\; S_y \left(y_w - y_{w_{\min}}\right) + y_{v_{\min}}$ | Device units |
| Similarity condition | $S_x \;=\; S_y$ | No distortion |
| Aspect ratio (window) | $AR_w \;=\; \dfrac{x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}}}{y_{w_{\max}} - y_{w_{\min}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Aspect ratio (viewport) | $AR_v \;=\; \dfrac{x_{v_{\max}} - x_{v_{\min}}}{y_{v_{\max}} - y_{v_{\min}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Composite affine matrix | $T \;=\; T(x_{v_{\min}}, y_{v_{\min}})\cdot S(S_x, S_y)\cdot T(-x_{w_{\min}},-y_{w_{\min}})$ | $3 \times 3$ |

> [!WARNING]
> Do not write the formula as $x_v = (x_{v_{\max}} - x_{v_{\min}}) \cdot (x_w - x_{w_{\min}}) / (x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}}) + x_{v_{\min}}}$ in board exams — the compact two-line form with $S_x$ defined separately earns the examiner's favor.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAD / CAM Systems:** Engineers draw machine parts in millimeters (world units) and view them in pixel coordinates on a 4K monitor.
- **Medical Imaging (CT/MRI Scans):** Patient anatomy is in physical space (cm); the radiologist's screen has pixels. Window-to-Viewport allows **zoom and pan** of internal organs.
- **GIS (Geographic Information Systems):** Earth's latitude/longitude (world) is mapped onto the user's mobile screen (viewport).
- **Game Engines:** The 3D world's projection onto the 2D screen is a generalized, perspective-aware cousin of this 2D mapping.
- **Flight Simulators & Drone Dashboards:** A small window in the world is amplified onto a large viewport for the pilot's head-up display (HUD).

The single underlying idea — *select a rectangle from a large space and project it onto a smaller display rectangle* — is ubiquitous in modern engineering visualization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the X-Component Mapping Equation

We begin with the requirement that the mapping be **affine** (a linear function of $x_w$). Let the mapping be of the form:

$$
x_v \;=\; A \cdot x_w + B
$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants to be determined from the boundary correspondences.

**Boundary Condition 1 — Lower-left corner of the window must map to the lower-left corner of the viewport:**

When $x_w = x_{w_{\min}}$, we require $x_v = x_{v_{\min}}$. Substituting:

$$
x_{v_{\min}} \;=\; A \cdot x_{w_{\min}} + B \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

**Boundary Condition 2 — Upper-right corner of the window must map to the upper-right corner of the viewport:**

When $x_w = x_{w_{\max}}$, we require $x_v = x_{v_{\max}}$. Substituting:

$$
x_{v_{\max}} \;=\; A \cdot x_{w_{\max}} + B \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Solving for $A$ by subtraction (ii) − (i):**

The $B$ terms cancel because both contain $+B$, leaving:

$$
x_{v_{\max}} - x_{v_{\min}} \;=\; A \cdot \left(x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}}\right)
$$

Dividing both sides by $(x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}})$:

$$
A \;=\; \frac{x_{v_{\max}} - x_{v_{\min}}}{x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}}} \;\equiv\; S_x
$$

**Solving for $B$ by substitution back into (i):**

$$
B \;=\; x_{v_{\min}} - S_x \cdot x_{w_{\min}}
$$

**Composing the final expression $x_v = A \cdot x_w + B$:**

$$
x_v \;=\; S_x \cdot x_w + \left(x_{v_{\min}} - S_x \cdot x_{w_{\min}}\right)
$$

**Distributing and grouping for the canonical KTU form:**

$$
x_v \;=\; S_x \cdot x_w - S_x \cdot x_{w_{\min}} + x_{v_{\min}}
$$

$$
x_v \;=\; S_x \left(x_w - x_{w_{\min}}\right) + x_{v_{\min}}
$$

This is the **standard KTU board answer form** for the X-axis mapping. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Y-Component Mapping Equation

The Y-axis derivation is **completely analogous** to the X-axis derivation, with $y$ replacing $x$ throughout. Therefore, by direct substitution of variables:

$$
y_v \;=\; S_y \left(y_w - y_{w_{\min}}\right) + y_{v_{\min}}
$$

where the Y-axis scaling factor is independently defined as:

$$
S_y \;=\; \frac{y_{v_{\max}} - y_{v_{\min}}}{y_{w_{\max}} - y_{w_{\min}}}
$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Composite Affine Transformation Matrix

The complete window-to-viewport operation can be expressed as a single $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Window}\to\text{Viewport}} \;&=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & x_{v_{\min}} \\
0 & 1 & y_{v_{\min}} \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
S_x & 0 & 0 \\
0 & S_y & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -x_{w_{\min}} \\
0 & 1 & -y_{w_{\min}} \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading right-to-left (matrix multiplication order), this applies the operations in the canonical sequence:

**Translate to origin → Scale → Translate to viewport corner.**

Multiplying the rightmost two matrices first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \cdot T_{\text{shift}} \;&=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
S_x & 0 & -S_x \cdot x_{w_{\min}} \\
0 & S_y & -S_y \cdot y_{w_{\min}} \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying this by the leftmost translation matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{final}} \;&=\; 
\begin{bmatrix}
S_x & 0 & -S_x \cdot x_{w_{\min}} + x_{v_{\min}} \\
0 & S_y & -S_y \cdot y_{w_{\min}} + y_{v_{\min}} \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

When this matrix acts on the homogeneous point $\begin{bmatrix} x_w & y_w & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T$, the result is exactly the canonical KTU formulas. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (For Board Exam Practice)

**Problem Statement:**  
A window has its lower-left corner at $(1, 1)$ and upper-right corner at $(5, 9)$. The viewport has its lower-left corner at $(0, 0)$ and upper-right corner at $(200, 100)$. Map the world point $P(3, 5)$ to viewport coordinates.

**Step 1 — Compute the scaling factors $S_x$ and $S_y$:**

$$
S_x \;=\; \frac{200 - 0}{5 - 1} \;=\; \frac{200}{4} \;=\; 50
$$

$$
S_y \;=\; \frac{100 - 0}{9 - 1} \;=\; \frac{100}{8} \;=\; 12.5
$$

**Step 2 — Note that $S_x \neq S_y$:**

Since $50 \neq 12.5$, this is a **non-similarity transformation**. Distortion will occur: a unit square in the world will appear as a $50 \times 12.5$ rectangle on screen, with its horizontal side stretched 4× more than the vertical side.

**Step 3 — Apply the mapping formulas to the point $P(3, 5)$:**

$$
x_v \;=\; S_x (x_w - x_{w_{\min}}) + x_{v_{\min}} \;=\; 50 \cdot (3 - 1) + 0 \;=\; 50 \cdot 2 \;=\; 100
$$

$$
y_v \;=\; S_y (y_w - y_{w_{\min}}) + y_{v_{\min}} \;=\; 12.5 \cdot (5 - 1) + 0 \;=\; 12.5 \cdot 4 \;=\; 50
$$

**Final Answer:** The world point $P(3, 5)$ maps to the viewport point $P'(100, 50)$.

**Verification by Boundary Check:**

- Window corner $(1, 1) \to$ Viewport corner $(0, 0)$ ✓
- Window corner $(5, 9) \to$ Viewport corner $(200, 100)$ ✓
- World point $(3, 5)$ lies *exactly* halfway along X and halfway along Y in the window (since $\frac{3-1}{5-1} = 0.5$ and $\frac{5-1}{9-1} = 0.5$). The viewport point $(100, 50)$ also lies exactly halfway along X and Y in the viewport ✓ (proportional position is preserved even when aspect ratio is not).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**  
**Define the terms *window* and *viewport* in the context of 2D computer graphics. State the fundamental difference between them in terms of the coordinate system in which they are defined.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**  
A **window** is a rectangular region in the **World Coordinate System (WCS)** that is used to select the portion of a scene that is to be displayed. It is specified using world coordinates $(x_{w_{\min}}, y_{w_{\min}})$ and $(x_{w_{\max}}, y_{w_{\max}})$. A **viewport** is a rectangular region in the **Device Coordinate System (DCS)** that specifies where on the output display device the contents of the selected window are to be mapped. It is specified using device coordinates $(x_{v_{\min}}, y_{v_{\min}})$ and $(x_{v_{\max}}, y_{v_{\max}})$.  
The fundamental difference is that the window exists in the abstract, application-specific world coordinate space (which can be infinite and uses units like meters, inches, or abstract CAD units), whereas the viewport exists in the bounded, physical device coordinate space (which is finite, usually measured in pixels, and lies within the physical screen's resolution).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**  
**Distinguish between *similarity transformation* and *non-similarity transformation* in the context of window-to-viewport mapping. Under what condition does each occur?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**  
A **similarity transformation** is one in which the scaling factors along the X and Y axes are equal, i.e., $S_x = S_y$. In this case, the relative proportions (aspect ratio) of objects are preserved exactly — a circle in the world remains a circle in the viewport, a square remains a square.  
A **non-similarity transformation** is one in which the scaling factors are unequal, i.e., $S_x \neq S_y$. This occurs when the **aspect ratio of the window does not match the aspect ratio of the viewport**. In this case, objects get distorted — a circle in the world becomes an ellipse in the viewport, and a square becomes a rectangle.  
The condition is: if $\dfrac{x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}}}{y_{w_{\max}} - y_{w_{\min}}} = \dfrac{x_{v_{\max}} - x_{v_{\min}}}{y_{v_{\max}} - y_{v_{\min}}}$, then $S_x = S_y$ and a similarity transformation occurs; otherwise it is a non-similarity transformation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of **window-to-viewport coordinate transformation** in detail. Derive the transformation equations for both the X and Y coordinates. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A window in world coordinates is the rectangular region from which we want to display content. A viewport in device coordinates is the rectangular area on the output screen where this content will be drawn. The window-to-viewport transformation maps every point $(x_w, y_w)$ in the window to a corresponding point $(x_v, y_v)$ in the viewport.

The transformation is an **affine** map of the form $x_v = A \cdot x_w + B$. Using the boundary correspondences:
- $(x_{w_{\min}}, y_{w_{\min}}) \longrightarrow (x_{v_{\min}}, y_{v_{\min}})$
- $(x_{w_{\max}}, y_{w_{\max}}) \longrightarrow (x_{v_{\max}}, y_{v_{\max}})$

We solve for the X-axis constants. Substituting both boundary conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{v_{\min}} &= A \cdot x_{w_{\min}} + B \\
x_{v_{\max}} &= A \cdot x_{w_{\max}} + B
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the first equation from the second:

$$
x_{v_{\max}} - x_{v_{\min}} = A \cdot (x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}})
$$

Hence the X-scaling factor:

$$
A \;\equiv\; S_x \;=\; \frac{x_{v_{\max}} - x_{v_{\min}}}{x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}}}
$$

Substituting back to find $B$:

$$
B \;=\; x_{v_{\min}} - S_x \cdot x_{w_{\min}}
$$

Therefore the canonical X-mapping equation:

$$
x_v \;=\; S_x \cdot (x_w - x_{w_{\min}}) + x_{v_{\min}}
$$

By complete analogy (replace $x$ with $y$ throughout), the Y-mapping equation is:

$$
y_v \;=\; S_y \cdot (y_w - y_{w_{\min}}) + y_{v_{\min}}
$$

where

$$
S_y \;=\; \frac{y_{v_{\max}} - y_{v_{\min}}}{y_{w_{\max}} - y_{w_{\min}}}
$$

This is the **complete derivation** of the window-to-viewport transformation equations. *(7 marks awarded for: definition 1m, diagram 1m, affine form setup 1m, X-derivation 2m, Y-derivation 1m, final boxed formulas 1m.)*

---

**(b)** Given a window with lower-left corner at $(2, 1)$ and upper-right corner at $(8, 5)$, and a viewport with lower-left corner at $(0, 0)$ and upper-right corner at $(400, 300)$:  
(i) Compute the scaling factors $S_x$ and $S_y$.  
(ii) Identify whether this is a similarity or non-similarity transformation.  
(iii) Map the world point $P(5, 3)$ to its viewport coordinates.  
(iv) Verify the mapping by checking the four corner correspondences. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Compute $S_x$ and $S_y$:**

$$
S_x \;=\; \frac{400 - 0}{8 - 2} \;=\; \frac{400}{6} \;=\; \frac{200}{3} \;\approx\; 66.67
$$

$$
S_y \;=\; \frac{300 - 0}{5 - 1} \;=\; \frac{300}{4} \;=\; 75
$$

**Step (ii) — Similarity check:**  
Since $S_x = 66.67 \neq 75 = S_y$, this is a **non-similarity transformation**. There will be aspect-ratio distortion in the displayed output.

**Step (iii) — Map $P(5, 3)$:**

$$
x_v \;=\; \frac{200}{3} \cdot (5 - 2) + 0 \;=\; \frac{200}{3} \cdot 3 \;=\; 200
$$

$$
y_v \;=\; 75 \cdot (3 - 1) + 0 \;=\; 75 \cdot 2 \;=\; 150
$$

Mapped viewport point: $P'(200, 150)$.

**Step (iv) — Corner verification:**  
- $W(2, 1) \to V\!\left(\frac{200}{3}\cdot 0 + 0,\; 75\cdot 0 + 0\right) = (0, 0)$ ✓  
- $W(8, 1) \to V\!\left(\frac{200}{3}\cdot 6 + 0,\; 75\cdot 0 + 0\right) = (400, 0)$ ✓  
- $W(2, 5) \to V\!\left(\frac{200}{3}\cdot 0 + 0,\; 75\cdot 4 + 0\right) = (0, 300)$ ✓  
- $W(8, 5) \to V\!\left(\frac{200}{3}\cdot 6 + 0,\; 75\cdot 4 + 0\right) = (400, 300)$ ✓  

All four corners map exactly to the viewport boundary, confirming the correctness of the derived formulas. *(7 marks awarded for: 2m on Sx/Sy, 1m on similarity classification, 2m on point mapping, 2m on verification.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** What is **clipping**? Why must clipping be performed *before* the window-to-viewport transformation? Describe the **Cohen–Sutherland line clipping algorithm** in detail with all 9 region codes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Clipping:**  
Clipping is the process of removing those portions of a graphical primitive (line, polygon, etc.) that lie **outside** the window (or viewport). Only the part of the object that lies *inside* the window is retained for display.

**Why clip *before* the window-to-viewport transformation:**  

1. **Computational efficiency:** If a 10,000-vertex polygon has only 50 vertices inside the window, transforming all 10,000 world points to device coordinates wastes enormous computation. Clipping first reduces the data to only the relevant subset.
2. **Avoidance of ill-defined mappings:** Points strictly *outside* the window have no meaningful fractional position $t \in [0,1]$ in the window, so the linear mapping $x_v = S_x(x_w - x_{w_{\min}}) + x_{v_{\min}}$ would still produce a *number*, but that number would correspond to a screen position *outside* the viewport. The displayed output would be garbage pixels scattered on the screen.
3. **Memory and bandwidth savings:** In hardware pipelines, fewer clipped vertices means less data shuttled to the rasterizer.

**Cohen–Sutherland Line Clipping Algorithm:**  
Cohen–Sutherland is an **outcode-based** algorithm that classifies each line endpoint into one of **9 regions** using a 4-bit region code. The four bits, from MSB to LSB, indicate:  
- Bit 1 (Top): $y > y_{w_{\max}}$  
- Bit 2 (Bottom): $y < y_{w_{\min}}$  
- Bit 3 (Right): $x > x_{w_{\max}}$  
- Bit 4 (Left): $x < x_{w_{\min}}$  

The 9 region codes are:  

| **Code** | **Region** |
|---|---|
| 0000 | Inside the window (Trivially Accept) |
| 0001 | Left of window |
| 0010 | Right of window |
| 0100 | Below window |
| 1000 | Above window |
| 0101 | Lower-Left |
| 1001 | Upper-Left |
| 0110 | Lower-Right |
| 1010 | Upper-Right |

**Algorithm steps:**  
1. Compute the outcode for both endpoints $P_1$ and $P_2$.  
2. If $\text{outcode}(P_1) = \text{outcode}(P_2) = 0000$, the line is **trivially accepted** (fully inside).  
3. If $(\text{outcode}(P_1) \;\text{AND}\; \text{outcode}(P_2)) \neq 0000$, the line is **trivially rejected** (both endpoints lie on the *same outside side*).  
4. Otherwise, the line is **partially inside**. Find the intersection of the line with the window boundary corresponding to the first non-zero bit of an endpoint's outcode, clip the endpoint to that boundary, recompute the outcode, and repeat from step 1.  
*(7 marks awarded for: clipping definition 1m, justification 2m, Cohen–Sutherland description with 4-bit code and 9 regions 3m, algorithm steps 1m.)*

---

**(b)** Given a window $W$ with corners $(1, 1)$ and $(7, 5)$ and a viewport $V$ with corners $(0, 0)$ and $(640, 480)$:  
(i) Compute the scaling factors $S_x$ and $S_y$.  
(ii) Compute and tabulate the viewport coordinates of the four corners of the window.  
(iii) Compute the viewport coordinates of the world point $P(4, 3)$.  
(iv) State and verify whether this transformation is a similarity transformation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Compute $S_x$ and $S_y$:**

$$
S_x \;=\; \frac{640 - 0}{7 - 1} \;=\; \frac{640}{6} \;=\; \frac{320}{3} \;\approx\; 106.67
$$

$$
S_y \;=\; \frac{480 - 0}{5 - 1} \;=\; \frac{480}{4} \;=\; 120
$$

**Step (ii) — Corner mapping table:**

| **World Corner** | **Computation** | **Viewport Corner** |
|---|---|---|
| $(1, 1)$ | $\left(\frac{320}{3}\cdot 0 + 0,\;120\cdot 0 + 0\right)$ | $(0, 0)$ |
| $(7, 1)$ | $\left(\frac{320}{3}\cdot 6 + 0,\;120\cdot 0 + 0\right)$ | $(640, 0)$ |
| $(1, 5)$ | $\left(\frac{320}{3}\cdot 0 + 0,\;120\cdot 4 + 0\right)$ | $(0, 480)$ |
| $(7, 5)$ | $\left(\frac{320}{3}\cdot 6 + 0,\;120\cdot 4 + 0\right)$ | $(640, 480)$ |

All four corners map exactly to the viewport boundary. ✓

**Step (iii) — Map $P(4, 3)$:**

$$
x_v \;=\; \frac{320}{3} \cdot (4 - 1) + 0 \;=\; \frac{320}{3} \cdot 3 \;=\; 320
$$

$$
y_v \;=\; 120 \cdot (3 - 1) + 0 \;=\; 120 \cdot 2 \;=\; 240
$$

Mapped viewport point: $P'(320, 240)$.

**Step (iv) — Similarity check:**  
Since $S_x = 106.67 \neq 120 = S_y$, this is **not** a similarity transformation. It is a **non-similarity transformation**.  
**Verification:** A unit-length horizontal segment in the world (length 1 in X, 0 in Y) would render with horizontal pixel length $106.67$ on screen. A unit-length vertical segment in the world would render with vertical pixel length $120$ on screen. The unequal stretch factors confirm distortion. *(7 marks awarded for: 2m on Sx/Sy, 2m on corner table, 1m on point mapping, 2m on similarity check.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students 2 to 4 Marks Every Year:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the minus sign in $(x_w - x_{w_{\min}})$:** Many students write $x_v = S_x \cdot x_w + x_{v_{\min}}$ instead of the correct $x_v = S_x \cdot (x_w - x_{w_{\min}}) + x_{v_{\min}}$. This single missing parentheses loses **2 full marks** in Part B derivations.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up the scaling formula:** Students often compute $S_x = \frac{x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}}}{x_{v_{\max}} - x_{v_{\min}}}$ (inverted). Remember: the *numerator is the viewport* and the *denominator is the window*.
> 
> 3. **Not stating units or the coordinate system:** Always explicitly state whether a value is in *world units* or *device pixels*. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the boundary verification step:** The examiner expects you to verify that the four corners map correctly. Skipping this verification in a 14-mark question costs 1 mark minimum.
> 
> 5. **Confusing "similarity" with "identity":** Similarity means $S_x = S_y$ (preserved proportions), **not** that the transformation is the identity ($S_x = S_y = 1$). Identity is a special case of similarity.
> 
> 6. **Applying the formula to points outside the window:** The mapping equation is only valid for points *inside* the window. Mapping an outside point is a logical error worth 1 to 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Window to Viewport Transformation:**
> 
> - **Window** is in *World Coordinates (WCS)*; **Viewport** is in *Device Coordinates (DCS)*.
> - The transformation is a **three-stage affine pipeline**: Translate → Scale → Translate.
> - **X-mapping formula:** $x_v = S_x \cdot (x_w - x_{w_{\min}}) + x_{v_{\min}}$
> - **Y-mapping formula:** $y_v = S_y \cdot (y_w - y_{w_{\min}}) + y_{v_{\min}}$
> - **X-scaling factor:** $S_x = \dfrac{x_{v_{\max}} - x_{v_{\min}}}{x_{w_{\max}} - x_{w_{\min}}}$
> - **Y-scaling factor:** $S_y = \dfrac{y_{v_{\max}} - y_{v_{\min}}}{y_{w_{\max}} - y_{w_{\min}}}$
> - **Similarity** (no distortion) requires $S_x = S_y$, i.e., window aspect ratio = viewport aspect ratio.
> - **Non-similarity** (distortion) occurs when $S_x \neq S_y$ — circles become ellipses, squares become rectangles.
> - **Proportional position** $t$ is preserved: a point at fraction $t$ in the window is at fraction $t$ in the viewport, regardless of distortion.
> - **Clipping** must occur **before** the mapping, to avoid wasted computation and undefined output positions.
> - **Cohen–Sutherland** is the standard outcode-based line clipping algorithm (4-bit code, 9 regions).
> - The composite transformation is a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix: $T = T_{\text{viewport}} \cdot S \cdot T_{\text{window}}$.
> - Common applications: CAD, GIS, medical imaging, game engines, dashboards, and flight simulators.
> - **Verify the mapping** by checking the four corners — examiners reward this with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cohen Sutherland and Midpoint Subdivision Line Clipping Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cohen Sutherland and Midpoint Subdivision Line Clipping Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Line Clipping — Foundational Definition

**Line Clipping** is the process of cutting off (or "clipping away") the portions of a line segment that lie outside a specified region of interest, conventionally called the **clipping window**. In the context of 2D Computer Graphics (KTU Module 3), the clipping window is almost always an **axis-aligned rectangle** defined by the boundaries $(x_{min}, y_{min})$ and $(x_{max}, y_{max})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Line clipping is the operation of determining which portions of a line segment lie inside the rectangular clipping window and discarding (or retaining) the portions that lie outside it, using an efficient algorithmic procedure.*

A line segment $\overline{P_1P_2}$ with respect to the clipping window can fall into one of the following three categories:

1. **Trivially Acceptable** — the entire line lies inside the window. Output: draw the full line.
2. **Trivially Rejectable** — the entire line lies outside the window. Output: discard the line.
3. **Partially Clippable** — the line crosses one or more window edges. Output: clip at the intersection points and draw only the inner portion.

The two algorithms covered in this module — **Cohen-Sutherland** and **Midpoint Subdivision** — are classical, exam-favorite procedures that determine the above categories efficiently without performing expensive arithmetic on every line.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Picture Frame

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are holding a **rectangular picture frame** and a long **stick** (your line segment). You want to know exactly how much of the stick is visible *through* the frame.
>
> - **Cohen-Sutherland** is like tagging **both ends of the stick with directional stickers** (Top/Bottom/Right/Left) and then looking at the stickers to instantly decide:
>   - If **both stickers say "inside"** → the whole stick fits → draw the whole stick.
>   - If **both stickers share a "outside" direction** (e.g., both point Left) → the stick misses the frame completely → throw the stick away.
>   - Otherwise → **cut the stick where it crosses the frame's edge**, relabel, and repeat.
>
> - **Midpoint Subdivision** is like repeatedly **breaking the stick in half**:
>   - If a half is entirely outside (both ends on the same side of the frame) → throw it away.
>   - If a half has one end inside and one end outside → break it again, because it must cross the edge.
>   - Keep breaking until the boundary-crossing point is located with sufficient accuracy.

This "binary search" / "sticker" intuition is exactly the conceptual backbone the KTU examiner tests for.

---

## 1.3 Why Two Algorithms?

| Aspect | Cohen-Sutherland | Midpoint Subdivision |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategy** | Encodes position using bit-codes | Recursively halves the line |
| **Strength** | Very fast for trivial cases | Excellent for parallel/hardware implementation |
| **Weakness** | Slow for lines crossing many edges | Many midpoint evaluations needed |
| **Exam Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Highest) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme:** Both algorithms are part of the **core OECST835 syllabus** and are routinely asked in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) sections. Numerical problems on Cohen-Sutherland are among the **highest-weighted questions** in this module.

---

## 1.4 Formal Setup for Both Algorithms

Let the clipping window be:

$$W = \{(x, y) \mid x_{min} \le x \le x_{max} \ \text{and} \ y_{min} \le y \le y_{max}\}$$

Let the line segment endpoints be $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$. Both algorithms aim to compute the clipped segment (or signal rejection).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cohen-Sutherland Line Clipping Algorithm

### 2.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

The Cohen-Sutherland algorithm (1974) assigns a **4-bit binary code** (called the **outcode** or **region code**) to every point in the 2D plane, relative to the clipping window. By examining the bit patterns of the two endpoints, the algorithm can instantly determine the three cases (accept, reject, partial) using **bitwise OR** and **bitwise AND** operations.

### 2.1.2 The 4-Bit Outcode — TBRL Convention

The four bits are assigned as follows (with **bit 4 = Most Significant**):

| Bit Position | Name | Bit Set to 1 if... | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bit 4 (T) | Top | $y > y_{max}$ | Point is above the window |
| Bit 3 (B) | Bottom | $y < y_{min}$ | Point is below the window |
| Bit 2 (R) | Right | $x > x_{max}$ | Point is to the right of the window |
| Bit 1 (L) | Left | $x < x_{min}$ | Point is to the left of the window |

> [!NOTE]
> **The order TBRL (Top–Bottom–Right–Left)** is the universally followed convention. Memorize it as **"T-B-R-L"** — *Top, Bottom, Right, Left*. Examiners frequently give a 1-mark sub-question asking students to write this bit assignment table.

### 2.1.3 The 9 Regions of the 2D Plane

The 4 bits produce $2^4 = 16$ possible codes, but only **9 regions** are geometrically distinct (the four corner regions share a code with nothing else, and the four edge regions also share). The code **0000** is the "inside" region.

### 2.1.4 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. **Compute outcodes** $C_1$ and $C_2$ for endpoints $P_1$ and $P_2$.
2. **Bitwise test**:
   - If $C_1 \text{ OR } C_2 = 0000$ → **Trivial Accept** (line entirely inside, draw it).
   - If $C_1 \text{ AND } C_2 \neq 0000$ → **Trivial Reject** (line entirely outside, discard).
3. **Otherwise** (partial case):
   - Pick an endpoint $P$ that is outside the window (one of $P_1$ or $P_2$).
   - Determine which edge of the window the line crosses first (the leftmost 1-bit in $C$).
   - Find the **intersection point** of the line with that edge using the line equation.
   - **Replace** the outside endpoint with this intersection point.
   - **Recompute** its outcode and **go back to Step 2**.

### 2.1.5 Edge Intersection Formulas

For a line $P_1P_2$ with slope $m = (y_2 - y_1)/(x_2 - x_1)$:

$$y = y_1 + m(x - x_1) \quad \text{(slope-intercept form)}$$

$$x = x_1 + \frac{y - y_1}{m} \quad \text{(inverse, used when } m = 0 \text{ or } m \to \infty\text{)}$$

The intersection with each edge:

| Edge | Substitute in line equation |
|---|---|
| **Left** $x = x_{min}$ | $y = y_1 + m(x_{min} - x_1)$ |
| **Right** $x = x_{max}$ | $y = y_1 + m(x_{max} - x_1)$ |
| **Bottom** $y = y_{min}$ | $x = x_1 + (y_{min} - y_1)/m$ |
| **Top** $y = y_{max}$ | $x = x_1 + (y_{max} - y_1)/m$ |

### 2.1.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cohen-Sutherland is used as the **first stage of hierarchical clipping** in windowing systems, GUI toolkits (X11, early Windows GDI), and as a **pre-filter** in CAD software before more sophisticated polygon clipping (Sutherland-Hodgman) is applied.

---

## 2.2 Midpoint Subdivision Line Clipping Algorithm

### 2.2.1 Conceptual Foundation

The Midpoint Subdivision algorithm (also called the **Subdivision Method**) avoids computing line-equation intersections altogether. Instead, it uses a **divide-and-conquer** strategy combined with a simple **point-in-window test** (called the **Outcode Test** or **Position Test**).

The key insight: if a line segment has **one endpoint inside** and **one endpoint outside** the window, then it **must cross the window boundary exactly once**, and the crossing point lies in that segment.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

Given a line segment $P_1P_2$:

1. **Trivial checks**:
   - If both $P_1$ and $P_2$ are inside → **Trivial Accept**.
   - If both $P_1$ and $P_2$ are outside and lie in the **same region** (same outcode) → **Trivial Reject**.
2. **Otherwise**, compute the midpoint $M$ of $P_1P_2$.
3. Test the position of $M$:
   - If $M$ is **inside** the window → it is a point of the visible segment. Save $M$ as a boundary candidate. **Subdivide** into two sub-segments $P_1M$ and $MP_2$ and **recurse** on both.
   - If $M$ is **outside** and lies in the same region as both endpoints → **reject** the entire sub-segment.
   - If $M$ is **outside** but on the *opposite* side from one endpoint → the crossing point is in that sub-segment; recurse only on that part.
4. **Stop** when the sub-segment is reduced to pixel-size (length $\le \epsilon$), at which point its endpoint is taken as the boundary point.

### 2.2.3 Recursion Depth

For a line of length $L$ on a screen of pixel size $\delta$, the algorithm performs approximately:

$$N = \lceil \log_2(L / \delta) \rceil$$

levels of subdivision. For a 1024-pixel screen, this is at most **10 levels**, which is why this method is **favored for hardware implementation** (where each subdivision can be done in parallel using fixed-point arithmetic).

### 2.2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Midpoint subdivision is the workhorse of **GPU rasterization pipelines**, **hardware clipper units** in graphics accelerators, and **parallel line-clipping ASICs** because the midpoint calculation is a single add-and-shift — ideal for VLSI implementation.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers ~80% of all numerical/Part-A questions on this topic.**

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Condition / Use |
|---|---|---|
| $C = [T:B:R:L]$ | Outcode (4-bit TBRL) | Computed per endpoint |
| $T = 1$ | Top bit set | $y > y_{max}$ |
| $B = 1$ | Bottom bit set | $y < y_{min}$ |
| $R = 1$ | Right bit set | $x > x_{max}$ |
| $L = 1$ | Left bit set | $x < x_{min}$ |
| $C_1 \text{ OR } C_2 = 0000$ | **Trivial Accept** | Both inside window |
| $C_1 \text{ AND } C_2 \neq 0000$ | **Trivial Reject** | Both share outside region |
| $m = (y_2 - y_1) / (x_2 - x_1)$ | Slope of the line | Assumes $x_2 \neq x_1$ |
| $y = y_1 + m(x - x_1)$ | Slope-intercept form | For $x$-edge intersections |
| $x = x_1 + (y - y_1)/m$ | Inverse form | For $y$-edge intersections |
| $M = \left( \dfrac{x_1 + x_2}{2}, \dfrac{y_1 + y_2}{2} \right)$ | Midpoint of segment | Midpoint subdivision core |
| $\lvert x_M - x_P \rvert < \epsilon$ | Convergence criterion | $\epsilon$ = pixel size |
| $N = \lceil \log_2(L/\delta) \rceil$ | Recursion depth | $L$ = line length, $\delta$ = pixel size |

> **Note on notation:** $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ denotes **absolute value**. The bitwise OR and AND are written out as $\text{OR}$ and $\text{AND}$ to avoid the pipe symbol `$\vert$` colliding with markdown table syntax.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked numerical example** is provided below for both algorithms on the *same* line so you can compare their behavior side-by-side. This is the style KTU board examiners use in the 7-mark sub-parts.

## 3.1 Common Problem Setup

**Given:**

- Clipping window: $W = (x_{min}, y_{min}, x_{max}, y_{max}) = (1, 1, 5, 5)$
- Line endpoints: $P_1 = (0, 0)$ and $P_2 = (6, 6)$

**Goal:** Find the portion of $\overline{P_1P_2}$ that lies inside $W$ using both algorithms.

---

## 3.2 Cohen-Sutherland — Complete Step-by-Step Execution

### Step 1: Compute Outcodes

For $P_1 = (0, 0)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T: \quad & y_1 = 0, \; y_{max} = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; 0 > 5 \text{ is FALSE} \;\Rightarrow\; T = 0 \\
B: \quad & y_1 = 0, \; y_{min} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; 0 < 1 \text{ is TRUE} \;\Rightarrow\; B = 1 \\
R: \quad & x_1 = 0, \; x_{max} = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; 0 > 5 \text{ is FALSE} \;\Rightarrow\; R = 0 \\
L: \quad & x_1 = 0, \; x_{min} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; 0 < 1 \text{ is TRUE} \;\Rightarrow\; L = 1 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$C_1 = [T:B:R:L] = [0:1:0:1] = (0101)_2 = (5)_{10}$$

For $P_2 = (6, 6)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T: \quad & 6 > 5 \;\Rightarrow\; T = 1 \\
B: \quad & 6 < 1 \;\Rightarrow\; B = 0 \\
R: \quad & 6 > 5 \;\Rightarrow\; R = 1 \\
L: \quad & 6 < 1 \;\Rightarrow\; L = 0 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$C_2 = [T:B:R:L] = [1:0:1:0] = (1010)_2 = (10)_{10}$$

### Step 2: Bitwise Trivial Checks

$$C_1 \text{ OR } C_2 = (0101) \text{ OR } (1010) = (1111) \neq 0000 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NOT trivial accept}$$

$$C_1 \text{ AND } C_2 = (0101) \text{ AND } (1010) = (0000) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NOT trivial reject}$$

The line is **partially inside** the window.

### Step 3: First Clipping Iteration

Pick an outside endpoint, say $P_1$ (since $C_1 = 0101 \neq 0000$). The **leftmost set bit** in $C_1$ is the **L bit** (bit 1), so clip against the **left edge** $x = x_{min} = 1$.

**Slope computation:**

$$m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{6 - 0}{6 - 0} = 1$$

**Intersection with $x = 1$:**

$$y = y_1 + m(x_{min} - x_1) = 0 + 1 \cdot (1 - 0) = 1$$

**New endpoint:** $P_1' = (1, 1)$.

### Step 4: Recompute Outcode for $P_1'$

For $P_1' = (1, 1)$:

- $1 > 5$? No → $T = 0$
- $1 < 1$? No → $B = 0$
- $1 > 5$? No → $R = 0$
- $1 < 1$? No → $L = 0$

$$C_1' = 0000$$

### Step 5: Re-test with Updated Endpoints

New segment: $P_1'(1,1)$ to $P_2(6,6)$, codes $C_1' = 0000$, $C_2 = 1010$.

$$C_1' \text{ OR } C_2 = (0000) \text{ OR } (1010) = (1010) \neq 0000 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Still NOT trivial accept}$$

$$C_1' \text{ AND } C_2 = (0000) \text{ AND } (1010) = (0000) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Still NOT trivial reject}$$

### Step 6: Second Clipping Iteration

Now pick the outside endpoint, $P_2$ (code $C_2 = 1010$). The leftmost set bit is **R** (bit 2), so clip against the **right edge** $x = x_{max} = 5$.

**Intersection with $x = 5$:**

$$y = y_1 + m(x_{max} - x_1) = 0 + 1 \cdot (5 - 0) = 5$$

**New endpoint:** $P_2' = (5, 5)$.

### Step 7: Final Trivial Accept

Recompute $C_2'$ for $P_2' = (5, 5)$:

- $5 > 5$? No → $T = 0$
- $5 < 1$? No → $B = 0$
- $5 > 5$? No → $R = 0$
- $5 < 1$? No → $L = 0$

$$C_2' = 0000$$

$$C_1' \text{ OR } C_2' = (0000) \text{ OR } (0000) = 0000 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{TRIVIAL ACCEPT}$$

### ✅ Final Result (Cohen-Sutherland):

$$\boxed{\text{Clipped line segment} = \overline{(1, 1)\, (5, 5)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Midpoint Subdivision — Complete Step-by-Step Execution

**Same problem:** $P_1 = (0, 0)$, $P_2 = (6, 6)$, $W = (1, 1, 5, 5)$.

### Level 0: Initial Segment

- $P_1(0, 0)$ is **outside** (BL region).
- $P_2(6, 6)$ is **outside** (TR region).
- **Midpoint** $M_0$:

$$M_0 = \left( \frac{0+6}{2}, \frac{0+6}{2} \right) = (3, 3)$$

- **Position test for $M_0$:** Is $1 \le 3 \le 5$ and $1 \le 3 \le 5$? **YES, inside.**

→ **Save $M_0(3, 3)$** as a candidate boundary. Subdivide into two sub-segments: $P_1M_0$ and $M_0P_2$.

### Level 1A: Left Sub-segment $P_1(0,0)$ to $M_0(3,3)$

- Midpoint $M_1$:

$$M_1 = \left( \frac{0+3}{2}, \frac{0+3}{2} \right) = (1.5, 1.5)$$

- Position test for $M_1$: $1.5 \ge 1$ and $1.5 \le 5$? **YES, inside** (just barely, since $1.5 > 1$ and $1.5 < 5$).

→ **Save $M_1(1.5, 1.5)$** as a candidate boundary. Subdivide into $P_1M_1$ and $M_1M_0$.

### Level 1B: Right Sub-segment $M_0(3,3)$ to $P_2(6,6)$

- Midpoint $M_2$:

$$M_2 = \left( \frac{3+6}{2}, \frac{3+6}{2} \right) = (4.5, 4.5)$$

- Position test: $4.5 \le 5$? **YES, inside.**

→ **Save $M_2(4.5, 4.5)$** as a candidate boundary. Subdivide into $M_0M_2$ and $M_2P_2$.

### Level 2A: Sub-segment $P_1(0,0)$ to $M_1(1.5, 1.5)$ — Both Outside (BL region)

- Midpoint $M_3$:

$$M_3 = \left( \frac{0+1.5}{2}, \frac{0+1.5}{2} \right) = (0.75, 0.75)$$

- Position test: $0.75 < 1$ → **OUTSIDE** (BL region, same as both endpoints).
- **Trivial Reject** the entire sub-segment $P_1M_1$.

### Level 2B: Sub-segment $M_1(1.5, 1.5)$ to $M_0(3, 3)$

- $M_1$ is **inside**, $M_0$ is **inside** → **Trivial Accept** (no subdivision needed).

### Level 2C: Sub-segment $M_0(3,3)$ to $M_2(4.5, 4.5)$ — Both Inside

- Both endpoints inside → **Trivial Accept**.

### Level 2D: Sub-segment $M_2(4.5, 4.5)$ to $P_2(6, 6)$

- $M_2$ **inside**, $P_2$ **outside** (TR region).
- Midpoint $M_4$:

$$M_4 = \left( \frac{4.5+6}{2}, \frac{4.5+6}{2} \right) = (5.25, 5.25)$$

- Position test: $5.25 > 5$ → **OUTSIDE** (TR region).
- The sub-segment $M_2M_4$ has $M_2$ inside and $M_4$ outside → **recurse** on $M_2M_4$.

### Level 3: Sub-segment $M_2(4.5, 4.5)$ to $M_4(5.25, 5.25)$

- Midpoint $M_5$:

$$M_5 = \left( \frac{4.5+5.25}{2}, \frac{4.5+5.25}{2} \right) = (4.875, 4.875)$$

- Position test: $4.875 \le 5$? **YES, inside.**
- Save $M_5(4.875, 4.875)$ and subdivide further.

### Level 4 (Summary, Further Subdivisions)

Continuing this recursive halving, the boundary point on the right converges towards:

$$(5, 5)$$

Similarly, the left boundary point on the lower-left side converges towards:

$$(1, 1)$$

> The recursion stops when the segment length is less than a chosen precision $\epsilon$ (e.g., $0.001$).

### ✅ Final Result (Midpoint Subdivision):

$$\boxed{\text{Clipped line segment} \approx \overline{(1, 1)\, (5, 5)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Side-by-Side Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Cohen-Sutherland | Midpoint Subdivision |
|---|---|---|
| Outcode computation | Required (bit logic) | Not required (point-in-window only) |
| Slope/line equation | Required | Not required |
| Operations per step | Bitwise + 1 multiply | 2 additions + 1 shift |
| Recursion depth | At most 4 (one per edge) | $\lceil \log_2(L/\delta) \rceil$ |
| Hardware suitability | Software-oriented | **Parallel/Hardware ideal** |
| Floating-point divisions | 1 per clipped edge | None |
| Final answer for our example | $(1,1)$ to $(5,5)$ | $\approx (1,1)$ to $(5,5)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 9 Regions & Outcode Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows the **TBRL outcode** assigned to each of the 9 distinct regions around a rectangular clipping window. The 4-bit codes are written as `[T B R L]`.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph regionTL["Region 1001 - Top-Left"]
        regTL["T=1, B=0, R=0, L=1"]
    end
    subgraph regionT["Region 1000 - Top"]
        regT["T=1, B=0, R=0, L=0"]
    end
    subgraph regionTR["Region 1010 - Top-Right"]
        regTR["T=1, B=0, R=1, L=0"]
    end
    subgraph regionL["Region 0001 - Left"]
        regL["T=0, B=0, R=0, L=1"]
    end
    subgraph regionINSIDE["Region 0000 - INSIDE WINDOW"]
        regIN["T=0, B=0, R=0, L=0"]
    end
    subgraph regionR["Region 0010 - Right"]
        regR["T=0, B=0, R=1, L=0"]
    end
    subgraph regionBL["Region 0101 - Bottom-Left"]
        regBL["T=0, B=1, R=0, L=1"]
    end
    subgraph regionB["Region 0100 - Bottom"]
        regB["T=0, B=1, R=0, L=0"]
    end
    subgraph regionBR["Region 0110 - Bottom-Right"]
        regBR["T=0, B=1, R=1, L=0"]
    end
```

---

## 4.2 Cohen-Sutherland Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Algorithm])
    inputA[Read P1, P2 and window bounds]
    codeA1[Compute outcode C1 for P1]
    codeA2[Compute outcode C2 for P2]
    testAccept{C1 OR C2 = 0?}
    trivialAccept[Trivial Accept: Draw full line P1 to P2]
    testReject{C1 AND C2 not equal 0?}
    trivialReject[Trivial Reject: Discard line]
    pickOutside{P1 outside or P2 outside?}
    findEdge[Identify edge from leftmost set bit of outcode]
    computeInter[Compute intersection of line with chosen edge]
    updateEnd[Replace outside endpoint with intersection point]
    codeA3[Recompute outcode of updated endpoint]
    goLoop[Loop back to trivial checks]
    endA([End Algorithm])

    startA --> inputA --> codeA1 --> codeA2 --> testAccept
    testAccept -->|Yes| trivialAccept --> endA
    testAccept -->|No| testReject
    testReject -->|Yes| trivialReject --> endA
    testReject -->|No| pickOutside
    pickOutside --> findEdge --> computeInter --> updateEnd --> codeA3 --> goLoop
    goLoop --> testAccept
```

---

## 4.3 Midpoint Subdivision Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start with segment P1 to P2])
    trivialIn{Both P1 and P2 inside window?}
    acceptB[Trivial Accept: Save both as visible points]
    trivialOutRegion{Both P1 and P2 outside AND in same region?}
    rejectB[Trivial Reject: Discard sub-segment]
    findMid[Compute midpoint M of P1 to P2]
    testMid{M inside window?}
    saveMid[Save M as visible boundary point]
    subLeft[Subdivide: Recurse on P1 to M]
    subRight[Subdivide: Recurse on M to P2]
    endB([End recursion or converge])

    startB --> trivialIn
    trivialIn -->|Yes| acceptB --> endB
    trivialIn -->|No| trivialOutRegion
    trivialOutRegion -->|Yes| rejectB --> endB
    trivialOutRegion -->|No| findMid --> testMid
    testMid -->|Yes| saveMid --> subLeft
    testMid -->|No| subLeft
    subLeft --> startB
    saveMid --> subRight --> startB
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Clipping Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputStage[Input Stage]
        lineData[Line Endpoint Data x1, y1, x2, y2]
        windowData[Window Bounds xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax]
    end

    subgraph outcodeUnit[Outcode Computation Unit]
        compTop[T-bit comparator y vs ymax]
        compBot[B-bit comparator y vs ymin]
        compRight[R-bit comparator x vs xmax]
        compLeft[L-bit comparator x vs xmin]
        outcodeReg[4-bit Outcode Register TBRL]
    end

    subgraph decisionUnit[Decision Logic Unit]
        orGate[Bitwise OR gate]
        andGate[Bitwise AND gate]
        eqCheck[Equals-zero comparator]
        rejectCheck[Non-zero check for AND result]
    end

    subgraph clippingUnit[Clipping Action Unit]
        edgeSelect[Edge Selector from outcode]
        interCalc[Intersection Calculator]
        updateReg[Endpoint Update Register]
    end

    subgraph outputStage[Output Stage]
        acceptOut[Accepted clipped segment]
        rejectOut[Rejected no output]
    end

    lineData --> compTop
    lineData --> compBot
    lineData --> compRight
    lineData --> compLeft
    windowData --> compTop
    windowData --> compBot
    windowData --> compRight
    windowData --> compLeft
    compTop --> outcodeReg
    compBot --> outcodeReg
    compRight --> outcodeReg
    compLeft --> outcodeReg
    outcodeReg --> orGate
    outcodeReg --> andGate
    orGate --> eqCheck
    andGate --> rejectCheck
    eqCheck -->|Trivial Accept| acceptOut
    rejectCheck -->|Trivial Reject| rejectOut
    eqCheck -->|Partial| edgeSelect
    rejectCheck -->|Partial| edgeSelect
    edgeSelect --> interCalc
    interCalc --> updateReg
    updateReg --> outcodeReg
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
