# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

---

### Q1. Define Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm. State and explain the 4-bit region code with the help of a diagram. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm** is an efficient procedure for clipping a line segment against an axis-aligned rectangular clipping window. It uses a **4-bit region code (outcode)** assigned to each endpoint to instantly determine whether the line is entirely inside, entirely outside, or partially inside the window.

The 4 bits are arranged as **T B R L** (Top–Bottom–Right–Left), with **T as the most significant bit**:

| Bit | Set to 1 if... |
|---|---|
| T (bit 4) | $y > y_{max}$ (above the window) |
| B (bit 3) | $y < y_{min}$ (below the window) |
| R (bit 2) | $x > x_{max}$ (right of the window) |
| L (bit 1) | $x < x_{min}$ (left of the window) |

A point inside the window gets code **0000**. The algorithm performs a **bitwise OR** of the two endpoint codes — if the result is 0000, the line is trivially accepted. A **bitwise AND** — if non-zero — implies trivial rejection. Otherwise, the line is clipped at the appropriate window edge, the endpoint is updated, and the test is repeated. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for table + 1 mark for trivial accept/reject logic.)*

---

### Q2. Compare Cohen-Sutherland line clipping with Midpoint Subdivision line clipping algorithm. Mention any two advantages of the Midpoint method. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cohen-Sutherland | Midpoint Subdivision |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | Uses outcode bit-pattern for rejection/acceptance | Uses recursive midpoint + position test |
| Arithmetic | Requires slope, line equation, multiplication | Only additions and bit-shifts |
| Hardware suitability | Software-friendly | **Parallel / VLSI friendly** |
| Recursion depth | At most 4 iterations (one per edge) | $\lceil \log_2(L/\delta) \rceil$ iterations |

**Two advantages of Midpoint Subdivision:**

1. **No floating-point multiplication required** — only additions and right-shifts to compute midpoints, making it ideal for fixed-point hardware.
2. **Inherently parallelizable** — both halves of the subdivision can be processed simultaneously, allowing high-speed VLSI clipper implementations.

*(1.5 marks for comparison table + 1.5 marks for the two advantages.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks, with Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7** between two sub-parts (a) and (b). You must answer **either OR** the full set.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Q8(a,b)]`

#### (a) Explain the Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm in detail. What are the trivial acceptance and trivial rejection conditions? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**

The Cohen-Sutherland algorithm (1974) clips a line segment against an axis-aligned rectangular clipping window $W = [(x_{min}, y_{min}), (x_{max}, y_{max})]$. It works by tagging each endpoint of the line with a 4-bit **outcode** indicating its position relative to the window.

**4-Bit Outcode (2 marks):**

The bits are arranged in **TBRL** order (Top–Bottom–Right–Left). For a point $(x, y)$:

$$T = 1 \text{ if } y > y_{max}, \quad B = 1 \text{ if } y < y_{min}$$
$$R = 1 \text{ if } x > x_{max}, \quad L = 1 \text{ if } x < x_{min}$$

A point **inside** the window has code **0000**.

**Trivial Acceptance (1 mark):**

If both endpoints have code 0000, the segment is trivially accepted:

$$C_1 \text{ OR } C_2 = 0000 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Trivial Accept}$$

**Trivial Rejection (1 mark):**

If both endpoints lie on the same side of the window (i.e., share at least one "outside" bit), the segment lies entirely outside:

$$C_1 \text{ AND } C_2 \neq 0000 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Trivial Reject}$$

**Clipping Step (2 marks):**

If neither trivial condition holds, the algorithm selects an outside endpoint, identifies the appropriate window edge (from the leftmost 1-bit), finds the intersection point using the line equation, replaces the endpoint, recomputes its outcode, and **loops back** to the trivial checks.

> *Examiner's note: Students frequently lose marks by forgetting to recompute the outcode after the intersection — always **re-test after every update**.*

---

#### (b) Apply the Cohen-Sutherland line clipping algorithm to clip the line segment from $P_1(0, 0)$ to $P_2(6, 6)$ against the window defined by $x_{min} = 1$, $y_{min} = 1$, $x_{max} = 5$, $y_{max} = 5$. Show all intermediate outcodes. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Outcodes (1 mark):**

- $P_1(0, 0)$: $T=0, B=1, R=0, L=1$ → $C_1 = 0101$
- $P_2(6, 6)$: $T=1, B=0, R=1, L=0$ → $C_2 = 1010$

**Step 2 — Trivial Checks (1 mark):**

- $C_1 \text{ OR } C_2 = 1111 \neq 0000$ → Not trivial accept.
- $C_1 \text{ AND } C_2 = 0000 = 0$ → Not trivial reject.

**Step 3 — First Clip (2 marks):**

$C_1 = 0101$, leftmost 1-bit is $L$, so clip against $x = 1$. Slope $m = 1$.

$$y = 0 + 1 \cdot (1 - 0) = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; P_1' = (1, 1)$$

New outcode $C_1' = 0000$.

**Step 4 — Re-test (1 mark):**

$C_1' \text{ OR } C_2 = 0000 \text{ OR } 1010 = 1010 \neq 0000$, $C_1' \text{ AND } C_2 = 0$. Still partial.

**Step 5 — Second Clip (1 mark):**

$C_2 = 1010$, leftmost 1-bit is $R$, so clip against $x = 5$.

$$y = 0 + 1 \cdot (5 - 0) = 5 \;\Longrightarrow\; P_2' = (5, 5)$$

New outcode $C_2' = 0000$.

**Step 6 — Trivial Accept (1 mark):**

$C_1' \text{ OR } C_2' = 0000$ → Trivial Accept.

$$\boxed{\text{Clipped segment} = \overline{(1, 1)\, (5, 5)}}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q9(a,b)]`

#### (a) Explain the Midpoint Subdivision line clipping algorithm. State its advantages over Cohen-Sutherland. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):**

Midpoint Subdivision is a **divide-and-conquer** line clipping algorithm that avoids computing line equations altogether. It repeatedly halves the segment at its midpoint and uses a simple **point-in-window test** to determine whether each half is entirely outside (discard) or crosses the window (recurse).

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

1. **Trivial checks** for both endpoints:
   - Both inside → accept.
   - Both outside in the same region → reject.
2. Compute **midpoint** $M$ of the segment.
3. If $M$ is **inside** → it is part of the visible line. Save it. Recurse on both halves $P_1M$ and $MP_2$.
4. If $M$ is **outside** in the same region as both endpoints → reject the half.
5. **Stop** when segment length $\le \epsilon$ (pixel size).

**Advantages (2 marks):**

1. **No slope or line-equation computation** — only additions and bit-shifts.
2. **Ideal for parallel/hardware implementation** (e.g., in GPU rasterizers and VLSI clipper units).
3. Numerically stable for very long lines where Cohen-Sutherland's slope can overflow.

---

#### (b) Apply the Midpoint Subdivision algorithm to clip the line from $P_1(0, 0)$ to $P_2(6, 6)$ against the window $W = (1, 1, 5, 5)$. Show at least 3 levels of subdivision. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Level 0 (1 mark):**

- $P_1(0, 0)$ outside (BL), $P_2(6, 6)$ outside (TR).
- Midpoint $M_0 = (3, 3)$ — **inside window**.
- Save $M_0$; subdivide into $P_1M_0$ and $M_0P_2$.

**Level 1 (2 marks):**

- Left half $P_1M_0$: midpoint $M_1 = (1.5, 1.5)$ — **inside**. Save. Subdivide.
- Right half $M_0P_2$: midpoint $M_2 = (4.5, 4.5)$ — **inside**. Save. Subdivide.

**Level 2 (2 marks):**

- $P_1(0, 0)$ to $M_1(1.5, 1.5)$: midpoint $M_3 = (0.75, 0.75)$ — outside BL (same region) → **Trivial Reject**.
- $M_1(1.5, 1.5)$ to $M_0(3, 3)$: both inside → **Trivial Accept**.
- $M_0(3, 3)$ to $M_2(4.5, 4.5)$: both inside → **Trivial Accept**.
- $M_2(4.5, 4.5)$ to $P_2(6, 6)$: midpoint $M_4 = (5.25, 5.25)$ — outside TR; recurse on $M_2M_4$.

**Level 3 (1 mark):**

- $M_2(4.5, 4.5)$ to $M_4(5.25, 5.25)$: midpoint $M_5 = (4.875, 4.875)$ — **inside**. Recurse further.
- By Level 4, the right boundary point converges to $(5, 5)$.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\text{Clipped segment} \approx \overline{(1, 1)\, (5, 5)}}$$

> *Examiner's note: Students often forget to do a final convergence check — always state explicitly that further subdivision yields $(1,1)$ and $(5,5)$ as the limiting boundary points.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Cohen-Sutherland problems:**
>
> 1. **Reversed bit order (LRBT instead of TBRL):** If you write bits in LRBT order, the binary codes become wrong, and the entire solution collapses. **Always use TBRL, with T as MSB.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to recompute the outcode** after each intersection update. The algorithm is iterative — every clip must be followed by a fresh outcode calculation and a fresh OR/AND test.
>
> 3. **Using wrong edge-selection rule:** Some students pick the first 1-bit arbitrarily. The convention is the **leftmost 1-bit** (lowest-significant 1-bit) of the endpoint's outcode — i.e., L, then R, then B, then T, in that priority.
>
> 4. **Slope-zero / vertical-line special case:** When $x_1 = x_2$, slope $m$ is undefined. The examiner may deduct a full mark if you divide by zero. Use the inverse equation $x = x_1 + (y - y_1)/m$ for vertical-line handling.
>
> 5. **For Midpoint Subdivision, students often confuse "both outside same region" with "anywhere outside":** The trivial reject condition requires **same outcode region**, not just "both outside." If endpoints are in different outside regions (e.g., one in TR, one in BL), the line *must* cross the window, so it is NOT trivially rejected.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### Cohen-Sutherland — Key Takeaways
- ✅ **4-bit outcode** in **T-B-R-L** order (T is MSB).
- ✅ Bitwise **OR = 0** → Trivial **Accept**.
- ✅ Bitwise **AND ≠ 0** → Trivial **Reject**.
- ✅ Otherwise, pick outside endpoint, find intersection with the **edge indicated by the leftmost 1-bit**, replace endpoint, **recompute** outcode, **loop**.
- ✅ Maximum 4 clipping iterations (one per window edge).

### Midpoint Subdivision — Key Takeaways
- ✅ **No line equation needed** — only midpoint + position test.
- ✅ **Divide-and-conquer** with at most $\lceil \log_2(L/\delta) \rceil$ recursions.
- ✅ Trivial reject requires **same outside region** for both endpoints.
- ✅ Midpoint inside → save and recurse on both halves.
- ✅ **Hardware-friendly**: ideal for parallel VLSI / GPU rasterization.

### Universal Points
- 🎯 Both algorithms handle **all three cases** (accept, reject, partial) using only **constant-time tests**.
- 🎯 Cohen-Sutherland is **faster for simple cases**; Midpoint is **faster in hardware for complex/long lines**.
- 🎯 Both are **exam-favorites** — expect a 3-mark Part A question and a 7-mark sub-part in Part B every semester.
- 🎯 Memorize the **TBRL bit assignment table** and the **trivial accept/reject conditions** verbatim — these alone fetch 2–3 marks.
- 🎯 Always show **all intermediate outcodes** in numerical Cohen-Sutherland problems — omitting them is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
- 🎯 When slope $m$ is undefined (vertical line) or zero (horizontal line), use the **alternate parametric form** to avoid division-by-zero errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sutherland Hodgeman and Weiler Atherton Polygon Clipping Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST835 - COMPUTER GRAPHICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sutherland Hodgeman and Weiler Atherton Polygon Clipping Algorithms # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Polygon Clipping? **Polygon Clipping** is the process of removing portions of a polygon that lie **outside** a specified region of the display area, known as the **clipping window** (or clip region). The result of clipping is one or more polygons that represent the intersection of the original polygon with the clipping region. Unlike line clipping (which is solved using algorithms like Cohen–Sutherland, Liang–Barsky, or Nicholl–Lee–Nicholls), polygon clipping must preserve the **topology, connectivity, and fill properties** of the original shape. This makes it significantly more complex. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):** > *"Polygon clipping is the process of cutting away portions of a polygon that lie outside a defined region (clipping window) so that only the visible portion is retained for display/rendering."* --- ## 1.2 The Two Algorithms at a Glance | Algorithm | Best Suited For | Clipping Region | Polygon Type | Strategy | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Sutherland–Hodgeman** | Convex clip regions | Convex (rectangle, hexagon, etc.) | **Convex** polygons (only) | Sequential edge clipping (Divide & Conquer) | | **Weiler–Atherton** | General cases | Convex or **Concave** | Convex or **Concave** polygons | Vertex classification + boundary traversal | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ### 🍪 The Cookie Cutter Analogy (Sutherland–Hodgeman) Imagine you have a **dough in the shape of a large polygon** spread on a baking tray. The **clipping window** is your **cookie cutter** (a rectangular frame). You press the cutter down, and the dough outside the cutter is **scraped away**. You are left with only the portion that fits inside the rectangular boundary. This is exactly what Sutherland–Hodgeman does — it **scrapes away** the polygon piece by piece against each of the four rectangular edges (Left, Right, Bottom, Top), one edge at a time. ### 🧩 The Jigsaw Puzzle Analogy (Weiler–Atherton) Now suppose your **dough is shaped like a star** (concave polygon) and your **cookie cutter is also a star** (concave clip region). The "scraping" approach fails because concave shapes have inward dents. Weiler–Atherton works like a **jigsaw puzzle** — it identifies **entry and exit points** where the polygon boundary crosses the clip region boundary, and **traces** the new combined outline step-by-step to construct a clean, closed final polygon. --- ## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Must-Know) > [!NOTE] > **Inside Test:** Given a vertex, a fast method to determine if it lies inside the clip region. For a rectangular window, the test is: > - $Inside = (x \geq x_{min}) \land (x \leq x_{max}) \land (y \geq y_{min}) \land (y \leq y_{max})$ > - The inequality direction **flips** depending on the edge being processed (left/right/top/bottom). > [!NOTE] > **Subject Polygon:** The polygon to be clipped (input). > > **Clip Polygon:** The polygon defining the clipping region (window). > [!NOTE] > **Vertex Classification:** Each vertex of the polygon is classified as: > - **IN** → lies strictly inside the clip region > - **OUT** → lies strictly outside the clip region > > This classification is performed against **each edge** of the clip region separately in Sutherland–Hodgeman. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sutherland–Hodgeman Polygon Clipping Algorithm ### 📘 Background Proposed by **Ivan Sutherland** and **Gary Hodgeman** in **1974**, this algorithm uses a **divide-and-conquer** strategy. The polygon is clipped **one edge of the clipping window at a time**. The output polygon from clipping against one edge becomes the **input polygon** for clipping against the next edge. ### 📐 Operational Logic (4 Stages for a Rectangular Window) For a rectangular clipping window, the algorithm processes **four boundaries** sequentially: | Stage | Boundary | Retained Half-Plane | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Left** edge ($x = x_{min}$) | $x \geq x_{min}$ | | 2 | **Right** edge ($x = x_{max}$) | $x \leq x_{max}$ | | 3 | **Bottom** edge ($y = y_{min}$) | $y \geq y_{min}$ | | 4 | **Top** edge ($y = y_{max}$) | $y \leq y_{max}$ | ### 🔍 Per-Edge Rule (Core Logic) For each edge of the clip window, the algorithm walks around the polygon's vertices in order. For each **consecutive pair** of vertices (a "spoke" of the polygon), the following four cases are possible: | Case | Vertex $S$ (Start) | Vertex $P$ (End) | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | OUT | OUT | **Add nothing** to the output list | | 2 | OUT | IN | **Compute intersection** $I$, add $I$ to output list | | 3 | IN | IN | **Add $P$** to output list | | 4 | IN | OUT | **Add $P$** and **compute intersection** $I$, add $I$ to output list | The process is repeated for the **last vertex to the first vertex** to close the polygon. ### 🧮 Intersection Computation (Parametric Line) Given a line segment from $S = (x_s, y_s)$ to $P = (x_p, y_p)$ crossing a clip boundary: $$ t = \frac{x_{clip} - x_s}{x_p - x_s} $$ $$ y_I = y_s + t \cdot (y_p - y_s) $$ For vertical/horizontal edges, $t$ is computed using the corresponding coordinate. ### ⚠️ Limitations of Sutherland–Hodgeman > [!WARNING] > - ❌ Works **only for convex** clipping regions. > - ❌ Works **only for convex** polygons being clipped (will fail for concave polygons). > - ❌ Produces **redundant edges** when concave polygons are clipped against a rectangular window (the output may not close properly because every "spoke" generates a new edge, even when the spoke is partially inside and the inside section forms a straight line). > - ❌ For **self-intersecting polygons**, the result is **undefined**. ### ✅ Advantages - ✅ **Simple** to implement. - ✅ **Efficient** ($O(n \cdot m)$ where $n$ = number of polygon vertices, $m$ = number of clip region edges). - ✅ **Pipeline-friendly** (each stage's output is the next stage's input). - ✅ **Hardware-suitable** (used in many early graphics systems like the SGI Geometry Engine). ### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility Sutherland–Hodgeman is heavily used in: - **CAD/CAM systems** for design cropping. - **GIS (Geographic Information Systems)** for map overlay operations. - **2D game engines** for visibility culling of UI elements. - **SVG and PostScript renderers** for vector graphics cropping. --- ## 2.2 Weiler–Atherton Polygon Clipping Algorithm ### 📘 Background Proposed by **Kevin Weiler** and **Peter Atherton** in **1977**, this is a **general-purpose polygon clipping algorithm** that can handle **both convex and concave polygons** being clipped against **convex or concave clip regions**. ### 📐 Core Strategy Instead of sequentially clipping against each edge, Weiler–Atherton: 1. Builds a **linked-list representation** of both polygons. 2. Marks each vertex as **IN** or **OUT**. 3. Computes **all intersection points** between the two polygon boundaries. 4. **Inserts** intersection points into both lists (in sorted order). 5. **Traverses** the lists in a specific pattern to construct closed, non-intersecting output polygons. ### 🧩 The Four Key Phases | Phase | Name | Operation | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Setup** | Build linked lists; mark IN/OUT status of every vertex | | 2 | **Intersection** | Find all intersections; insert into both lists in order | | 3 | **Entry/Exit Tagging** | For each intersection, mark it as **ENTRY** (OUT→IN) or **EXIT** (IN→OUT) | | 4 | **Traversal** | Follow subject boundary from ENTRY → next EXIT, then follow clip boundary backward to next ENTRY, repeat | ### 🔁 The Clipping Loop (Pseudocode Logic) 1. Start at an unprocessed **ENTRY** point on the subject polygon. 2. **Follow the subject polygon boundary** in order, adding vertices to the output, until an **EXIT** point is reached. 3. **Switch to the clip polygon boundary** at the EXIT point. 4. **Follow the clip polygon boundary in reverse**, adding vertices, until the next **ENTRY** point is reached. 5. **Switch back** to the subject polygon boundary. 6. **Repeat** until all ENTRY points are processed. ### 🧮 Parametric Intersection (Same as Sutherland–Hodgeman) For each edge of the subject polygon, check intersection with each edge of the clip polygon: $$ t = \frac{x_{clip} - x_s}{x_p - x_s}, \qquad y_I = y_s + t \cdot (y_p - y_s) $$ Only intersections where $0 \leq t \leq 1$ are valid. ### ✅ Advantages - ✅ Handles **concave** polygons AND **concave** clip regions. - ✅ Produces **correctly closed** output polygons. - ✅ Handles **multiple disjoint output polygons** (e.g., one concave clip region can produce several clipped regions). - ✅ Handles **holes** in polygons (polygons with holes via even-odd rule). ### ⚠️ Limitations - ❌ More **complex to implement**. - ❌ Slower ($O(n \cdot m)$ worst case, but with higher constant factors). - ❌ Requires careful handling of **tangent cases** (where the polygon just grazes the clip region boundary). - ❌ Numerical precision issues at intersection points. ### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility Weiler–Atherton is used in: - **Advanced CAD systems** with complex boundary shapes. - **Cartography** for clipping irregular geographic regions. - **3D printing slicer software** for generating toolpaths. - **Computer vision** for object silhouette extraction. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet > [!NOTE] > **MASTER REFERENCE — Print This Page Before Exam** | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Inside Test (Rectangular)** | $x_{min} \le x \le x_{max} \;\land\; y_{min} \le y \le y_{max}$ | IN/OUT classification | | 2 | **Parametric Edge Parameter** | $t = (x_{clip} - x_s)/(x_p - x_s)$ | Find intersection on segment | | 3 | **Y-Coordinate of Intersection** | $y_I = y_s + t \cdot (y_p - y_s)$ | Required to output vertex | | 4 | **S-H Complexity** | $O(n \cdot m)$ | $n$ = polygon vertices, $m$ = clip edges | | 5 | **S-H Constraint** | Polygon AND clip region must be **convex** | Limitation check | | 6 | **W-A Step Rule** | Subject $\to$ Exit $\to$ Clip reverse $\to$ Entry | Traversal pattern | | 7 | **W-A Constraint** | Works for **concave–concave** | General case | | 8 | **Boundary Edge Cases** | Tangent, coincident, zero-length | Numerical guard checks | | 9 | **Closed Loop Check** | First vertex $\equiv$ last vertex | Validation post-clip | | 10 | **Vertex Output Table** | 4 cases (OUT-OUT, OUT-IN, IN-IN, IN-OUT) | S-H per-edge logic | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Sutherland–Hodgeman: Full Worked Example ### 📋 Problem Setup Clip the polygon with vertices $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ against a rectangular clipping window with $x_{min}=2$, $x_{max}=6$, $y_{min}=2$, $y_{max}=5$. **Input polygon vertices (in order):** - $P_1 = (1, 2)$ - $P_2 = (5, 1)$ - $P_3 = (7, 4)$ - $P_4 = (3, 6)$ --- ### ✂️ STAGE 1: Clipping Against LEFT Edge ($x = 2$, retain $x \ge 2$) **Inside Test:** Vertex is IN if $x \ge 2$. | Edge (S → P) | $x_s$ | $x_p$ | Status S | Status P | Action | New Vertices | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $P_1 \to P_2$ | 1 | 5 | OUT | IN | Compute $I$ | $I_{12} = (2,\ 2.5)$ | | $P_2 \to P_3$ | 5 | 7 | IN | IN | Add $P$ | $P_2$ | | $P_3 \to P_4$ | 7 | 3 | IN | OUT | Add $P$ + $I$ | $P_3,\ I_{34} = (2,\ 5.5)$ | | $P_4 \to P_1$ | 3 | 1 | IN | OUT | Add $P$ + $I$ | $P_4,\ I_{41} = (2,\ 2.5)$ | **Intersection calculation for $P_1 \to P_2$:** $$ t = \frac{2 - 1}{5 - 1} = \frac{1}{4} $$ $$ y_I = 2 + \frac{1}{4} \cdot (1 - 2) = 2 - 0.25 = 1.75 $$ Wait, $P_1 = (1,2)$, $P_2 = (5,1)$, so $y_s = 2$, $y_p = 1$: $$ y_I = 2 + \frac{1}{4} \cdot (1 - 2) = 2 - 0.25 = 1.75 $$ **Corrected intersection $I_{12} = (2,\ 1.75)$** **Stage 1 Output (in order):** $$I_{12} = (2,\ 1.75),\ P_2 = (5,\ 1),\ P_3 = (7,\ 4),\ I_{34} = (2,\ 5.5),\ P_4 = (3,\ 6),\ I_{41} = (2,\ 2.5)$$ (Note: $I_{12}$ falls below $y_{min} = 2$, but is still retained as a candidate for the next stage — it will be removed in Stage 3.) --- ### ✂️ STAGE 2: Clipping Against RIGHT Edge ($x = 6$, retain $x \le 6$) **Inside Test:** Vertex is IN if $x \le 6$. Walking around the Stage 1 output list, plus the closing edge back to the first vertex: | Edge (S → P) | Status S | Status P | Action | New Vertices | |---|---|---|---|---| | $I_{12} \to P_2$ | $(2,1.75)$ IN | $(5,1)$ IN | Add $P$ | $I_{12}$ | | $P_2 \to P_3$ | $(5,1)$ IN | $(7,4)$ OUT | Add $P$ + $I$ | $P_2,\ I_{23} = (6,\ 1.75)$ | | $P_3 \to I_{34}$ | $(7,4)$ OUT | $(2,5.5)$ IN | Add $I$ | $I_{34}$ | | $I_{34} \to P_4$ | IN | IN | Add $P$ | $P_4$ | | $P_4 \to I_{41}$ | IN | $(2,2.5)$ IN | Add $P$ | $I_{41}$ | | $I_{41} \to I_{12}$ (close) | IN | IN | Add $P$ | (loop complete) | **Stage 2 Output (in order):** $$(2, 1.75),\ (5, 1),\ (6, 1.75),\ (2, 5.5),\ (3, 6),\ (2, 2.5)$$ --- ### ✂️ STAGE 3: Clipping Against BOTTOM Edge ($y = 2$, retain $y \ge 2$) **Inside Test:** Vertex is IN if $y \ge 2$. | Edge (S → P) | Status S | Status P | Action | New Vertices | |---|---|---|---|---| | $(2,1.75) \to (5,1)$ | OUT | OUT | Nothing | – | | $(5,1) \to (6,1.75)$ | OUT | OUT | Nothing | – | | $(6,1.75) \to (2,5.5)$ | OUT | IN | Add $I$ | $I_{1} = (5.5,\ 2)$ | | $(2,5.5) \to (3,6)$ | IN | IN | Add $P$ | $(2,5.5)$ | | $(3,6) \to (2,2.5)$ | IN | IN | Add $P$ | $(3,6)$ | | $(2,2.5) \to (2,1.75)$ (close) | IN | OUT | Add $P$ + $I$ | $(2,2.5),\ I_{2} = (2,\ 2)$ | **Intersection $(6, 1.75) \to (2, 5.5)$ with $y = 2$:** $$ t = \frac{2 - 1.75}{5.5 - 1.75} = \frac{0.25}{3.75} = \frac{1}{15} $$ $$ x_I = 6 + \frac{1}{15} \cdot (2 - 6) = 6 - \frac{4}{15} = \frac{86}{15} \approx 5.733 $$ **Refined $I_1 = (5.733,\ 2)$** **Stage 3 Output (in order):** $$(5.733, 2),\ (2, 5.5),\ (3, 6),\ (2, 2.5),\ (2, 2)$$ --- ### ✂️ STAGE 4: Clipping Against TOP Edge ($y = 5$, retain $y \le 5$) **Inside Test:** Vertex is IN if $y \le 5$. | Edge (S → P) | Status S | Status P | Action | New Vertices | |---|---|---|---|---| | $(5.733,2) \to (2,5.5)$ | IN | OUT | Add $P$ + $I$ | $(5.733,2),\ I = (4.529,\ 5)$ | | $(2,5.5) \to (3,6)$ | OUT | OUT | Nothing | – | | $(3,6) \to (2,2.5)$ | OUT | IN | Add $I$ | $I = (2.286,\ 5)$ | | $(2,2.5) \to (2,2)$ | IN | IN | Add $P$ | $(2,2.5)$ | | $(2,2) \to (5.733,2)$ (close) | IN | IN | Add $P$ | $(2,2)$ | **Final Clipped Polygon (vertices in order):** $$(5.733,\ 2),\ (4.529,\ 5),\ (2.286,\ 5),\ (2,\ 2.5),\ (2,\ 2)$$ This pentagon is the **visible portion** of the original polygon inside the clipping window. --- ## 3.2 Weiler–Atherton: Full Worked Example ### 📋 Problem Setup A **concave** subject polygon $S$ must be clipped against a **convex** clip region $C$ (rectangle). **Subject polygon $S$ vertices (clockwise):** $$S_1 = (2, 1),\ S_2 = (5, 1),\ S_3 = (4, 3),\ S_4 = (6, 3),\ S_5 = (6, 6),\ S_6 = (1, 6)$$ (Note: the indentation at $S_3$–$S_4$ makes this **concave**.) **Clip polygon $C$ vertices (counter-clockwise, inward-normal):** $$C_1 = (2, 2),\ C_2 = (5, 2),\ C_3 = (5, 5),\ C_4 = (2, 5)$$ ### 🪜 Step-by-Step Execution #### **Phase 1: Setup — IN/OUT Classification** For each $S_i$, check if it lies inside $C$ using: $x_{min}=2,\ x_{max}=5,\ y_{min}=2,\ y_{max}=5$. | Vertex | Coordinates | Status | |---|---|---| | $S_1$ | $(2, 1)$ | OUT (below) | | $S_2$ | $(5, 1)$ | OUT (below) | | $S_3$ | $(4, 3)$ | **IN** | | $S_4$ | $(6, 3)$ | OUT (right) | | $S_5$ | $(6, 6)$ | OUT (right + top) | | $S_6$ | $(1, 6)$ | OUT (left + top) | #### **Phase 2: Intersection Computation** Compute intersections between subject edges and clip edges. **Edge $S_1 \to S_2$ (line $y=1$) with Bottom clip edge $C_1 \to C_2$ (line $y=2$):** Parallel — no intersection. **Edge $S_1 \to S_2$ (line $y=1$) with Top clip edge $C_3 \to C_4$ (line $y=5$):** Parallel — no intersection. **Edge $S_1 \to S_2$ (line $y=1$) with Left clip edge $C_4 \to C_1$ (line $x=2$):** $$ t = \frac{2 - 2}{5 - 2} = 0 $$ Intersection at $S_1$ itself — degenerate (vertex on boundary). **Edge $S_2 \to S_3$ with Right clip edge $x=5$:** $$ t = \frac{5 - 5}{4 - 5} = \frac{0}{-1} = 0 $$ At $S_2$ — degenerate. Treated as a tangent case. **Edge $S_2 \to S_3$ with Bottom clip edge $y=2$:** Parametric from $(5,1)$ to $(4,3)$: $$ t = \frac{2 - 1}{3 - 1} = \frac{1}{2} $$ $$ x = 5 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot (4 - 5) = 5 - 0.5 = 4.5 $$ **Intersection $I_1 = (4.5, 2)$** **Edge $S_3 \to S_4$ with Right clip edge $x=5$:** From $(4,3)$ to $(6,3)$: $$ t = \frac{5 - 4}{6 - 4} = 0.5 $$ **Intersection $I_2 = (5, 3)$** **Edge $S_4 \to S_5$ with Right clip edge $x=5$:** At $S_4 = (6,3)$ — outside, no useful intersection. **Edge $S_5 \to S_6$ with Top clip edge $y=5$:** From $(6,6)$ to $(1,6)$: line $y=6$ is parallel to $y=5$ — no intersection. **Edge $S_6 \to S_1$ with Left clip edge $x=2$:** From $(1,6)$ to $(2,1)$: $$ t = \frac{2 - 1}{2 - 1} = 1 $$ At $S_1$ — degenerate. **Edge $S_6 \to S_1$ with Top clip edge $y=5$:** From $(1,6)$ to $(2,1)$: $$ t = \frac{5 - 6}{1 - 6} = \frac{-1}{-5} = 0.2 $$ $$ x = 1 + 0.2 \cdot (2 - 1) = 1.2 $$ **Intersection $I_3 = (1.2, 5)$** #### **Phase 3: ENTRY / EXIT Tagging** Following the subject polygon in order: | Vertex/Intersection | Position | Direction | Tag | |---|---|---|---| | $S_1 = (2, 1)$ | OUT | Start | – | | $I_1 = (4.5, 2)$ | boundary | OUT → IN | **ENTRY** | | $S_3 = (4, 3)$ | IN | – | – | | $I_2 = (5, 3)$ | boundary | IN → OUT | **EXIT** | | $S_4, S_5, S_6$ | OUT | – | – | | $I_3 = (1.2, 5)$ | boundary | OUT → IN | **ENTRY** | | $S_1 = (2, 1)$ | OUT | loop complete | – | #### **Phase 4: Boundary Traversal** **Loop 1: Start at $I_1$ (ENTRY)** - Follow subject boundary: $I_1 \to S_3 \to I_2$. - At $I_2$ (EXIT), switch to clip boundary. - Follow clip boundary **in reverse**: $I_2$ corresponds to right clip edge between $C_2$ and $C_3$. The previous ENTRY $I_1$ corresponds to bottom clip edge between $C_1$ and $C_2$. - Traverse clip boundary in reverse: $I_2 \to C_3 \to C_4 \to I_1$. **Output Polygon 1:** $I_1 \to S_3 \to I_2 \to C_3 \to C_4 \to I_1$ Expanded: $(4.5, 2) \to (4, 3) \to (5, 3) \to (5, 5) \to (2, 5) \to (4.5, 2)$ **Loop 2: Start at $I_3$ (ENTRY)** - Follow subject boundary: $I_3 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to I_1$. - At $I_1$ (EXIT — but $I_1$ was already used as ENTRY in Loop 1, so this is actually the second traversal). - Switch to clip boundary in reverse. **Output Polygon 2:** $I_3 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to I_1 \to C_1 \to I_3$ Expanded: $(1.2, 5) \to (2, 1) \to (5, 1) \to (4.5, 2) \to (2, 2) \to (1.2, 5)$ **Final Output:** Two polygons correctly partitioning the visible region. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Sutherland–Hodgeman | Weiler–Atherton | |---|---|---| | **Time Complexity** | $O(n \cdot m)$ | $O(n \cdot m)$ with higher constant | | **Space Complexity** | $O(n)$ | $O(n + m + k)$ where $k$ = intersections | | **Convex Subject** | ✅ Works | ✅ Works | | **Concave Subject** | ❌ Fails (redundant edges) | ✅ Works | | **Convex Clip** | ✅ Works | ✅ Works | | **Concave Clip** | ❌ Fails | ✅ Works | | **Hole Support** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (even-odd rule) | | **Multiple Outputs** | ❌ One polygon (may be degenerate) | ✅ Yes | | **Implementation Difficulty** | Low | High | | **Numerical Stability** | High | Moderate (needs epsilon checks) | | **Pipeline Suitability** | Excellent | Poor | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sutherland–Hodgeman High-Level Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA([Start]) --> nodeB[Read Subject Polygon Vertices
