> The A-Buffer, on the other hand, is like a careful student who keeps **all the sheets in a pile, ordered by height**, and then carefully projects them layer by layer, blending the colors based on how transparent each sheet is. The result is a much richer image where you can see a hint of objects behind glass, or the soft edge of a leaf against the sky (anti-aliasing).

## 1.3 Origin and Historical Context

- Invented by **Loren Carpenter** in **1984** at Lucasfilm Computer Graphics Project.
- Designed to overcome two specific weaknesses of the Z-Buffer:
  1. Inability to render **transparent** (alpha-blended) surfaces.
  2. Inability to perform proper **anti-aliasing** of edges and textures.
- Often described in classical texts (Foley, van Dam, Feiner, Hughes — *Computer Graphics: Principles and Practice*).

## 1.4 Why A-Buffer is Important — The "Why" Question

| Weakness of Z-Buffer | How A-Buffer Solves It |
|---|---|
| Stores only ONE depth per pixel | Stores a LIST of depths per pixel |
| Cannot handle transparency | Maintains a per-fragment $\alpha$ (coverage/opacity) |
| Edges are jagged ("jaggies") | Computes sub-pixel coverage areas |
| No surface identity preserved | Stores full surface attributes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** Coverage value $\alpha$ is bounded as $0 \le \alpha \le 1$. An $\alpha = 1$ means the surface is **fully opaque**; $\alpha = 0$ means **fully transparent** (invisible). This is the same convention used in modern **RGBA color** systems and PNG image formats.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Per-Pixel Data Structure — The Heart of the Algorithm

Unlike the Z-Buffer which is a 2D array of single floats, the A-Buffer is a **2D array of linked-list heads**. Each element of this 2D array, denoted $\text{ABuf}[x][y]$, points to the first node of a chain of *fragment records* (buckets).

Each **fragment record** (bucket) is a C-style struct of the form:

$$
\text{struct Fragment} = \Big\{\ \text{depth}: z,\ \text{color}: I,\ \text{coverage}: \alpha,\ \text{next}: \text{Fragment}^*,\ \text{flag}: \text{bool}\ \Big\}
$$

There are two states (encoded by `flag`):

1. **Resolved flag = TRUE**: The bucket contains a single opaque surface, so no further blending is required. Treated like a Z-Buffer cell.
2. **Resolved flag = FALSE**: The bucket contains a single (possibly transparent) surface, but more surfaces may exist behind it; the list is open for appending.

## 2.2 Operational Steps of the Algorithm

The A-Buffer works by processing every polygon (or surface primitive) in the scene, one at a time, in **arbitrary order** (no painter's sort needed):

1. **Rasterize** the current polygon into the pixel grid.
2. For each pixel $(x,y)$ touched by the polygon:
   a. Compute the polygon's depth $z$ at that pixel (interpolated from vertex depths).
   b. Compute the **coverage area** $\alpha$ of the polygon over the pixel (sub-pixel mask).
   c. Examine $\text{ABuf}[x][y]$:
      - If the list is **empty** (no fragment yet), create a new bucket with $\alpha = 1$ and the polygon's color and depth, mark `flag = TRUE`.
      - If the list contains a single fragment with `flag = TRUE` and the incoming polygon's $z$ is **in front of** the existing depth $z_{\text{old}}$, overwrite the bucket.
      - Otherwise, **append** a new fragment node to the linked list with its $z$, $I$, and $\alpha$.
3. After **all polygons are processed**, perform a **resolve pass**:
   - Walk each pixel's linked list front-to-back (or back-to-front).
   - Combine colors using the $\alpha$-blending equation.
4. The final blended color $C_{\text{final}}$ at pixel $(x,y)$ is written to the **frame buffer**.

## 2.3 The Blending Equation (Resolve Pass)

When resolving a pixel whose list has $k$ fragments sorted in back-to-front order $z_1 > z_2 > \dots > z_k$ (closest last), the composite color is:

$$
C_{\text{final}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \alpha_i \cdot I_i \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}(1 - \alpha_j)
$$

In expanded form (this is the form examiners love to ask):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{final}} &= \alpha_1 \cdot I_1 \\
&\quad + (1 - \alpha_1) \cdot \alpha_2 \cdot I_2 \\
&\quad + (1 - \alpha_1)(1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \alpha_3 \cdot I_3 \\
&\quad + \cdots \\
&\quad + \left[\prod_{j=1}^{k-1}(1 - \alpha_j)\right] \cdot \alpha_k \cdot I_k
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Math:** The term $\prod_{j=1}^{i-1}(1 - \alpha_j)$ represents the **light that has not yet been absorbed** by any of the layers in front of fragment $i$. It is the *transmittance* of the stack above $i$. This is a direct application of **Beer-Lambert attenuation** from physics, repurposed for graphics.

## 2.4 Coverage Calculation

For anti-aliased rendering, the coverage $\alpha_i$ is the fraction of the pixel's area covered by polygon $i$. If a pixel is divided into an $N \times N$ sample grid (e.g., $N = 4$ for $4\times4$ super-sampling), then:

$$
\alpha_i = \frac{n_{\text{hit}}}{N^2}
$$

where $n_{\text{hit}}$ is the number of sub-samples inside polygon $i$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $C_{\text{final}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \alpha_i I_i \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}(1-\alpha_j)$ | Compositing of $k$ fragments back-to-front | **Resolve pass** |
| 2 | $\alpha_i = n_{\text{hit}} / N^2$ | Sub-pixel coverage of polygon $i$ | Anti-aliasing |
| 3 | $0 \le \alpha_i \le 1$ | Opacity range | All blending |
| 4 | $T_i = \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}(1-\alpha_j)$ | Transmittance of stack above $i$ | Compositing |
| 5 | $M_{\text{ABuf}} = N_x \cdot N_y \cdot (\text{sizeof header} + k \cdot \text{sizeof frag})$ | Memory of A-Buffer | Complexity analysis |
| 6 | $\text{Time} = O(P \cdot A + N_x N_y \cdot k_{\text{avg}})$ | Per-frame cost | Performance |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical imaging (CT/MRI volume rendering):** Each slice is a transparent layer; A-Buffer principles yield correct blending.
- **Automotive heads-up displays (HUD):** Multiple semitransparent icons stacked on the road view.
- **Film VFX (e.g., Pixar RenderMan, mental ray):** Production renderers use A-Buffer-like accumulation for volumetrics and hair.
- **Scientific visualization:** Cloud, smoke, and atmospheric scattering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Compositing Equation (Back-to-Front)

We want to derive the back-to-front compositing operator used in the resolve pass of the A-Buffer.

**Setup:** Consider a single pixel hit by $k$ overlapping surfaces. Sort them so the farthest from the viewer is fragment 1, and the closest to the viewer is fragment $k$. Denote:

- $C_i$ — intrinsic color of fragment $i$ (the color it would show if seen alone).
- $\alpha_i$ — opacity of fragment $i$ ($1 - \alpha_i$ = transparency).

**Step 1 — Write down what fragment 1 contributes.**
Fragment 1 is the farthest, so nothing covers it from behind in our stack. The light from fragment 1 that reaches the viewer is simply its own color attenuated by its own opacity:

$$
C_1^{\text{out}} = \alpha_1 \cdot C_1
$$

**Step 2 — Add fragment 2 on top.**
Fragment 2 lets through a fraction $(1 - \alpha_2)$ of whatever is behind it, and contributes $\alpha_2 C_2$ of its own color. So the partial output up to fragment 2 is:

$$
C_{1,2}^{\text{out}} = \alpha_2 \cdot C_2 + (1 - \alpha_2) \cdot C_1^{\text{out}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression from Step 1:**

$$
C_{1,2}^{\text{out}} = \alpha_2 C_2 + (1 - \alpha_2) \alpha_1 C_1
$$

**Step 4 — Inductively add fragment 3.**
The pattern that emerges is: each fragment $i$ adds $\alpha_i C_i$ and multiplies the cumulative stack from behind by $(1 - \alpha_i)$.

$$
C_{1,2,3}^{\text{out}} = \alpha_3 C_3 + (1 - \alpha_3)\big[\alpha_2 C_2 + (1-\alpha_2)\alpha_1 C_1\big]
$$

**Step 5 — Expand fully:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1,2,3}^{\text{out}} &= \alpha_3 C_3 \\
&\quad + (1-\alpha_3)\alpha_2 C_2 \\
&\quad + (1-\alpha_3)(1-\alpha_2)\alpha_1 C_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Generalize to $k$ fragments:**
Read off the pattern: the coefficient of $C_i$ is the product of all $(1 - \alpha_j)$ for the layers $j$ that are *in front of* $i$, multiplied by $\alpha_i$. Thus:

$$
\boxed{\,C_{\text{final}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \alpha_i C_i \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}(1 - \alpha_j)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention used:** In the formula, fragment index $i = 1$ is the *farthest* and $i = k$ is the *closest*. The product is **empty (equals 1)** when $i = 1$, so the first term is simply $\alpha_1 C_1$, matching Step 1.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Three Overlapping Pixels

**Problem:** A pixel is hit by three surfaces, processed back-to-front:

| Fragment $i$ | Color $C_i$ | Opacity $\alpha_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (farthest) | Red, $C_1 = 1.0$ | $\alpha_1 = 0.5$ |
| 2 (middle)   | Green, $C_2 = 1.0$ | $\alpha_2 = 0.4$ |
| 3 (closest)  | Blue, $C_3 = 1.0$ | $\alpha_3 = 0.6$ |

**Compute the final composited color.**

**Step 1 — Compute transmittance terms.**

- $T_1 = \prod_{j=1}^{0}(\cdot) = 1$ (empty product, by convention).
- $T_2 = (1 - \alpha_1) = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$.
- $T_3 = (1 - \alpha_1)(1 - \alpha_2) = 0.5 \times (1 - 0.4) = 0.5 \times 0.6 = 0.3$.

**Step 2 — Compute each weighted contribution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_1 &= \alpha_1 \cdot C_1 \cdot T_1 = 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 0.5 \\
\text{Term}_2 &= \alpha_2 \cdot C_2 \cdot T_2 = 0.4 \times 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.2 \\
\text{Term}_3 &= \alpha_3 \cdot C_3 \cdot T_3 = 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 0.3 = 0.18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum.**

$$
C_{\text{final}} = 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.18 = 0.88
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check.**
Total contribution must be $\le 1$. Here $C_{\text{final}} = 0.88 \le 1$. ✓

The final pixel is **88% of full brightness**, dominated by red (farthest layer, biggest single contribution), with smaller but visible green and blue tints — exactly what back-to-front compositing should produce.

## 3.3 Memory & Time Complexity Matrix

Let $N_x \times N_y$ be the resolution, $P$ the number of polygons, and $A$ the average polygon area in pixels. Let $k_{\text{avg}}$ be the average number of fragments per pixel.

| Resource | Z-Buffer | A-Buffer |
|---|---|---|
| Per-pixel storage | 1 depth value (e.g., **4 bytes**) | 1 list pointer + $k_{\text{avg}}$ buckets |
| Typical bucket size | — | $\sim$**32–64 bytes** (color + depth + $\alpha$ + flag + next) |
| Total memory (1920×1080, $k_{\text{avg}} = 3$) | $\approx$ **8 MB** | $\approx$ **400 MB** |
| Worst-case memory (each pixel hit by all $P$ polygons) | $\sim 8$ MB | $\sim N_x N_y P \cdot 64$ bytes (can be GB) |
| Time per frame | $O(P \cdot A)$ | $O(P \cdot A + N_x N_y \cdot k_{\text{avg}})$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why modern GPUs use a **hybrid** approach: a hardware Z-Buffer for opaque geometry, and a separate **per-pixel ordered transparency list** (sometimes called an "A-Buffer tile") for transparent or alpha-tested geometry (e.g., DirectX 12 *Execute Indirect* tier, NVIDIA *Meshlet* rendering). The classical 1984 paper is alive and well in production engines.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the A-Buffer Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram describes the **functional flow** of the A-Buffer algorithm, from polygon input to final image output. Node IDs are alphanumeric per the safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polygon List Input] --> B[Polygon Rasterizer]
    B --> C[Sub-pixel Coverage Test]
    C --> D[Depth and Color Interpolation]
    D --> E{Per-Pixel List Empty}
    E -- Yes --> F[Create New Fragment Bucket]
    E -- No --> G{List Has Single Resolved Opaque Fragment}
    G -- Yes --> H{Incoming Polygon in Front}
    H -- Yes --> I[Overwrite Existing Bucket]
    H -- No --> J[Append New Fragment to List]
    G -- No --> J
    F --> K[Process Next Pixel]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{All Polygons Processed}
    L -- No --> B
    L -- Yes --> M[Resolve Pass: Sort Each Pixel by Depth]
    M --> N[Apply Back-to-Front Compositing]
    N --> O[Write Final Color to Framebuffer]
    O --> P[Output Final Image]
```

## 4.2 Per-Pixel Linked-List Memory Layout

This diagram shows how a single pixel $(x, y)$ stores a chain of fragment buckets. Each node is a `Fragment` record as defined in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Abuf[ABuf x y Header] --> N1[Bucket 1: z 0.9, I red, alpha 0.4, flag false]
    N1 --> N2[Bucket 2: z 0.5, I green, alpha 0.7, flag false]
    N2 --> N3[Bucket 3: z 0.1, I blue, alpha 1.0, flag true]
    N3 --> N4[NULL terminator]
```

**How to read this:** the chain is stored front-to-back by depth, with the closest opaque surface (`flag = true`) terminating the list. During the **resolve pass**, the renderer walks the list **back-to-front** (Bucket 1 → Bucket 2 → Bucket 3) and applies the compositing equation from Section 2.3.

## 4.3 A-Buffer vs Z-Buffer — Conceptual Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZBUF[Z-Buffer Approach]
        Z1[Pixel Stores: 1 depth value] --> Z2[Discarded surfaces: lost forever]
    end
    subgraph ABUF[A-Buffer Approach]
        A1[Pixel Stores: linked list of fragments] --> A2[All surfaces retained with alpha and color]
        A2 --> A3[Resolve pass blends in correct order]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** When asked to "explain the A-Buffer with a diagram" in a 7-mark question, drawing a **2D array of pointers → linked lists** is the *single most credit-worthy* visual. The first node of each list MUST be labelled with `flag` and `alpha` to score full marks.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Differentiate between Z-Buffer and A-Buffer algorithms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Z-Buffer | A-Buffer |
|---|---|---|
| Per-pixel storage | Single depth value | Linked list of fragment records |
| Transparency | Not supported | Fully supported via per-fragment $\alpha$ |
| Anti-aliasing | Not supported (jagged edges) | Supported via sub-pixel coverage $\alpha$ |
| Memory | Fixed: $N_x N_y$ depths | Variable: depends on overlap count |
| Resolve step | Not needed | Mandatory back-to-front compositing pass |
| Surface order | Not preserved | Preserved per pixel |

*(Examiner note: 1 mark for correct storage, 1 mark for transparency/anti-aliasing difference, 1 mark for memory or resolve.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: What is the role of the "coverage" value $\alpha$ in the A-Buffer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The coverage value $\alpha$ is a scalar in the range $[0, 1]$ that represents the **fractional area of a pixel covered by a polygon**, computed by sub-pixel sampling. It serves a **dual role**:

1. **Anti-aliasing role:** By storing $\alpha < 1$ for pixels only partially covered by a polygon, edges appear smooth rather than jagged.
2. **Transparency role:** When $\alpha < 1$, the surface is interpreted as semi-transparent, and the resolve pass blends its color with underlying surfaces using the compositing equation $C_{\text{final}} = \sum \alpha_i C_i \prod (1 - \alpha_j)$.

*(1 mark each for: range & definition; anti-aliasing use; transparency use.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the A-Buffer algorithm in detail. Explain its data structure and how it differs from the Z-Buffer.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A pixel is hit by three semi-transparent surfaces, processed back-to-front as follows: $C_1 = 0.8,\ \alpha_1 = 0.4$; $C_2 = 0.6,\ \alpha_2 = 0.5$; $C_3 = 1.0,\ \alpha_3 = 0.7$. Compute the final composited color using the A-Buffer resolve equation.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** A-Buffer is an accumulation-buffer / generalized Z-Buffer that stores a linked list of surface fragments per pixel.
2. **Data structure [2 marks]:** 2D array $\text{ABuf}[x][y]$ of pointers; each pointer heads a linked list of `Fragment` structs containing $z$, color $I$, coverage $\alpha$, `flag` (resolved/unresolved), and `next`.
3. **Two states of a fragment [1 mark]:** `flag = true` ⇒ single opaque surface (acts like Z-Buffer cell); `flag = false` ⇒ unresolved, more surfaces may exist.
4. **Algorithm flow [2 marks]:** (i) Rasterize each polygon. (ii) For each pixel, compute $z$ and $\alpha$. (iii) Append or overwrite bucket depending on existing list. (iv) After all polygons, run resolve pass with back-to-front compositing.
5. **Differences from Z-Buffer [1 mark]:** Z-Buffer stores 1 depth; A-Buffer stores a list. Z-Buffer cannot do transparency or anti-aliasing; A-Buffer can.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

Given (back-to-front): $C_1 = 0.8,\ \alpha_1 = 0.4$; $C_2 = 0.6,\ \alpha_2 = 0.5$; $C_3 = 1.0,\ \alpha_3 = 0.7$.

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Compute transmittance terms $T_i$:

$$
T_1 = 1,\quad T_2 = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6,\quad T_3 = (1 - 0.4)(1 - 0.5) = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.3
$$

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Compute each weighted contribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_1 &= \alpha_1 C_1 T_1 = 0.4 \times 0.8 \times 1.0 = 0.32 \\
\text{Term}_2 &= \alpha_2 C_2 T_2 = 0.5 \times 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.18 \\
\text{Term}_3 &= \alpha_3 C_3 T_3 = 0.7 \times 1.0 \times 0.3 = 0.21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 [1 mark]:** Sum:

$$
C_{\text{final}} = 0.32 + 0.18 + 0.21 = 0.71
$$

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** Show expanded back-to-front blending (algebraic form for credit):

$$
C_{\text{final}} = 0.7(1.0) + (1 - 0.7)(0.5 \cdot 0.6) + (1 - 0.7)(1 - 0.5)(0.4 \cdot 0.8)
$$

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Numerical evaluation showing 0.71 and final answer.

**Sanity check:** $0.71 \le 1$ ✓.

**Final Answer:** $C_{\text{final}} = 0.71$.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the per-pixel data structure of the A-Buffer and the role of the `flag` bit. List two advantages of the A-Buffer over the Z-Buffer.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A pixel is hit by four surfaces, processed back-to-front. The data is given in the table below. Compute the final color and identify the dominant contribution.
>
> | Fragment $i$ | $C_i$ | $\alpha_i$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 1.0 | 0.25 |
> | 2 | 0.5 | 0.50 |
> | 3 | 0.9 | 0.80 |
> | 4 | 0.2 | 1.00 |

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Diagram [3 marks]:** 2D array `ABuf[x][y]` of pointers → each points to head of a linked list of `Fragment` records. Each record: $z$, $I$, $\alpha$, `next`, `flag`. (Without diagram, 2 marks maximum.)
2. **Role of `flag` [2 marks]:**
   - `flag = TRUE` ⇒ bucket represents a single opaque surface; no further surfaces may be appended at this depth — equivalent to a Z-Buffer cell.
   - `flag = FALSE` ⇒ bucket is a "tentative" surface; new surfaces in front of or behind it are appended to the chain for later compositing.
3. **Two advantages of A-Buffer [2 marks]:**
   - **(i)** Supports **transparent** surfaces through per-fragment $\alpha$ and back-to-front compositing.
   - **(ii)** Supports **anti-aliasing** of polygon edges through sub-pixel coverage $\alpha$.
   - *(Any two of: transparency, anti-aliasing, full surface attributes preserved, multiple layers per pixel.)*

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Compute transmittance products.

$$
T_1 = 1,\quad T_2 = 0.75,\quad T_3 = 0.75 \times 0.5 = 0.375,\quad T_4 = 0.375 \times 0.2 = 0.075
$$

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Compute weighted contributions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_1 &= 0.25 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 0.25 \\
\text{Term}_2 &= 0.50 \times 0.5 \times 0.75 = 0.1875 \\
\text{Term}_3 &= 0.80 \times 0.9 \times 0.375 = 0.27 \\
\text{Term}_4 &= 1.00 \times 0.2 \times 0.075 = 0.015
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 [1 mark]:** Sum.

$$
C_{\text{final}} = 0.25 + 0.1875 + 0.27 + 0.015 = 0.7225
$$

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** Identify dominant contribution. Looking at the four terms, $\text{Term}_3 = 0.27$ is the largest. Hence the **third fragment** (color $0.9$, $\alpha = 0.8$) is the dominant contributor. This is consistent with intuition: high $\alpha$ and high $C_i$ at mid-stack.

**Step 5 [2 marks]:** Final answer and explanation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Order convention mistake:** Many students treat fragment 1 as the *closest* and fragment $k$ as the *farthest*. In the back-to-front compositing equation used here, **fragment 1 is FARTHEST, fragment $k$ is CLOSEST**. Mixing this up will give wrong values and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Empty product forgotten:** $T_1 = \prod_{j=1}^{0}(\cdot) = 1$. Forgetting this is a frequent error; examiners specifically look for the first term written as simply $\alpha_1 C_1$.
> 3. **Mixing Z-Buffer terminology:** Saying A-Buffer "stores the minimum z" loses a mark. It stores the **list of all z's with their $\alpha$**.
> 4. **Forgetting the resolve pass:** Many answers describe appending fragments but forget the **back-to-front blending** step. The resolve pass is what makes the A-Buffer *produce a final image*. Always mention it.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark question:** Even a hand-drawn-looking diagram in text-form (e.g., a labelled box "ABuf[x][y] → Bucket1 → Bucket2 → NULL") scores 2–3 extra marks.
> 6. **Not stating $\alpha$ range:** Always write $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Box):**
>
> - [ ] A-Buffer = **A**ccumulation Buffer, generalized Z-Buffer, invented 1984.
> - [ ] Stores a **linked list of fragment records** per pixel, not a single depth.
> - [ ] Each fragment = $\{z,\ I,\ \alpha,\ \text{next},\ \text{flag}\}$.
> - [ ] **`flag = true`** ⇒ single opaque surface (closed bucket).
> - [ ] **`flag = false`** ⇒ may still receive more surfaces (open bucket).
> - [ ] Coverage $\alpha \in [0, 1]$: 0 = invisible, 1 = opaque, fractional = sub-pixel area / transparency.
> - [ ] **Compositing (back-to-front)**: $C_{\text{final}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \alpha_i C_i \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}(1 - \alpha_j)$.
> - [ ] **Fragment 1 = farthest**, fragment $k$ = **closest** in the equation.
> - [ ] **Transmittance** $T_i = \prod_{j=1}^{i-1}(1-\alpha_j)$ is the light reaching $i$ from layers above.
> - [ ] A-Buffer enables **transparency + anti-aliasing** — two things Z-Buffer cannot do.
> - [ ] A-Buffer requires a **resolve pass** at the end (back-to-front blending).
> - [ ] **Worst-case memory** can be $O(N_x N_y P)$ — far higher than Z-Buffer's $O(N_x N_y)$.
> - [ ] The first term in the composite is **always** $\alpha_1 C_1$ (empty product = 1).
> - [ ] Used in volume rendering, VFX, scientific viz, and modern GPU **transparency pipelines**.
> - [ ] Exam mantra: **"List per pixel, alpha per fragment, blend back-to-front."**
